title: 🫀 AORTIC STENOSIS (SAD TRIAD)
content:
The most common valvular disease in the elderly.

The SAD Triad (Symptoms):
1. Syncope (Exertional).
2. Angina.
3. Dyspnea (Heart Failure).

• Murmur: Harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at RUSB radiating to Carotids.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient has Symptomatic Aortic Stenosis (even just one of the triad), the next step is NOT more meds. It is Urgent Valve Replacement. Symptomatic AS has a 2-year mortality of 50%!

quiz.q: What is the classic physical exam finding for Aortic Stenosis pulse?
quiz.a: Pulsus parvus et tardus (weak and delayed pulse).

title: 🧩 INTUSSUSCEPTION (TELESCOPING)
content:
Most common cause of obstruction in kids 6-36 months.

• Triad: Colicky pain (legs to chest) + Sausage mass + Red Currant Jelly Stool.
• U/S: Target sign / Bull's eye.


⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canadian exams, the Air Enema is both the gold standard for diagnosis and the first-line treatment. If it fails or there's peritonitis, go to Surgery.

quiz.q: What is the classic stool appearance in intussusception?
quiz.a: Red currant jelly stool (blood mixed with mucus).

title: 🤰 MAGNESIUM SULFATE (ECLAMPSIA)
content:
Used for seizure prophylaxis and treatment in Preeclampsia/Eclampsia.

• Goal: Prevent seizures (NOT to lower blood pressure).
• Monitoring: Must check Urine Output, Respiratory Rate, and Deep Tendon Reflexes (DTRs).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Magnesium is excreted by the kidneys. If Urine Output is low (<30cc/hr), the risk of Magnesium toxicity is HIGH. First sign of toxicity is Loss of DTRs. Antidote: Calcium Gluconate.

quiz.q: What is the first clinical sign of Magnesium sulfate toxicity?
quiz.a: Loss of Deep Tendon Reflexes (Patellar reflex).

title: ⚖️ POWER OF ATTORNEY (POA)
content:
A legal document where a patient appoints an SDM.

• POA for Personal Care: Makes medical decisions if patient loses capacity.
• POA for Property: Makes financial decisions (NOT medical).

⚠️ Exam Trap: A POA for Personal Care only becomes active AFTER a physician determines the patient lacks capacity for that specific decision. The patient can revoke it at any time while they still have capacity.

quiz.q: Who makes medical decisions if no POA exists and the patient is incapacitated?
quiz.a: The default Substitute Decision Maker (usually Spouse/Partner, then children).

title: 🌬️ PLEURAL EFFUSION (LIGHT'S CRITERIA)
content:
Differentiating Exudate from Transudate.

Exudate if ANY of these:
1. Fluid Protein / Serum Protein > 0.5.
2. Fluid LDH / Serum LDH > 0.6.
3. Fluid LDH > 2/3 upper limit of normal.

TypeCommon Causes
TransudateCHF, Nephrotic, Cirrhosis.
ExudatePneumonia, Malignancy, PE.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the effusion is large/asymmetric, the first step is Thoracentesis to analyze the fluid. Do not start diuretics unless you are sure it's CHF!

quiz.q: Which condition classically causes a transudative pleural effusion?

title: 🧠 PANIC DISORDER MANAGEMENT
content:
Recurrent, unexpected panic attacks + ≥ 1 month of 'worry about more attacks'.

• Acute (Abortive): Benzodiazepines (Short-term use only).
• Chronic (Maintenance): SSRIs + Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In the Emergency Room, a patient with a panic attack MUST first have an ECG and physical exam to rule out MI, Arrhythmia, or PE before diagnosing 'Panic Attack'.

quiz.q: What is the first-line long-term pharmacotherapy for Panic Disorder?

title: 🔪 CHOLELITHIASIS VS CHOLECYSTITIS
content:
FeatureBiliary ColicAcute Cholecystitis
Pain Duration< 6 hours> 6 hours (Constant)
Fever / WBCNoneElevated
Murphy SignNegativePositive

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has RUQ pain + Jaundice + Fever (Charcot's Triad), it is NOT cholecystitis. It is ASCENDING CHOLANGITIS (Emergent ERCP needed!).

quiz.q: What is the imaging modality of choice for suspected gallbladder disease?

title: 👶 OTITIS MEDIA (AOM) IN CANADA
content:
CPS guidelines for 'Watchful Waiting' in children.

• Treat immediately if: < 6 months old, severe pain, or fever > 39°C.
• Watchful waiting (48-72h): If > 6 months, mild symptoms, and reliable follow-up.

⚠️ Exam Trap: First-line antibiotic is High-dose Amoxicillin. If they were on Amoxicillin in the last 30 days, use Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Clavulin).

quiz.q: What is the most common bacterial cause of Acute Otitis Media?

title: 🦋 PHEOCHROMOCYTOMA (TRIAD)
content:
Catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla.

The Classic Triad (PHE):
1. Palpitations.
2. Headache.
3. Episodic Sweating (Diaphoresis).
+ Resistant Hypertension.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Alpha-blockers FIRST (Phenoxybenzamine), then Beta-blockers. If you give a Beta-blocker first, it causes 'Unopposed Alpha Stimulation' and a life-threatening hypertensive crisis!

quiz.q: What is the screening test of choice for Pheochromocytoma?
quiz.a: 24-hour urinary metanephrines or Plasma free metanephrines.

title: 🩸 TTP (THE PENTAD)
content:
Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura. A medical emergency!

FAT RN Mnemonic:
• F: Fever.
• A: Anemia (MAHA / Schistocytes).
• T: Thrombocytopenia.
• R: Renal failure.
• N: Neuro symptoms (Confusion).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you suspect TTP, the next step is Immediate Plasmapheresis (Plasma Exchange). Do NOT give Platelets; it's like adding fuel to the fire!

quiz.q: What enzyme deficiency causes TTP?

title: 👶 APGAR SCORE
content:
Assessed at 1 and 5 minutes after birth.

Category0 Points1 Point2 Points
AppearanceBlue/PaleBody pink, limbs blueAll pink
PulseAbsent< 100 bpm> 100 bpm
GrimaceNo responseGrimaceCry/Cough
ActivityLimpSome flexionActive move
RespirationAbsentSlow/IrregularStrong cry

⚠️ Exam Trap: A low APGAR at 1 minute does NOT predict long-term outcome. It's the 5-minute and 10-minute scores that correlate with neurological prognosis. If score is < 7, continue assessing every 5 mins up to 20 mins.

quiz.q: What is the maximum score a neonate can receive on the APGAR scale?

title: 🔥 PARKLAND FORMULA
content:
Calculating fluid resuscitation for burn patients (2nd/3rd degree only).

4 mL × Weight(kg) × % TBSA

• Give HALF in the first 8 hours.
• Give the other half over the next 16 hours.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canadian exams, do NOT include 1st-degree burns (sunburns) in the % TBSA calculation. Also, use Ringer’s Lactate as the fluid of choice, not Normal Saline!

quiz.q: Over how many hours should the first half of the calculated fluid be administered?
quiz.a: The first 8 hours from the time of injury.

title: 🫁 CURB-65 (PNEUMONIA)
content:
Deciding where to treat Community-Acquired Pneumonia.

• C: Confusion.
• U: Urea > 7 mmol/L.
• R: Resp Rate ≥ 30.
• B: BP < 90/60.
• 65: Age ≥ 65.

ScoreManagement
0 - 1Outpatient (Home)
2Inpatient (Ward)
3+ICU consideration

⚠️ Exam Trap: CURB-65 is a tool, but clinical judgment comes first. If a patient scores 0 but has no social support or cannot take oral meds, they still need Admission.

quiz.q: What does the 'U' stand for in the CURB-65 score?

title: 🧪 ANION GAP (MUDPILES)
content:
Used to differentiate causes of Metabolic Acidosis.

Na - (Cl + HCO3) | Normal = 12 ± 2

High Anion Gap (MUDPILES):
Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, INH/Iron, Lactate, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the anion gap is high, always check the Osmolar Gap. If it's > 10, suspect toxic alcohol ingestion (Methanol or Ethylene Glycol).

quiz.q: What is the primary treatment for Ethylene Glycol (anti-freeze) poisoning?

title: 💥 AORTIC DISSECTION (STANFORD)
content:
TypeLocationManagement
Type AInvolves Ascending AortaSurgical Emergency
Type BDescending Aorta onlyMedical (BP Control)

[attachment_0](attachment)
⚠️ Exam Trap: For Type B dissection, the first-line treatment is IV Beta-blockers (Labetalol) to reduce Heart Rate and Shear Stress. Surgery is only for complications.

quiz.q: What is the classic pain description for Aortic Dissection?
quiz.a: Sudden, tearing or ripping chest pain radiating to the back.

title: 🩸 SECONDARY AMENORRHEA WORKUP
content:
Absence of menses for > 3 months (if regular) or > 6 months (if irregular).

Step-wise Approach:
1. Rule out Pregnancy (#1 Step - β-hCG).
2. Check TSH, Prolactin, and FSH.
3. Progestin Challenge Test.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If FSH is High (> 40) in a woman < 40 years old, the diagnosis is Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI).

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of secondary amenorrhea?

title: 👶 CHILD ABUSE (RED FLAGS)
content:
Recognizing Non-Accidental Injury (NAI).

• Injuries: Spiral fractures (non-walking kids), Posterior rib fractures, Retinal hemorrhages (Shaken Baby).
• Pattern: Bruises in different stages of healing or in 'protected' areas (buttocks, ears).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Your duty is to Report suspicion to Child Protective Services (CAS) immediately. You do not need to prove it, and you should not delay reporting to perform your own investigation.

quiz.q: What skeletal finding is highly specific for child abuse?
quiz.a: Metaphyseal 'bucket-handle' fractures or posterior rib fractures.

title: ⚖️ INVOLUNTARY ADMISSION (FORM 1)
content:
Ontario Mental Health Act (Similar across Canada).

• Criteria: Danger to Self, Danger to Others, or Inability to care for self (Self-neglect).
• Duration: Holds the patient for up to 72 hours for assessment.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A Form 1 allows you to Hold the patient, but it does NOT give you the right to Treat them without their consent (unless it's an emergency). Treatment capacity is a separate assessment.

quiz.q: Does a Form 1 allow a physician to force a patient to take long-term medication?
quiz.a: No, it only allows for detention and assessment.

title: 🟡 HEPATITIS B SEROLOGY
content:
MarkerMeaning
HBsAgActive Infection (Acute or Chronic)
Anti-HBsImmunity (Vaccine or Recovery)
Anti-HBc IgMAcute Infection (Window Period)
Anti-HBc IgGPast exposure (Chronic or Recovered)

⚠️ Exam Trap: A patient with ONLY Anti-HBs positive is Vaccinated. A patient with Anti-HBs + Anti-HBc IgG is Immune due to natural infection.

quiz.q: Which marker is the first to appear in an acute Hepatitis B infection?

title: 🧠 MYASTHENIA GRAVIS (MG)
content:
Autoantibodies against Post-synaptic Ach receptors.

• Symptoms: Ptosis, Diplopia, Dysphagia, Muscle weakness that worsens with use (Fatigable).
• Dx: AchR antibodies (Specific), Edrophonium (Tensilon) test.

⚠️ Exam Trap: 15% of MG patients have a Thymoma. Every newly diagnosed MG patient MUST have a CT Chest to screen for it!

quiz.q: What is the first-line chronic treatment for Myasthenia Gravis?

title: 🦴 SALTER-HARRIS FRACTURES
content:
A must-know classification for pediatric growth plate (Physis) injuries.

SALTR Mnemonic:
• S (Type I): Straight across (through physis).
• A (Type II): Above (physis + metaphysis) - #1 most common.
• L (Type III): Lower (physis + epiphysis).
• T (Type IV): Through (meta + physis + epi).
• R (Type V): Rammed (crush injury - worst prognosis).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Type II is the most common, but Type IV and V have the highest risk of growth arrest and limb length discrepancy. All require close follow-up.

quiz.q: Which Salter-Harris type involves a fracture through the metaphysis, physis, and epiphysis?

title: 🌬️ PRAM SCORE (KIDS ASTHMA)
content:
The standard Canadian tool for assessing asthma severity in children.

5 Components:
1. O2 Saturation.
2. Suprasternal Retractions.
3. Scalene Muscle use.
4. Air Entry.
5. Wheezing.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In acute pediatric asthma, the first-line treatment is Salbutamol + Ipratropium (Combo) + Oral Steroids (Dexamethasone). If PRAM score is high (Severe), consider IV Magnesium Sulfate.

quiz.q: Which medication is added to Salbutamol for initial 'burst' therapy in severe asthma?

title: 🤱 SHOULDER DYSTOCIA (ALARM)
content:
Emergency where the fetal head delivers but the anterior shoulder is stuck.

Management (McRoberts Maneuver):
• Hyperflex the mother's legs onto her abdomen.
• Apply Suprapubic Pressure (to disimpact the shoulder).

⚠️ Exam Trap: NEVER apply Fundal Pressure; it worsens the impaction and can cause uterine rupture. The 'Turtle Sign' is the classic clinical clue.

quiz.q: What is the first-line maneuver for shoulder dystocia?

title: 🧱 UC VS CROHN'S (IBD)
content:
FeatureUlcerative ColitisCrohn's Disease
LocationColon only (Rectum always)Anywhere (Mouth to Anus)
DepthMucosa/SubmucosaTransmural
PatternContinuousSkip Lesions
SmokingProtective!Risk Factor (Worsens)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Crohn's disease is associated with Fistulas, Abscesses, and Granulomas. UC is associated with Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) and Toxic Megacolon.

quiz.q: Which IBD is characterized by 'skip lesions' and a 'cobblestone' appearance?

title: 🧠 NMS VS SEROTONIN SYNDROME
content:
Critical to distinguish in psych cases.

FeatureNeuroleptic MalignantSerotonin Syndrome
CauseAntipsychotics (D2 block)SSRIs, MAOIs, TCAs
ReflexesHyporeflexia / RigidityHyperreflexia / Clonus
OnsetDays to WeeksHours (< 24h)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lead-pipe Rigidity = NMS (Tx: Dantrolene/Bromocriptine). Hyperreflexia and Clonus (shaking) = Serotonin Syndrome (Tx: Cyproheptadine).

quiz.q: Which physical exam finding most reliably distinguishes Serotonin Syndrome from NMS?
quiz.a: Hyperreflexia and neuromuscular clonus (present in Serotonin Syndrome).

title: 🚑 PEA / ASYSTOLE (H's & T's)
content:
Non-shockable rhythms in ACLS. Focus on reversible causes.

H's: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen (Acidosis), Hypo/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia.
T's: Tension Pneumo, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (PE), Thrombosis (MI).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In PEA/Asystole, do NOT shock. The priority is High-quality CPR + Epinephrine every 3-5 mins while searching for the 5 H's and 5 T's.

quiz.q: Is defibrillation indicated for a patient in Asystole?
quiz.a: No, defibrillation is only for shockable rhythms (VF and pulseless VT).

title: 🍼 HIRSCHSPRUNG DISEASE
content:
Congenital aganglionic megacolon.

• Presentation: Failure to pass meconium within 48h, abdominal distension, bilious vomiting.
• Physical: 'Blast sign' (explosive release of gas/stool on rectal exam).
• Dx: Rectal Suction Biopsy (Gold Standard) showing absence of ganglion cells.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Associated with Down Syndrome. On X-ray, you see a dilated proximal colon and a 'narrowed' distal segment (where the nerves are missing).

quiz.q: What is the gold standard for diagnosing Hirschsprung disease?

title: 🦴 ANTERIOR SHOULDER DISLOCATION
content:
The most common joint dislocation (95% are anterior).

• Mechanism: Abduction and External Rotation.
• Physical: 'Squared-off' shoulder appearance.
• Nerve Risk: Axillary Nerve (Check sensation over 'Regimental Badge' area).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the shoulder is Internally Rotated and 'Fixed', think Posterior Dislocation (Classic for seizures or electrocution). Always get 'Y-view' or Axillary X-ray.

quiz.q: Which nerve is most commonly injured in an anterior shoulder dislocation?

title: 🦋 DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS (DKA)
content:
Triad: Hyperglycemia + Anion Gap Acidosis + Ketonemia.

• Management: SIP (Saline, Insulin, Potassium).
• Potassium: Even if K+ is normal on lab, the total body K+ is LOW. Add K+ to IV fluids once K+ < 5.0.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT start insulin if Potassium is < 3.3. Correct K+ first! Insulin will drive K+ into cells and cause fatal arrhythmia. Goal is to close the Anion Gap, not just lower glucose.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of death in children with DKA?
quiz.a: Cerebral Edema (often due to overly rapid fluid correction).

title: ⚖️ CAPACITY VS COMPETENCE
content:
Crucial distinction for Canadian legal/ethics stems.

• Capacity: A Clinical determination made by a physician for a specific decision at a specific time.
• Competence: A Legal determination made only by a Judge.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Capacity is Task-Specific. A patient may have the capacity to decide what to eat, but lack the capacity to decide on a life-saving surgery. A diagnosis of Dementia does NOT automatically mean a patient lacks capacity.

quiz.q: Who is the primary person responsible for assessing a patient's capacity to consent to a treatment?

title: 🎀 BENIGN BREAST MASSES
content:
Differentiating common lumps in younger women.

ConditionTypical AgeFeatures
Fibroadenoma15 - 35yFirm, 'Slippery' (mobile), painless.
Fibrocystic Changes20 - 50yCyclical pain (worse before menses).
Fat NecrosisAnyHistory of trauma, firm, irregular.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A Fibroadenoma does not change with the menstrual cycle. Fibrocystic changes do. If a mass is suspicious in a woman > 35y, always perform a Mammogram and Core Biopsy regardless of clinical feel.

quiz.q: What is the most common benign breast tumor in women under 30?

title: 🔪 ACUTE APPENDICITIS (ALVARADO)
content:
The most common surgical emergency. Diagnosis is primarily clinical.

Alvarado Score (MANTRELS):
• M: Migration of pain to RLQ.
• A: Anorexia.
• N: Nausea/Vomiting.
• T: Tenderness in RLQ (2 pts).
• R: Rebound tenderness.
• E: Elevated temp.
• L: Leukocytosis (2 pts).
• S: Shift to the left (neutrophilia).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In a classic presentation in a male, you may go straight to Surgery. In females or uncertain cases, CT with contrast (adults) or Ultrasound (kids/pregnant) is the next step.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive physical exam finding for appendicitis?

title: 🤰 ECTOPIC PREGNANCY: MTX CRITERIA
content:
When can we use Methotrexate (Medical management) instead of surgery?

Ideal Candidate:
• Hemodynamically Stable.
• β-hCG < 5000 IU/L.
• Fetal heart activity is absent.
• Ectopic mass < 3.5 - 4 cm.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has Abdominal Pain + Hypotension, the ectopic has likely Ruptured. The only correct answer is Immediate Laparoscopy/Laparotomy. Methotrexate is contraindicated in rupture!

quiz.q: What is the most common location for an ectopic pregnancy?
quiz.a: The Ampulla of the Fallopian Tube.

title: 🧠 LITHIUM TOXICITY
content:
Narrow therapeutic index (0.6 - 1.2 mmol/L).

• Acute Signs: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, Coarse Tremor.
• Chronic Signs: Nephrogenic DI, Hypothyroidism.
• Precipitating Factors: Dehydration, NSAIDs, Thiazides, ACE-Inhibitors.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the Lithium level is > 2.5 mmol/L (or > 4.0 in acute) OR if the patient has severe neuro symptoms/renal failure, the treatment of choice is Hemodialysis.

quiz.q: Which class of common painkillers can increase Lithium levels?

title: 👁️ RETINAL DETACHMENT
content:
Separation of the neurosensory retina from the RPE.

• Symptoms: Sudden onset of Flashes (Photopsia), Floaters, and a 'Curtain' or 'Shadow' over the vision.
• Pain: Painless vision loss.


⚠️ Exam Trap: This is a surgical emergency. The patient should be kept NPO and referred to Ophthalmology immediately for surgical repair. Do not perform a Tonometry (pressure check) if rupture is suspected.

quiz.q: What is the classic visual field description in retinal detachment?
quiz.a: A dark curtain or shadow descending over the field of vision.

title: 🌾 CELIAC DISEASE (WORKUP)
content:
Autoimmune reaction to gluten (wheat, barley, rye).

• Initial Screen: IgA tissue Transglutaminase (tTG-IgA).
• Confirmation: Small Bowel Biopsy (showing Villous Atrophy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The patient MUST be on a gluten-containing diet at the time of testing. If they are IgA deficient (common in Celiac), the tTG-IgA will be false negative; check IgG-deamidated gliadin peptides instead.

quiz.q: What is the classic skin manifestation of Celiac disease?

title: 🫀 POST-MI COMPLICATIONS (TIMING)
content:
Critical to know when things go wrong after an MI.

• Day 1-3: Arrhythmias (Most common cause of death) / Pericarditis.
• Day 3-7: Papillary Muscle Rupture (leads to Acute MR) or VSD.
• Day 5-14: Free Wall Rupture (leads to Tamponade).
• Weeks Later: Dressler Syndrome (Autoimmune pericarditis).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient has a new Harsh Holosystolic Murmur on Day 4 after an MI, it is either a VSD or Papillary Muscle Rupture. Get an ECHO immediately!

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of death in the first 48 hours after an MI?

title: 🩸 HEMOPHILIA A & B
content:
X-linked recessive bleeding disorders.

• Hemophilia A: Factor VIII deficiency.
• Hemophilia B: Factor IX deficiency.
• Labs: Prolonged PTT, Normal PT, Normal Platelets.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The classic presentation is Hemarthrosis (bleeding into joints) after minor trauma. Treatment is factor replacement. In Hemophilia A, Desmopressin (DDAVP) can be used for mild cases.

quiz.q: What is the inheritance pattern of Hemophilia A and B?

title: ⚖️ MAID (ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA)
content:
Medical Assistance in Dying - updated Canadian rules.

• Eligibility: 18+ years, Capable of making decisions, has a Grievous and Irremediable medical condition.
• Condition: Incurable, advanced state of decline, and causing enduring suffering.

⚠️ Exam Trap: As of now, Mental Illness as the sole underlying condition is still under review/delayed. A patient must be able to withdraw their request at any time, up until the moment of the procedure.

quiz.q: How many independent medical assessments are required for MAID in Canada?

title: 🌬️ CROUP (WESTLEY SCORE)
content:
Laryngotracheobronchitis (Parainfluenza virus).

• Signs: Barking cough, Inspiratory stridor, Hoarseness.
• X-ray: Steeple Sign (subglottic narrowing).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment for ALL cases (even mild) is a single dose of Dexamethasone. If the child has Stridor at rest, they need Nebulized Epinephrine and observation for 3-4 hours to monitor for 'rebound'.

quiz.q: What is the classic x-ray finding in Croup?

title: 🦵 KNEE INJURIES (ACL, MCL, MENISCUS)
content:
A high-yield topic for clinical cases involving athletes or falls.

InjuryMechanismKey Sign
ACLNon-contact twist / PopLachman Test (Most sensitive)
MCLValgus stress (from side)Medial joint line pain
MeniscusTwisting while weight-bearingLocking / Catching / McMurray Test

[attachment_0](attachment)
⚠️ Exam Trap: If the knee swells immediately (< 2 hours), it is likely an ACL tear (Hemarthrosis). If swelling is delayed (6-24 hours), think Meniscus or minor strain.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive physical exam maneuver for an acute ACL tear?

title: 🦴 SCOLIOSIS (ADOLESCENT IDIOPATHIC)
content:
Lateral curvature of the spine > 10 degrees.

• Screening: Adams Forward Bend Test (looking for rib hump).
• Measurement: Cobb Angle on standing X-ray.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Management is based on the Cobb Angle:
• < 20°: Observation only.
• 20° - 40°: Bracing (to prevent progression).
• > 40° - 50°: Surgery (Spinal Fusion).

quiz.q: What is the primary tool for measuring the degree of spinal curvature in scoliosis?

title: 🧱 COLORECTAL POLYPS & SURVEILLANCE
content:
Not all polyps are created equal. Management depends on pathology.

• High Risk: Villous adenomas, size > 1 cm, or > 3 polyps.
• Low Risk: Tubular adenomas, size < 1 cm.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a colonoscopy shows Hyperplastic polyps in the rectum/sigmoid, they are benign; routine screening (FIT every 2 years) is enough. If Adenomatous polyps are found, repeat colonoscopy in 3-5 years.

quiz.q: Which histological type of polyp has the highest risk of malignant transformation?

title: 🧂 ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (WORKUP)
content:
TypeSite of InsultClassic Sign
Primary (Addison)Adrenal GlandHyperpigmentation (high ACTH)
SecondaryPituitary GlandPale skin (low ACTH)

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Primary (Addison), both Cortisol and Aldosterone are low (High K+ / Low Na+). In Secondary, Aldosterone is Normal because it's regulated by RAAS, not ACTH!

quiz.q: What is the screening test of choice for suspected adrenal insufficiency?

title: 🤰 BREECH PRESENTATION & ECV
content:
When the baby is 'bottom-first'.

• Management: External Cephalic Version (ECV) can be attempted at 36-37 weeks.
• Contraindications: Prior C-section, Placenta Previa, Oligohydramnios, Ruptured membranes.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For a Breech baby at term, the preferred and safest mode of delivery in Canada is Planned C-Section, unless the patient is already in advanced labor with a favorable breech type.

quiz.q: At what gestational age is External Cephalic Version typically attempted?

title: 🧠 EPIDURAL VS SUBDURAL HEMATOMA
content:
FeatureEpidural HematomaSubdural Hematoma
VesselMiddle Meningeal ArteryBridging Veins
ShapeLens-shaped (Convex)Crescent-shaped (Concave)
HistoryLucid IntervalElderly / Alcoholics / Gradual


⚠️ Exam Trap: Epidural does NOT cross suture lines. Subdural DOES cross suture lines but cannot cross the midline (falx cerebri).

quiz.q: Which vessel is typically ruptured in an epidural hematoma?

title: 🚬 SMOKING CESSATION PHARMACO
content:
Helping Canadians quit smoking.

• Varenicline (Chantix): Partial nicotine agonist. Most effective.
• Bupropion (Zyban): Increases dopamine/NE. Good if patient has depression.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Bupropion is strictly contraindicated in patients with a history of Seizures or Eating Disorders (Bulimia/Anorexia) because it lowers the seizure threshold.

quiz.q: What is a major contraindication for using Bupropion for smoking cessation?
quiz.a: History of seizures or eating disorders.

title: ⚖️ REFUSAL OF TREATMENT
content:
A capable adult has the right to refuse even life-saving treatment.

• Principle: Autonomy.
• Action: If a capable patient refuses (e.g., Jehovah's Witness refusing blood), you must Respect their decision, even if it results in death.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must ensure the patient has Capacity for that specific decision (understands risks/consequences). If they are Incapable and it's an emergency, you treat to save life.

quiz.q: Can a parent refuse life-saving blood transfusion for their child in Canada?
quiz.a: No, if it is life-saving, a court order or emergency legislation allows the physician to treat the child despite parental refusal.

title: 🩸 MULTIPLE MYELOMA (CRAB)
content:
Malignant proliferation of plasma cells.

The CRAB Criteria:
• C: Calcium (Hypercalcemia).
• R: Renal Insufficiency.
• A: Anemia.
• B: Bone Lesions (Lytic).


⚠️ Exam Trap: On a Bone Scan, Multiple Myeloma lesions are often Normal/Cold because there is no osteoblastic activity. You MUST use Skeletal Survey (X-rays) or CT/MRI.

quiz.q: What protein is found in the urine of Multiple Myeloma patients?

title: 🦵 CHRONIC VENOUS INSUFFICIENCY
content:
Valvular incompetence in the lower limbs.

• Signs: Edema, Hyperpigmentation (Hemosiderin), Venous Ulcers (Medial Malleolus).
• Tx: Leg elevation, Compression Stockings (if no arterial disease), exercise.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Before applying compression stockings, you MUST check the ABI (Ankle-Brachial Index) to ensure there is no significant peripheral arterial disease (ABI must be > 0.5-0.8).

quiz.q: Where are venous insufficiency ulcers characteristically located?

title: 🩸 MICROCYTIC ANEMIA (MENTZER INDEX)
content:
Differentiating Iron Deficiency from Thalassemia.

• Mentzer Index: MCV / RBC count.
• Index > 13: Suggests Iron Deficiency.
• Index < 13: Suggests Thalassemia.


⚠️ Exam Trap: In Iron Deficiency, the RDW (Red Cell Distribution Width) is High (cells are different sizes). In Thalassemia trait, the RDW is usually Normal (cells are uniformly small).

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive test to confirm iron deficiency?

title: 🧪 H. PYLORI MANAGEMENT (CANADA)
content:
Triple and Quadruple therapy guidelines.

• Quadruple Therapy (First-line): PPI + Bismuth + Metronidazole + Tetracycline (PBMT).
• Duration: 14 days.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Always confirm eradication at least 4 weeks after treatment completion using Urea Breath Test or Stool Antigen. The patient must be off PPIs for 2 weeks before testing!

quiz.q: Which test is used for non-invasive confirmation of H. pylori eradication?
quiz.a: Urea Breath Test or Fecal Antigen Test.

title: 🧠 FACTITIOUS DISORDER VS MALINGERING
content:
Differentiating intentional production of symptoms.

• Factitious (Munchausen): Motivation is to assume the 'sick role' (Internal gain).
• Malingering: Motivation is Secondary Gain (External: money, avoiding work, drugs).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Malingering is NOT a psychiatric diagnosis; it is a behavior. If a patient 'loses' symptoms as soon as they get what they want (e.g., a work note), suspect Malingering.

quiz.q: What is the primary motivation in Malingering?
quiz.a: Secondary or external gain (e.g., drugs, financial compensation, avoiding legal issues).

title: 🧪 ACETAMINOPHEN OVERDOSE
content:
One of the most common overdoses in Canada.

• Antidote: N-acetylcysteine (NAC).
• Tool: Rumack-Matthew Nomogram (to decide treatment based on 4-hour level).


⚠️ Exam Trap: NAC is most effective when given within 8 hours. If the patient presents > 24h later with liver failure, still give NAC, but the prognosis is poorer. Look for high AST/ALT!

quiz.q: At what time point after ingestion should the first acetaminophen level be drawn for the nomogram?

title: 🦴 CAUDA EQUINA SYNDROME
content:
Surgical Emergency! Compression of multiple nerve roots.

• Signs: Saddle Anesthesia, Urinary retention/incontinence, Fecal incontinence, Lower limb weakness.
• Dx: Emergent MRI.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Urinary Retention is the most sensitive finding. If you suspect Cauda Equina, do not wait for specialists; order an Urgent MRI and call Neurosurgery/Ortho immediately for Decompression.

quiz.q: What is the hallmark sensory finding in Cauda Equina Syndrome?
quiz.a: Saddle Anesthesia (numbness in the perianal/perineal area).

title: 👃 EPISTAXIS: ANTERIOR VS POSTERIOR
content:
• Anterior: 90%, Kiesselbach’s plexus. (Tx: Pressure, Cautery, or Packing).
• Posterior: 10%, Sphenopalatine artery. (Tx: Posterior packing + Admission).

⚠️ Exam Trap: A patient with Posterior Packing MUST be Admitted to the hospital because of the risk of hypoventilation/apnea and to monitor for re-bleeding.

quiz.q: Which artery is most commonly involved in posterior epistaxis?

title: 🦠 PELVIC INFLAMMATORY DISEASE (PID)
content:
[attachment_0](attachment)
Infection of the upper female genital tract.

• Criteria: Cervical Motion Tenderness (Chandelier sign), Adnexal tenderness, Fever.
• Tx: Ceftriaxone (IM) + Doxycycline (14 days) +/- Metronidazole.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Long-term complications include Infertility and Ectopic Pregnancy. Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (RUQ pain/liver capsule adhesions) is a classic association.

quiz.q: What is the classic physical exam sign for PID?

title: 🍬 DIABETES: ORAL AGENTS (SGLT2i)
content:
Newer agents with cardiovascular/renal benefits.

• SGLT2 inhibitors (Gliflozins): Cause glycosuria (peeing out sugar).
• Benefits: Reduced Heart Failure hospitalization and Renal protection.
• Side Effects: UTI, Genital Candidiasis, Euglycemic DKA.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canadian guidelines, SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA are now First-line (along with Metformin) for patients with HFrEF or CKD, even if HbA1c is close to target.

quiz.q: Which class of diabetes medications is specifically recommended for patients with heart failure?

title: ⚖️ EXCEPTIONS TO CONFIDENTIALITY
content:
When MUST you break a patient's secret?

1. Harm to Self/Others: Imminent risk (Tarasoff).
2. Reportable Diseases: Public Health (Syphilis, TB, etc.).
3. Child/Elder Abuse.
4. Fitness to Drive.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For 'Fitness to Drive', you must first encourage the patient to report themselves. If they refuse, you must report to the Ministry of Transportation (in Ontario/Canada) without their consent.

quiz.q: What is the primary ethical principle being overridden when a physician reports an unfit driver?

title: 🌬️ EPIGLOTTITIS VS CROUP (RED FLAGS)
content:
FeatureCroupEpiglottitis
OrganismParainfluenzaHib
OnsetGradual / Barking coughSudden / Drooling
PositionAnyTripod Position
X-raySteeple SignThumbprint Sign

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you suspect Epiglottitis, do NOT examine the throat with a tongue depressor. The next step is Airway Management in the OR with ENT and Anesthesia present.

quiz.q: What is the classic radiographic finding in epiglottitis?

title: 🚽 BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERPLASIA (BPH)
content:
Prostate enlargement in aging men causing LUTS (Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms).

• Symptoms: Frequency, Urgency, Nocturia, Weak stream, Hesitancy.
• Medical Tx: Alpha-blockers (Tamsulosin) for fast relief; 5-Alpha-reductase inhibitors (Finasteride) to shrink the gland.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Finasteride takes 6-12 months to work and it will cut the PSA level in half. You must double the PSA result in your mind when screening for cancer!

quiz.q: What is the most common side effect of Tamsulosin?

title: 🦵 PERIPHERAL ARTERIAL DISEASE (PAD)
content:
Atherosclerosis of the lower extremities.

• Symptoms: Intermittent Claudication (pain with walking, relieved by rest).
• Signs: Decreased pulses, hair loss on legs, thick nails, cool skin.
• Dx: Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI).


⚠️ Exam Trap: ABI < 0.9 is diagnostic for PAD. If ABI > 1.4, the arteries are non-compressible (calcified), common in Diabetics; you must use Toe-Brachial Index instead.

quiz.q: What is the most effective lifestyle intervention for PAD?
quiz.a: Smoking cessation and a supervised walking program.

title: 👶 ROSEOLA (SIXTH DISEASE)
content:
Caused by Human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6). Very common in infants.

• Pattern: High Fever (3-5 days) that ends SUDDENLY, followed immediately by a rash.
• Rash: Rose-pink, maculopapular, starts on trunk and spreads to neck/limbs.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The classic clue is a child who was very sick with a high fever but is now acting well despite the new rash. High fever can trigger Febrile Seizures.

quiz.q: At what point in the illness does the rash of Roseola typically appear?
quiz.a: Immediately after the high fever resolves (defervescence).

title: 🧴 MELANOMA (ABCDE)
content:
The most deadly form of skin cancer.

ABCDE Criteria:
• A: Asymmetry.
• B: Border irregularity.
• C: Color variation.
• D: Diameter > 6mm.
• E: Evolution (changing over time) - Most important!


⚠️ Exam Trap: If a mole is suspicious, the next step is Excisional Biopsy with 1-3 mm margins. Do NOT perform a 'Shave Biopsy' as depth (Breslow thickness) is the most important prognostic factor.

quiz.q: What is the single most important prognostic factor in a primary melanoma?

title: ⚖️ CONFLICT OF INTEREST (COI)
content:
Canadian guidelines on interacting with the pharmaceutical industry.

• Rule: Physicians should not accept personal gifts, cash, or luxury travel from industry.
• Samples: Can be accepted but should be used for patients who cannot afford them, not for personal use.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you are giving a lecture and have received funding from a drug company, the first step is Full Disclosure to the audience. Transparency is the key to managing COI.

quiz.q: Is it ethical to accept a 'finder's fee' for recruiting patients into a clinical trial?
quiz.a: No, this is a major conflict of interest and is considered unethical.

title: 🤰 GROUP B STREP (GBS) IN CANADA
content:
Screening and Intrapartum Antibiotic Prophylaxis (IAP).

• Screening: Universal Vaginal-Rectal swab at 35-37 weeks.
• Tx: IV Penicillin G during labor (at least 4 hours before delivery).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do NOT need to swab if the woman had GBS Bacteriuria earlier in the same pregnancy or a previous infant with GBS disease; they get IAP automatically.

quiz.q: What is the first-line antibiotic for GBS prophylaxis in a patient with no allergies?

title: ☀️ HEAT STROKE VS EXHAUSTION
content:
FeatureHeat ExhaustionHeat Stroke
Temp< 40°C> 40°C
CNSNormal (maybe dizzy)Altered (Confusion/Seizure)
SkinSweatingOften Dry (but can be sweaty)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Heat Stroke is a medical emergency. The priority is Rapid Cooling (Evaporative or Cold-water immersion) and aggressive IV fluids.

quiz.q: What is the hallmark finding that distinguishes heat stroke from heat exhaustion?
quiz.a: Altered mental status (CNS dysfunction) and core temperature > 40°C.

title: 🫀 CARDIAC TAMPONADE (BECK'S)
content:
Fluid in the pericardial sac preventing heart filling.

Beck's Triad:
1. Hypotension.
2. JVD (Distended neck veins).
3. Muffled Heart Sounds.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Look for Pulsus Paradoxus (drop in SBP > 10 mmHg during inspiration). Treatment is Immediate Pericardiocentesis. Do not wait for an X-ray!

quiz.q: What classic finding is seen on ECG in a patient with large pericardial effusion/tamponade?
quiz.a: Electrical Alternans (alternating height of QRS complexes).

title: 🧠 ADHD (KIDS & ADULTS)
content:
Inattention, Hyperactivity, and Impulsivity.

• Criteria: Symptoms must be present in TWO or more settings (Home/School) and start before age 12.
• Tx: Stimulants (Methylphenidate/Lisdexamfetamine) are first-line.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Before starting stimulants, you must perform a thorough Cardiac History and Exam. Routine ECG is not mandatory unless there is a family history of sudden death or heart disease.

quiz.q: What is the most common non-stimulant medication used for ADHD in Canada?

title: 🎒 INGUINAL HERNIAS (DIRECT/INDIRECT)
content:
FeatureIndirect HerniaDirect Hernia
MechanismPatent Processus VaginalisWeak Transversalis Fascia
LocationThrough Internal RingMedial to Inf. Epigastric Art.
AgeYounger / InfantsOlder men


⚠️ Exam Trap: Indirect hernias are the most common type in BOTH males and females. If a hernia is Incarcerated (cannot be reduced), it needs urgent surgery.

quiz.q: Which type of inguinal hernia passes through the internal inguinal ring?

title: 🍼 MECKEL'S DIVERTICULUM
content:
The most common congenital anomaly of the GI tract (remnant of vitelline duct).

The Rule of 2s:
• 2% of population.
• 2 inches long / 2 feet from ileocecal valve.
• 2 types of ectopic tissue (Gastric/Pancreatic).
• Presents by Age 2.


⚠️ Exam Trap: The classic presentation is Painless Rectal Bleeding in a toddler. The best diagnostic test is the Meckel's Scan (Technetium-99m pertechnetate scan) which picks up the ectopic gastric mucosa.

quiz.q: What is the most common presentation of Meckel's diverticulum in a child?
quiz.a: Painless lower GI bleeding (maroon or bright red stool).

title: ⚡ SUPRAVENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA (SVT)
content:
Narrow complex, regular tachycardia (Rate 150-250).


Management Algorithm:
1. Vagal Maneuvers: (Carotid massage or Valsalva).
2. Adenosine: 6mg IV push (fast), then 12mg if needed.
3. CCBs/Beta-blockers: For rate control if stable.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is Unstable (Hypotension, Chest Pain, HF), ignore meds and go straight to Synchronized Cardioversion. Always check for WPW on the baseline ECG.

quiz.q: What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for stable SVT?

title: 🧷 EMERGENCY CONTRACEPTION
content:
Options after unprotected intercourse (UPSI).

MethodWindowNotes
Copper IUDUp to 7 daysMost Effective; long-term use.
Ulipristal (Ella)Up to 5 daysMore effective than Plan B.
Levonorgestrel (Plan B)Up to 3 daysLess effective if BMI > 25.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, the Copper IUD is the gold standard for emergency contraception and can be used regardless of the patient's weight. It also provides 10 years of ongoing contraception.

quiz.q: Which emergency contraception method is the most effective?

title: 🧠 NEXUS CRITERIA (C-SPINE)
content:
Deciding who needs a C-spine X-ray/CT after trauma.

No imaging needed if ALL are met:
1. No midline cervical tenderness.
2. No focal neurologic deficit.
3. Normal level of alertness.
4. No intoxication.
5. No painful distracting injury (e.g., femur fracture).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is Elderly (> 65y) or had a Dangerous Mechanism (fall > 3ft/5 stairs), NEXUS might be too lax; use the Canadian C-Spine Rules for higher sensitivity.

quiz.q: What is a 'distracting injury' in the context of C-spine clearance?
quiz.a: An injury so painful it might prevent the patient from noticing neck pain (e.g., a large fracture or deep burn).

title: ⚖️ MEDICAL ORDERS FOR LIFE-SUSTAINING TX
content:
In Canada, we define 'Levels of Care' clearly (DNR vs Full Code).

• Level M1: Comfort care only (Palliative). No CPR/Intubation.
• Level M2: Medical management but NO transfer to ICU.
• Level C1: Full code, but patient wishes for specific limitations.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A DNR (Do Not Resuscitate) order ONLY applies if the heart stops. It does NOT mean 'Do Not Treat'. You still provide antibiotics, fluids, and standard care unless specified otherwise.

quiz.q: Does a DNR order mean you should withhold antibiotics for a treatable pneumonia?
quiz.a: No, DNR only applies to CPR/Intubation in the event of cardiac/respiratory arrest.

title: 🌙 CUSHING'S SYNDROME (WORKUP)
content:
[attachment_0](attachment)
Hypercortisolism (Weight gain, Striae, Moon face).

Screening (Need 2 positive):
1. 24-hr Urinary Free Cortisol.
2. Late-night Salivary Cortisol.
3. Low-dose Dexamethasone Suppression Test.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most common cause is Exogenous Steroid use. The most common endogenous cause is Cushing's Disease (Pituitary Adenoma). Check ACTH next: if High, it's ACTH-dependent (Pituitary/Ectopic); if Low, it's Adrenal.

quiz.q: What is the next step in workup once hypercortisolism is confirmed?

title: 🍓 SCARLET FEVER (GAS)
content:
Caused by Group A Strep (erythrogenic toxin).

• Signs: Fever, Pharyngitis, Strawberry Tongue.
• Rash: 'Sandpaper' texture, starts in axilla/groin and spreads. Pastia's Lines in skin folds.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment is Penicillin V for 10 days. The goal is to prevent Acute Rheumatic Fever. Note: Antibiotics do NOT prevent Post-streptococcal Glomerulonephritis.

quiz.q: What is the primary reason for treating Strep pharyngitis/Scarlet fever with antibiotics?

title: 🧠 VALPROATE SIDE EFFECTS
content:
First-line for Bipolar mania and seizures.

• Common: Weight gain, Tremor, Hair loss.
• Severe: Hepatotoxicity, Pancreatitis, Thrombocytopenia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Valproate is highly Teratogenic (Neural Tube Defects/Spina Bifida). In a young woman of childbearing age, always check a Pregnancy Test before starting and discuss long-term contraception.

quiz.q: What specific congenital malformation is associated with Valproate use in pregnancy?

title: 🩸 ITP (IMMUNE THROMBOCYTOPENIA)
content:
Isolated low platelets due to anti-platelet antibodies.

• Presentation: Petechiae, Purpura, Mucosal bleeding (epistaxis).
• Labs: Low Platelets ONLY. Normal PT/PTT. Normal peripheral smear.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In kids, it often follows a viral infection and is self-limiting. In adults, it is usually chronic. Treatment (if Plt < 30) starts with Steroids or IVIG. Do not give platelets unless life-threatening bleed!

quiz.q: What is the hallmark finding in Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP)?
quiz.a: Isolated thrombocytopenia with no other CBC or smear abnormalities.

title: 🐦 ACHALASIA
content:
Failure of the LES (Lower Esophageal Sphincter) to relax.

• Symptoms: Dysphagia to BOTH solids and liquids, Regurgitation of undigested food.
• Dx: Manometry (Gold Standard) showing high LES pressure.


⚠️ Exam Trap: On Barium Swallow, look for the 'Bird's Beak' sign. Always perform an Endoscopy in older patients to rule out 'Pseudo-achalasia' (Esophageal Cancer).

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive diagnostic test for achalasia?

title: 💎 NEPHROLITHIASIS (KIDNEY STONES)
content:
Sudden, severe colicky flank pain radiating to the groin.

• Dx: Non-contrast CT Abdomen/Pelvis (Gold Standard).
• Management:
- < 5 mm: Likely to pass (Hydration + Analgesia + Tamsulosin).
- > 10 mm: Unlikely to pass (Shockwave Lithotripsy or Ureteroscopy).

[attachment_0](attachment)
⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has a Stone + Fever/Infection, this is a Surgical Emergency. You must decompress the kidney immediately (Stent or Nephrostomy) before dealing with the stone.

quiz.q: What is the most common type of kidney stone?

title: 👶 FAILURE TO THRIVE (FTT)
content:
Weight < 3rd or 5th percentile, or crossing two major percentiles downwards.

• Causes: 90% are Non-organic (Inadequate caloric intake, poverty, neglect).
• Workup: Detailed dietary history (Calorie count) is the most important step.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT order a battery of lab tests initially. Start with a Dietary Diary. If the child is not gaining weight despite adequate calories, then look for Malabsorption (Celiac, Cystic Fibrosis).

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of failure to thrive in children?

title: 🎀 CERVICAL CANCER SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Current Canadian guidelines for Pap testing.

• Start: Age 25 (regardless of sexual onset).
• Frequency: Every 3 years (Cytology/Pap).
• Stop: Age 70 (if previous results were negative).


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the result is ASC-US or LSIL in a woman > 30, the next step in many Canadian provinces is HPV Testing. If HPV is positive, refer for Colposcopy.

quiz.q: At what age should routine cervical cancer screening begin in most Canadian provinces?

title: 🫀 ACS INITIAL MANAGEMENT
content:
Time is Muscle! Management of suspected Acute Coronary Syndrome.

• First Step: Aspirin 160-325 mg (Chewed) immediately.
• Next: Nitroglycerin (SL), ECG (within 10 mins), Troponin.
• STEMI: PCI within 90 mins (or Fibrinolytics if > 120 mins from PCI center).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Nitroglycerin if you suspect a Right Ventricular MI (ST elevation in V4R) or if the patient used Sildenafil in the last 24h. It causes fatal hypotension!

quiz.q: What is the goal 'door-to-balloon' time for a patient with STEMI?

title: ⚖️ SDM HIERARCHY (ONTARIO/CANADA)
content:
Who makes the decision when the patient cannot?

1. Guardian (Appointed by court).
2. Attorney for Personal Care (Power of Attorney).
3. Representative (Appointed by CCB).
4. Spouse or Partner.
5. Children / Parents.
6. Siblings.


⚠️ Exam Trap: A Substitute Decision Maker (SDM) must make decisions based on the patient's prior capable wishes. If wishes are unknown, only then do they use the Best Interest principle.

quiz.q: Who is higher in the SDM hierarchy: a sibling or a parent?

title: 🧠 OCD VS OCPD
content:
FeatureOCD (Disorder)OCPD (Personality)
SymptomsIntrusive thoughts/RitualsPerfectionism / Control
InsightEgodystonic (Distressing)Egosyntonic (Thinks it's right)
InterferenceSignificant time/distressLifelong pattern of rigidity

⚠️ Exam Trap: In OCPD, the patient does not have true 'obsessions' or 'compulsions'; they are just overly focused on rules, details, and order to the point of being inefficient.

quiz.q: What is the first-line pharmacotherapy for Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)?
quiz.a: SSRIs (often at higher doses than for depression).

title: 🌬️ PULMONARY EMBOLISM (WELLS CRITERIA)
content:
Step-wise approach to suspected PE.

• Step 1: Calculate Wells Score.
• Low Probability: Use PERC Rule. If PERC is 0, rule out PE. If PERC > 0, do D-Dimer.
• High Probability: Skip D-dimer, go straight to CTPA (CT Pulmonary Angiography).


⚠️ Exam Trap: A Negative D-dimer only rules out PE in Low to Moderate risk patients. In a High-risk patient, a negative D-dimer is NOT enough; you still need a CT scan!

quiz.q: What is the gold standard imaging for diagnosing Pulmonary Embolism?

title: 🚑 FAST EXAM (ULTRASOUND)
content:
Used in trauma to detect free intraperitoneal fluid (blood).

4 Standard Views:
1. RUQ (Morison’s Pouch): Between liver and kidney.
2. LUQ: Between spleen and kidney.
3. Suprapubic: Pelvis / Bladder.
4. Subxiphoid: Pericardium (Tamponade).


⚠️ Exam Trap: If FAST is Positive and the patient is Unstable (Hypotensive), go straight to Laparotomy. If FAST is negative but patient is still unstable, look for extra-abdominal causes (e.g., tension pneumo).

quiz.q: What is the primary goal of the FAST exam in trauma?
quiz.a: To detect free intraperitoneal fluid (blood) or pericardial tamponade.

title: 🌬️ BRONCHIOLITIS (RSV)
content:
Viral infection (RSV) of small airways in kids < 2 years.

• Signs: Wheezing, tachypnea, nasal flaring, fine crackles.
• Management: Supportive Care Only (Oxygen, hydration, suctioning).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canadian guidelines, Salbutamol, Steroids, and Epinephrine are NOT recommended for bronchiolitis. Do not give them in the exam! Only use them for Asthma or Croup.

quiz.q: What is the most common virus causing bronchiolitis?

title: 🧠 ISCHEMIC STROKE (TPA WINDOW)
content:
Rapid management of 'Brain Attack'.

• First Step: Non-contrast CT Head (to rule out hemorrhage).
• tPA Window: Within 4.5 hours of symptom onset.
• BP Management: Must be < 185/110 before starting tPA.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient woke up with symptoms (Unknown onset time), the 'time starts' when they were last seen normal. Endovascular Thrombectomy (EVT) can be considered up to 24h in select patients.

quiz.q: What is the maximum time window for administering IV tPA in acute ischemic stroke?

title: 🔥 GERD & BARRETT'S ESOPHAGUS
content:
Chronic acid reflux can lead to metaplasia.

• Diagnosis: Clinical for GERD; Endoscopy + Biopsy for Barrett's.
• Barrett's Definition: Squamous to Columnar epithelium.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient has GERD for > 5 years + Age > 50 + Other risk factors (male, white, obese), the next step is Screening Endoscopy to rule out Barrett's, even if symptoms are controlled with PPIs.

quiz.q: What is the most serious complication of Barrett's esophagus?

title: 🧠 HEADACHE TYPES: COMPARISON
content:
TypeLocationFeature
TensionBand-like / BilateralStress-related, no nausea.
MigraineUnilateral / PulsatileNausea, Photo/Phonophobia.
ClusterBehind one eyeAutonomic signs (tearing, redness).

[attachment_0](attachment)
⚠️ Exam Trap: For Cluster headache, the first-line acute treatment is 100% Oxygen. For chronic prophylaxis of Migraine, use Beta-blockers (Propranolol) or Topiramate.

quiz.q: What is the acute 'abortive' treatment for severe migraines?

title: 🧪 CIRRHOSIS: CHILD-PUGH SCORE
content:
Assessing the prognosis of chronic liver disease.

The 5 Components:
1. Bilirubin.
2. Albumin.
3. INR (PT).
4. Ascites.
5. Encephalopathy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: INR and Albumin are the best markers of the liver's Synthetic Function. In acute liver failure, the first lab to become abnormal is the PT/INR due to the short half-life of Factor VII.

quiz.q: Which score is used in Canada to prioritize patients for liver transplant?
quiz.a: MELD Score (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease).

title: 🫀 ENDOCARDITIS PROPHYLAXIS
content:
Canadian guidelines on who needs antibiotics before dental work.

• Who: Mechanical valves, prior endocarditis, Cyanotic congenital heart disease.
• Med: Amoxicillin 2g (30-60 mins before).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Routine Mitral Valve Prolapse (MVP) or simple Bicuspid Aortic Valve NO LONGER requires prophylaxis in the latest guidelines. Only high-risk 'Elite' cardiac conditions do.

quiz.q: What is the alternative antibiotic for endocarditis prophylaxis in Penicillin-allergic patients?

title: 🧷 IUD: COPPER VS HORMONAL (LNG)
content:
FeatureCopper IUDHormonal (Mirena)
MensesHeavier / CrampyLighter / Amenorrhea
Duration10 Years5 Years
EmergencyMost EffectiveNot for emergency

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a woman has Menorrhagia (heavy periods), the Hormonal IUD (LNG-IUS) is the first-line treatment. If she wants to avoid hormones entirely, use Copper. Both are safe in nulliparous women.

quiz.q: Which IUD is preferred for a patient with copper allergy or Wilson's disease?

title: 👶 HAND-FOOT-MOUTH DISEASE
content:
Caused by Coxsackievirus A.

• Signs: Vesicles/Ulcers in the mouth, and maculopapular/vesicular rash on Palms and Soles.
• Tx: Supportive (hydration/pain relief).


⚠️ Exam Trap: The main complication is Dehydration because the mouth ulcers make it too painful for the child to drink. Advise parents on 'cold fluids' and analgesics.

quiz.q: What is the most common causative agent of Hand-Foot-Mouth disease?

title: 🌬️ PNEUMOTHORAX: SIMPLE VS TENSION
content:
FeatureSimple PTXTension PTX
TracheaMidlineDeviated (Away)
BPNormalHypotension (Shock)
Breath SoundsDecreasedAbsent


⚠️ Exam Trap: Tension Pneumothorax is a Clinical Diagnosis. If you see hypotension + tracheal deviation, do NOT order an X-ray. Perform Immediate Needle Decompression.

quiz.q: What is the definitive treatment for a large simple pneumothorax?

title: 💤 OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNEA (OSA)
content:
Recurrent airway collapse during sleep. High-yield for Medicine & Psych.

• Risk Factors: Obesity, thick neck, male sex.
• Signs: Daytime sleepiness, snoring, witnessed apneas.
• Dx: Polysomnography (Sleep Study).

⚠️ Exam Trap: OSA is a secondary cause of Hypertension. If a patient has resistant HTN, always screen for OSA. First-line treatment is CPAP and weight loss.

quiz.q: What is the primary gold-standard diagnostic test for OSA?

title: ⚖️ MATURE MINOR DOCTRINE
content:
Canadian legal principle for medical consent in minors.

• Rule: There is no fixed age for consent in most provinces (except Quebec - 14y).
• Key: The minor's Capacity to understand the nature, risks, and benefits of the treatment.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a 15-year-old is 'capable', they can consent to (or refuse) treatment without parental knowledge or consent. The physician's duty is to the patient, not the parents.

quiz.q: Can a mature minor refuse life-saving treatment in Canada?
quiz.a: Generally yes, if they are determined to have full capacity for that specific decision.

title: 🧪 HYPONATREMIA (SIADH VS DEPLETION)
content:
Evaluating Sodium < 135 mmol/L.

• Step 1: Check Serum Osmolality (True vs Pseudo).
• Step 2: Check Volume Status.
• SIADH: Euvolemic, Urine Osm > 100, Urine Sodium > 40.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If hyponatremia is Severe (< 120) or causing seizures, use 3% Hypertonic Saline. Correct slowly (Max 8-10 mmol/24h) to avoid Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome (Locked-in syndrome).

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of SIADH?
quiz.a: Small Cell Lung Cancer or CNS disorders/medications.

title: 🎒 STRANGULATED HERNIA (SIGNS)
content:
When a hernia becomes a surgical emergency.

• Incarcerated: Stuck but has blood flow.
• Strangulated: Ischemic (Blood supply cut off).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Red flags for Strangulation: Fever, high WBC, exquisite tenderness, and skin redness over the hernia. DO NOT try to reduce a strangulated hernia; you may push dead bowel back into the abdomen!

quiz.q: What is the first step in managing a suspected strangulated hernia?
quiz.a: NPO, IV fluids, and emergent surgical consultation.

title: 💪 NURSEMAID'S ELBOW (DETAILED)
content:
Radial head subluxation in kids 1-4 years old.

• History: Sudden pull on an extended, pronated arm.
• Exam: Child refuses to use the arm, held in slight flexion/pronation.

[attachment_1](attachment)
⚠️ Exam Trap: If the history is classic, you do NOT need X-rays. Perform Supination-Flexion or Hyperpronation maneuver. The child should start using the arm within minutes.

quiz.q: What is the anatomical structure that gets trapped in Nursemaid's elbow?

title: 🎗️ OVARIAN CANCER (RISK FACTORS)
content:
The 'Silent Killer' because it presents late.

• Risk Factors: Uninterrupted Ovulation (Nulliparity, Early menarche, Late menopause), BRCA1/2 mutations.
• Protective: OCPs, Breastfeeding, Pregnancy (anything that stops ovulation).

⚠️ Exam Trap: CA-125 is NOT a screening tool for the general population. It is used to monitor response to treatment or to evaluate a post-menopausal woman with a known adnexal mass.

quiz.q: What is the most common histological type of ovarian cancer?

title: 🦠 BACTERIAL MENINGITIS (EMPIRIC TX)
content:
Age GroupPathogensEmpiric Antibiotics
< 1 MonthGBS, E. coli, ListeriaAmpicillin + Gent/Cefotaxime
1m - 50yS. pneumo, N. meningitidisCeftriaxone + Vancomycin
> 50yAdd ListeriaCeftriaxone + Vanco + Ampicillin

⚠️ Exam Trap: Give Dexamethasone before or with the first dose of antibiotics in adults with suspected S. pneumoniae to reduce neurological complications (hearing loss).

quiz.q: Why is Ampicillin added to the empiric regimen in patients over 50?

title: 🧠 LITHIUM VS LAMOTRIGINE
content:
MedicationBest for...Monitor
LithiumAcute Mania / Suicide preventionTSH, Creatinine, Levels
LamotrigineBipolar Depression / MaintenanceSkin Rash (SJS)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lithium is the gold standard for reducing suicide risk in Bipolar. If a patient on Lamotrigine develops any new skin rash, they must STOP the drug immediately and go to the ED (Risk of Stevens-Johnson Syndrome).

quiz.q: Which mood stabilizer is most effective for preventing depressive relapses in Bipolar Disorder?

title: 🧠 GIANT CELL ARTERITIS (GCA)
content:
A medical emergency in patients > 50y. Risk of Permanent Blindness.

• Symptoms: New headache, Scalp tenderness, Jaw Claudication (pain when chewing).
• Labs: ESR > 50 mm/h (High sensitivity).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If suspicion is high, start High-dose Steroids (Prednisone 60mg) IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait for the Temporal Artery Biopsy results. Biopsy can still be positive up to 2 weeks after starting steroids.

quiz.q: What is the most common associated condition with GCA?

title: 🩸 HEMORRHOIDS: INTERNAL VS EXTERNAL
content:
FeatureInternal HemorrhoidsExternal Hemorrhoids
LocationAbove Dentate LineBelow Dentate Line
PainPainless BleedingExquisitely Painful
InnervationAutonomic (Visceral)Somatic (Sensitive)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Thrombosed External hemorrhoids cause sudden, severe pain and a bluish lump. Treatment is Excision within 72h. Internal ones are graded I-IV based on prolapse.

quiz.q: What is the hallmark symptom of internal hemorrhoids?
quiz.a: Painless bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR).

title: 🦒 RETROPHARYNGEAL ABSCESS
content:
Deep neck infection, usually in kids 2-4 years old.

• Symptoms: Fever, sore throat, drooling, Stiff neck (Torticollis), muffled voice.
• X-ray: Widened Prevertebral Space.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Distinguished from Epiglottitis by the Neck Stiffness and the fact that the child is usually younger. Management is IV antibiotics and Surgical Drainage.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive imaging for diagnosing a retropharyngeal abscess?

title: 🤰 GESTATIONAL DIABETES (GDM)
content:
Canadian screening (Diabetes Canada) is very specific.

• Screening: All pregnant women at 24-28 weeks.
• Test: 50g Glucose Challenge Test (GCT).
• Positive: If 1h BS ≥ 11.1 (Diagnostic) or 7.8 - 11.0 (requires 75g OGTT).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Insulin is the first-line medication if diet/exercise fails. Metformin is considered second-line in Canada. Goal is to prevent Macrosomia and Shoulder Dystocia.

quiz.q: When is routine screening for GDM performed in Canada?

title: 🧠 MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS (MS)
content:
Demyelinating disease of the CNS. Very high prevalence in Canada.

• Signs: Optic Neuritis (painful vision loss), Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia (INO), Lhermitte's Sign (shock-like feel down back).
• Dx: MRI (White matter plaques) and CSF (Oligoclonal bands).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Acute relapse is treated with High-dose IV Methylprednisolone. Chronic maintenance is with Disease Modifying Therapies (DMTs) like Interferon or newer biologicals.

quiz.q: What is Lhermitte’s sign?
quiz.a: An electric shock-like sensation that runs down the spine with neck flexion.

title: 🧠 CLUSTER A (ODD / ECCENTRIC)
content:
TypeCore Feature
ParanoidDistrustful, suspicious, perceives attacks.
SchizoidSocial detachment, prefers solitude, no interest in sex.
SchizotypalMagical thinking, odd beliefs, eccentric.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not confuse Schizoid (happy to be alone) with Avoidant (wants friends but afraid of rejection). Schizotypal is the 'pre-cursor' to Schizophrenia but without full psychosis.

quiz.q: Which Cluster A disorder is characterized by 'magical thinking'?

title: 🧪 OSMOLAR GAP POISONING
content:
Toxic alcohol ingestion (Methanol / Ethylene Glycol).

Measured Osmolality - Calculated Osmolality

• Positive Gap (> 10): Sugests Methanol or Ethylene Glycol.
• Methanol: Vision loss (snowfield).
• Ethylene Glycol: Kidney failure (Oxalate crystals).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Both cause a High Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis. Antidote is Fomepizole (blocks Alcohol Dehydrogenase). Hemodialysis is used for severe cases.

quiz.q: What crystal type is found in the urine of patients with ethylene glycol poisoning?

title: 🦋 SLE (LUPUS) DIAGNOSIS
content:
Multi-system autoimmune disease.

• Initial Screen: ANA (Sensitive, but not specific).
• Specific Tests: Anti-dsDNA (correlates with renal activity) and Anti-Smith (most specific).
• Labs: Low C3/C4 during flares.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Hydralazine, Procainamide, and Isoniazid can cause Drug-Induced Lupus. This is characterized by Anti-Histone antibodies and sparing of the kidneys.

quiz.q: Which antibody is most specific for SLE?

title: 🧱 LOWER GI BLEED (ELDERLY)
content:
Common causes in patients > 60y.

1. Diverticulosis: Most common cause of massive Painless bleed.
2. Angiodysplasia: Associated with Aortic Stenosis and CKD.
3. Colorectal Cancer: Chronic occult bleed / weight loss.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In an acute massive LGI bleed, the first step is Resuscitation (2 large-bore IVs + Fluids). Once stable, the diagnostic test of choice is Colonoscopy.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of painless bright red blood per rectum in the elderly?

title: ⚖️ WITHDRAWING VS WITHHOLDING
content:
Canadian legal and ethical stance on life-sustaining treatment.

• Withholding: Not starting a treatment (e.g., not starting a ventilator).
• Withdrawing: Stopping a treatment already in progress.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, there is NO legal or ethical difference between the two. If a treatment is no longer beneficial or goes against the patient's wishes, it can be stopped just as easily as it can be not started.

quiz.q: Is withdrawing a ventilator considered euthanasia in Canada?
quiz.a: No, it is considered allowing the underlying disease to take its natural course.

title: 🧬 CYSTIC FIBROSIS (DIAGNOSIS)
content:
Autosomal recessive mutation in the CFTR gene.

• Presentation: Recurrent pneumonias, Meconium Ileus (at birth), Failure to thrive (pancreatic insufficiency).
• Dx: Sweat Chloride Test (> 60 mmol/L on two occasions).


⚠️ Exam Trap: For the exam, the most common organism causing pneumonia in CF kids is S. aureus; in adults with CF, it is Pseudomonas. 95% of males are infertile (absent vas deferens).

quiz.q: What is the primary diagnostic test for Cystic Fibrosis?

title: 🩸 B12 VS FOLATE DEFICIENCY
content:
Both cause Megaloblastic Anemia (High MCV, Hypersegmented Neutrophils).

FeatureB12 (Cobalamin)Folate
NeurologicalPresent (Paresthesia/Ataxia)Absent
MMA LevelHighNormal
CauseVegan / Pernicious / Crohn'sAlcohol / Poor diet

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you treat B12 deficiency with Folate, the anemia will improve, but the Neurological damage will worsen and become irreversible. Always check both levels!

quiz.q: What specific neurological complication is associated with B12 deficiency?
quiz.a: Subacute Combined Degeneration (SCD) of the spinal cord.

title: 🦠 VAGINITIS: THE BIG THREE
content:
FeatureBacterial VaginosisTrichomoniasisYeast (Candida)
DischargeThin, Grey, FishyFrothy, Green-YellowCottage cheese
pH> 4.5> 4.5Normal (< 4.5)
MicroscopyClue CellsMotile TrichomonadsPseudohyphae

⚠️ Exam Trap: Trichomoniasis is the only one that is an STI. You MUST treat the partner. For BV and Yeast, partner treatment is not needed. Strawberry Cervix = Trich!

quiz.q: What is the treatment of choice for Trichomoniasis?
quiz.a: Oral Metronidazole for BOTH patient and partner.

title: 🦋 THYROID CANCER TYPES
content:
• Papillary (80%): Most common, best prognosis. (Psammoma bodies).
• Follicular: Spread via blood. Cannot diagnose with FNA (need biopsy).
• Medullary: Associated with MEN 2A/2B. Produces Calcitonin.
• Anaplastic: Elderly, very aggressive, poor prognosis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient has Medullary Thyroid Cancer, you MUST screen for Pheochromocytoma before surgery to avoid a hypertensive crisis in the OR.

quiz.q: What is the most common type of thyroid cancer?

title: 🧤 RAYNAUD'S PHENOMENON
content:
Vasospasm of digital arteries in response to cold/stress.

• Color Change: White (Ischemia) → Blue (Hypoxia) → Red (Reperfusion).
• Secondary Causes: Scleroderma, SLE, RA.

⚠️ Exam Trap: First-line treatment is Lifestyle (keep warm, stop smoking). If meds are needed, use Dihydropyridine CCBs (Nifedipine). Avoid Beta-blockers as they can worsen vasospasm!

quiz.q: What is the first-line pharmacotherapy for Raynaud's phenomenon?

title: 🩸 G6PD DEFICIENCY
content:
X-linked recessive enzymatic deficiency causing episodic hemolysis.

• Triggers: Fava beans, Infections, Drugs (Sulfa, Nitrofurantoin, Primaquine).
• Smear: Heinz Bodies and Bite Cells.

⚠️ Exam Trap: During an acute hemolytic episode, G6PD levels can be False Normal because the deficient cells have already been destroyed. You must repeat the test in 2-3 months!

quiz.q: What characteristic cells are seen on a peripheral smear in G6PD deficiency?

title: 🩸 HEREDITARY SPHEROCYTOSIS
content:
Autosomal dominant defect in RBC membrane proteins (Spectrin/Ankyrin).

• Signs: Anemia, Jaundice, Splenomegaly.
• Labs: High MCHC, Spherocytes on smear, Positive Osmotic Fragility Test.

⚠️ Exam Trap: These patients are at high risk for Aplastic Crisis if infected with Parvovirus B19. Bilirubin gallstones are common. Treatment is Splenectomy for severe cases.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive test for Hereditary Spherocytosis?
quiz.a: Eosin-5-maleimide (EMA) binding test or Osmotic Fragility test.

title: 🫀 MITRAL STENOSIS (MS)
content:
Most common cause is Rheumatic Heart Disease.

• Murmur: Mid-diastolic rumble with an Opening Snap (at the apex).
• Complication: Left Atrial enlargement leading to Atrial Fibrillation.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In MS, the shorter the interval between S2 and the Opening Snap (OS), the more severe the stenosis. Think of it as the valve snapping open under high pressure!

quiz.q: What is the hallmark physical exam finding for Mitral Stenosis?
quiz.a: Opening snap followed by a mid-diastolic rumble.

title: 🫀 MITRAL REGURGITATION (MR)
content:
Causes: MVP, Papillary muscle rupture (post-MI), Rheumatic fever.

• Murmur: Holosystolic blowing murmur at the apex radiating to the Axilla.
• Treatment: Afterload reduction (ACEi) or surgical repair.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient develops sudden MR 3-7 days after an MI, the cause is Papillary Muscle Rupture. This is a surgical emergency presenting with acute heart failure/pulmonary edema.

quiz.q: Where does the murmur of Mitral Regurgitation characteristically radiate?

title: 👶 RUBELLA (GERMAN MEASLES)
content:
Viral infection with significant teratogenic potential.

• Signs: Mild fever + Rash starting on face. Posterior Cervical/Auricular Adenopathy.
• Forchheimer Spots: Red petechiae on the soft palate.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Congenital Rubella Syndrome: Classic triad of Deafness, Cataracts, and PDA (Heart defect). This is why universal MMR vaccination is crucial!

quiz.q: What is the classic triad of Congenital Rubella Syndrome?
quiz.a: Sensorineural deafness, cataracts, and Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA).

title: 🐹 MUMPS (PARAMYXOVIRUS)
content:
Viral infection primarily affecting the salivary glands.

• Signs: Painful Parotitis (unilateral or bilateral), fever.
• Complications: Orchitis (usually post-pubertal), Aseptic Meningitis, Pancreatitis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Mumps is the most common cause of Acquired Deafness in children (unilateral). Orchitis rarely causes total infertility, but it can significantly reduce sperm count.

quiz.q: What is the most common complication of mumps in post-pubertal males?

title: 💓 VENTRICULAR SEPTAL DEFECT (VSD)
content:
The most common congenital heart defect.

• Murmur: Holosystolic at the LLSB (Lower Left Sternal Border).
• Management: Many small VSDs close spontaneously. Large ones lead to CHF.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Eisenmenger Syndrome: When a chronic left-to-right shunt causes pulmonary HTN, leading to shunt reversal (right-to-left) and Cyanosis. Once this happens, surgery is contraindicated!

quiz.q: What type of murmur is classic for a VSD?
quiz.a: Harsh holosystolic murmur heard best at the lower left sternal border.

title: 💓 PATENT DUCTUS ARTERIOSUS (PDA)
content:
Connection between Aorta and Pulmonary Artery fails to close.

• Murmur: Continuous 'Machine-like' murmur at the left infraclavicular area.
• Tx: Indomethacin (Prostaglandin inhibitor) helps close it. PGE1 keeps it open.

⚠️ Exam Trap: PDA is common in Premature infants and those born at high altitudes. If the baby has a 'ductal-dependent' lesion (like TGA), you MUST keep the PDA open with Alprostadil (PGE1).

quiz.q: Which medication is used to close a PDA in a premature infant?

title: 🧠 SOMATIC SYMPTOM VS ILLNESS ANXIETY
content:
FeatureSomatic Symptom DisorderIllness Anxiety Disorder
Physical SymptomsPresent and distressingMinimal or Absent
Core AnxietyAbout the symptom itselfAbout having a serious disease

⚠️ Exam Trap: Management for both is Regularly Scheduled Visits with a single primary care doctor. Avoid unnecessary testing or specialist referrals, as this reinforces the behavior.

quiz.q: What is the old name for Illness Anxiety Disorder?

title: 🧱 LARGE BOWEL OBSTRUCTION (LBO)
content:
FeatureSBOLBO
Most Common CauseAdhesionsColorectal Cancer
VomitingEarly and BiliousLate / Feculent
DistensionModerateMarked

⚠️ Exam Trap: On X-ray, LBO shows peripheral loops of bowel with Haustra (lines do not cross the full diameter). If the cecum is > 10-12 cm, there is a high risk of perforation!

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of large bowel obstruction in adults?

title: 🧂 PRIMARY HYPERPARATHYROIDISM
content:
Stones, Bones, Groans, and Psychiatric Overtones.

• Labs: High Calcium, High PTH, Low Phosphorus.
• Cause: Parathyroid Adenoma (80%).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has Secondary hyperparathyroidism (usually due to CKD), the Calcium will be Low and Phosphorus will be High. This is the key to differentiating them!

quiz.q: What is the classic electrolyte triad in primary hyperparathyroidism?

title: 🧪 AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS
content:
Chronic inflammation of the liver, usually in young/middle-aged women.

• Type 1: Anti-Smooth Muscle Antibody (ASMA) and ANA positive.
• Labs: High ALT/AST, Hypergammaglobulinemia (high IgG).
• Tx: Corticosteroids + Azathioprine.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is often associated with other autoimmune diseases (Type 1 DM, Thyroiditis). Definitive diagnosis requires Liver Biopsy (showing Interface Hepatitis).

quiz.q: Which antibody is most characteristic of Type 1 Autoimmune Hepatitis?

title: ⚖️ REPORTING UNFIT DRIVERS (CANADA)
content:
Canadian physicians have a legal mandate to report patients whose medical conditions make driving dangerous.

• Example: Uncontrolled seizures, severe dementia, substance abuse, or vision loss.
• Who: Reported to the Provincial Ministry of Transportation (e.g., MTO in Ontario).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You should inform the patient you are reporting them, but their consent is NOT required. Public safety overrides confidentiality. If you fail to report and the patient crashes, you could be legally liable.

quiz.q: Does a physician need a patient's permission to report them to the Ministry of Transportation?

title: 🎒 FEMORAL HERNIA
content:
A hernia passing through the femoral canal, Below the Inguinal Ligament.

• Demographics: More common in Females.
• Risk: Very high risk of Incarceration and Strangulation (due to narrow canal).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Because of the high risk of strangulation, all femoral hernias should be repaired surgically, even if asymptomatic. Do not 'watch and wait'.

quiz.q: In which gender is a femoral hernia more common?

title: 🧴 MOLLUSCUM CONTAGIOSUM
content:
Poxvirus infection causing benign skin lesions.

• Appearance: Small, flesh-colored, Umbilicated papules (dimple in the center).
• Transmission: Direct contact or fomites. In adults, consider sexual transmission.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In healthy children, the lesions are self-limiting and resolve within 6-12 months. If the lesions are giant or widespread in an adult, always test for HIV.

quiz.q: What is the hallmark physical appearance of a molluscum contagiosum lesion?
quiz.a: A flesh-colored papule with central umbilication (dimple).

title: 🫀 HEART FAILURE: NYHA CLASS
content:
Functional classification of heart failure symptoms.

• Class I: No limitation (Normal activity does not cause symptoms).
• Class II: Slight limitation (Comfortable at rest, symptoms with ordinary activity).
• Class III: Marked limitation (Comfortable only at rest, symptoms with less than ordinary activity).
• Class IV: Symptoms at Rest.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This classification is subjective and can change with treatment. The ACC/AHA Stages (A-D) are based on structural changes and are progressive (cannot go backwards).

quiz.q: In which NYHA class does a patient experience symptoms with minimal activity but is comfortable at rest?

title: 👶 GROSS MOTOR MILESTONES
content:
Key physical milestones for the exam.

• 4 Months: Rolls front to back.
• 6 Months: Sits with support.
• 9 Months: Sits without support / Pulls to stand.
• 12 Months: Walks alone.
• 18 Months: Runs / Kicks a ball.
• 2 Years: Walks up and down stairs (2 feet per step).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Red Flag: If a child is not walking by 18 months, they require a referral for developmental assessment.

quiz.q: At what age should a child be able to sit without support?

title: 🤰 MOLAR PREGNANCY (HYDATIDIFORM)
content:
Abnormal proliferation of trophoblastic tissue.

• Signs: Size > Dates, painless vaginal bleeding, hyperemesis, very high β-hCG.
• U/S: 'Snowstorm' appearance.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The priority after Suction & Curettage is Serial β-hCG monitoring weekly until zero, then monthly for 6-12 months. The patient MUST use contraception during this time to avoid confusing a new pregnancy with Choriocarcinoma.

quiz.q: What is the classic ultrasound finding in a molar pregnancy?

title: 🦵 ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA (6 P's)
content:
Surgical Emergency! Sudden decrease in limb perfusion.

The 6 P's:
1. Pain (Sudden/Severe).
2. Pallor.
3. Pulselessness.
4. Paresthesia.
5. Paralysis (Late sign).
6. Poikilothermia (Coldness).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Most common cause is Embolism from AFib. The first step is IV Heparin bolus followed by urgent surgical consultation for Embolectomy or thrombolysis.

quiz.q: What is the first medical intervention for suspected acute limb ischemia?

title: 🧠 DEPRESSION (SIGECAPS)
content:
Diagnosis of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD). Need ≥ 5 symptoms for ≥ 2 weeks.

SIGECAPS:
• S: Sleep (High/Low).
• I: Interest (Anhedonia - Mandatory).
• G: Guilt.
• E: Energy (Low).
• C: Concentration.
• A: Appetite change.
• P: Psychomotor (Agitation/Retardation).
• S: Suicidal Ideation.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Before starting an antidepressant, you MUST screen for a history of Mania. Giving an SSRI to a patient with Bipolar disorder can trigger a manic episode.

quiz.q: How long must symptoms be present to diagnose MDD?

title: 👶 KAWASAKI DISEASE (CRASH & BURN)
content:
Medium-vessel vasculitis in children.

• CRASH: Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue/lips, Hand/foot changes (swelling/desquamation).
• BURN: Fever for ≥ 5 days.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The main complication is Coronary Artery Aneurysms. Treatment is IVIG + High-dose Aspirin. (Note: This is the only exception where Aspirin is used in children despite the risk of Reye's).

quiz.q: What is the most serious complication of Kawasaki disease?

title: 🧪 NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (THE BIG 4)
content:
Characterized by glomerular damage.

The Tetrad:
1. Heavy Proteinuria (> 3.5 g/day).
2. Hypoalbuminemia.
3. Edema (Periorbital/Pitting).
4. Hyperlipidemia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Most common cause in children is Minimal Change Disease (Tx: Steroids). In adults, consider Focal Segmental (FSGS) or Membranous. These patients are at high risk for Thrombosis (PE/DVT) due to loss of Antithrombin III.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children?

title: 🚑 STATUS EPILEPTICUS
content:
Life-Threatening Emergency! Seizure > 5 mins or 2+ seizures without recovery.

Algorithm:
1. Stabilize: ABCs + Glucose check.
2. 0-5 mins: Lorazepam (IV) or Midazolam (IM/Buccal).
3. 5-20 mins: Phenytoin or Levetiracetam (Keppra).
4. > 20 mins: Intubation + Propofol/Phenobarbital.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for EEG to start treatment. If the patient is shaking for 5 minutes, give the Benzodiazepine immediately.

quiz.q: What is the first-line pharmacotherapy for acute management of status epilepticus?

title: 🎀 ABNORMAL PAP: NEXT STEPS
content:
ResultManagement (Canada)
ASC-USRepeat Pap in 6-12m OR HPV test (if > 30y).
LSILRefer for Colposcopy (or repeat in 6m).
HSILRefer for Immediate Colposcopy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In pregnant women, if you see LSIL/HSIL, you perform Colposcopy but NO Endocervical Curettage (ECC) due to the risk of preterm labor/bleeding.

quiz.q: What is the next step for a 35-year-old woman with an ASC-US result on Pap smear?

title: ⚖️ FORM 3 (INVOLUNTARY HOLD)
content:
Mental Health Act of Ontario (Principles apply across Canada).

• Form 1: Initial 72-hour hold for assessment.
• Form 3: Certificate of Involuntary Admission.
• Duration: Valid for up to 2 weeks (14 days).

⚠️ Exam Trap: To sign a Form 3, the patient must be assessed by a different physician than the one who signed the Form 1. This provides a 'second opinion' safeguard.

quiz.q: How long does a Form 3 allow a patient to be held in a psychiatric facility?

title: 🦶 GOUT: ACUTE VS CHRONIC
content:
PhaseTreatment
Acute FlareNSAIDs (Naproxen), Colchicine, or Steroids.
Chronic (PPX)Allopurinol (Xanthine oxidase inhibitor).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT start or stop Allopurinol during an acute flare; sudden changes in uric acid levels can worsen the inflammation. Wait 2 weeks after the flare resolves to start PPX.

quiz.q: What is the characteristic microscopic appearance of gout crystals?

title: 🧱 MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA (ACUTE)
content:
Ischemia of the small bowel, often due to an embolus.

• Key Clue: 'Pain out of proportion' to exam (severe pain but a soft, non-tender abdomen).
• Risk: Patient with AFib or recent MI.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has advanced signs (Rebound/Guard), the bowel is likely dead. Next step: CT Angiography (if stable) or Emergency Laparotomy. Look for high Lactate!

quiz.q: What is the classic clinical phrase used to describe acute mesenteric ischemia?
quiz.a: Pain out of proportion to the physical examination.

title: 🧪 HYPERKALEMIA (C-BIG-K-DROP)
content:
K+ > 5.5 mmol/L. Dangerous for heart rhythm.

Management:
1. Calcium Gluconate: Stabilizes cardiac membrane (Does NOT lower K+).
2. Bicarbonate / Insulin + Glucose: Shifting K+ into cells.
3. Kayexalate / Diuretics / Dialysis: Removing K+ from body.

⚠️ Exam Trap: ECG Changes (Peaked T-waves → QRS widening → Sine wave) are more important than the absolute number. If ECG is abnormal, give Calcium Gluconate first!

quiz.q: What is the first-line medication to stabilize the heart in severe hyperkalemia?

title: 🦠 SYPHILIS (TREPONEMA PALLIDUM)
content:
StageClassic Finding
PrimaryPainless Chancre (ulcer).
SecondaryRash (Palms/Soles), Condyloma Lata.
TertiaryGumma, Neurosyphilis, Tabes Dorsalis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Screening is with Non-treponemal tests (RPR/VDRL). Confirmation is with Treponemal-specific tests (FTA-ABS). Treatment is IM Penicillin G.

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for all stages of syphilis?

title: 🌬️ ASTHMA: CANADIAN CTS GUIDELINES
content:
Management based on control and severity.

• Mild: PRN SABA or Low-dose ICS-Formoterol (MART).
• Moderate: Regular Low-dose ICS + SABA.
• Severe: Add LABA (Combination inhaler).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, the preferred 'Reliever' for moderate-severe asthma is now the Combination (ICS-Formoterol) rather than just SABA alone, to reduce the risk of exacerbations.

quiz.q: What is the definitive diagnostic test for asthma in children > 6 years?

title: 🦴 SCFE VS LEGG-CALVÉ-PERTHES
content:
Two major causes of hip pain in children.

FeatureLCP (Perthes)SCFE
Age4 - 10 years10 - 16 years
PatientSmall, active boysObese / Rapid growth
PathologyAvascular NecrosisSalter-Harris Type I
PainInsidious limpSudden pain / Limp


⚠️ Exam Trap: In SCFE, the leg is usually held in External Rotation. It is an orthopedic emergency; the patient must be Non-weight bearing immediately until surgical pinning is done.

quiz.q: Which hip condition is characteristically seen in an obese adolescent with a limp?
