title: 🦵 ROTATOR CUFF TEARS (SUI)
content:
Common cause of shoulder pain, especially in overhead activities.

• Muscles (SITS): Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis.
• Supraspinatus (#1): Most common tear. Drop Arm Test and Empty Can Test.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Distinguish from Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder) where there is global loss of Active AND Passive range of motion. In Rotator Cuff tears, passive motion is usually preserved.

quiz.q: What is the most commonly injured muscle in a rotator cuff tear?

title: 🧱 ESOPHAGEAL CANCER
content:
TypeRisk FactorsLocation
Squamous CellSmoking, AlcoholUpper/Mid 1/3
AdenocarcinomaGERD / Barrett'sLower 1/3

⚠️ Exam Trap: The classic presentation is Progressive Dysphagia (Solid foods first, then liquids) + Weight loss. In Canada, Adenocarcinoma is now more common due to the obesity and GERD epidemic.

quiz.q: What is the primary risk factor for esophageal adenocarcinoma?
quiz.a: Chronic GERD leading to Barrett’s Esophagus.

title: 🟡 PANCREATIC CANCER (COURVOISIER)
content:
High mortality cancer, often presenting late.

• Signs: Painless Jaundice, Weight loss, Trousseau's sign (migratory thrombophlebitis).
• Courvoisier's Sign: A palpable, non-tender, enlarged gallbladder in a jaundiced patient.


⚠️ Exam Trap: The most sensitive imaging for diagnosis is CT Abdomen (Triple Phase/Pancreatic Protocol). CA 19-9 is used for monitoring response to therapy, NOT for screening.

quiz.q: What is Courvoisier's sign?
quiz.a: A palpable, non-tender gallbladder in the presence of jaundice, highly suggestive of malignancy.

title: 🩸 LEUKEMIA SUMMARY (CLL vs CML)
content:
FeatureCLLCML
AgeElderly (> 65y)Middle age (40-60y)
HallmarkSmudge CellsPhiladelphia Chromosome t(9;22)
TreatmentWatch & Wait (if asymptomatic)Imatinib (TKI)

⚠️ Exam Trap: CLL is the most common leukemia in the Western world. It is often diagnosed incidentally on CBC showing isolated lymphocytosis. Do not treat unless they have B-symptoms or marrow failure.

quiz.q: What chromosomal translocation is pathognomonic for CML?
quiz.a: t(9;22) forming the BCR-ABL fusion gene.

title: 🩸 LYMPHOMA (HODGKIN VS NHL)
content:
FeatureHodgkin LymphomaNon-Hodgkin (NHL)
NodesContiguous spreadNon-contiguous / Disseminated
Cell typeReed-Sternberg CellsB-cell (85%) or T-cell
AgeBimodal (20s and 60s)Increases with age


⚠️ Exam Trap: B-Symptoms (Fever, Night sweats, Weight loss) indicate a poorer prognosis. Diagnosis always requires Excisional Lymph Node Biopsy (FNA is NOT enough).

quiz.q: What is the name of the characteristic cell seen in Hodgkin Lymphoma?

title: 🌊 PPROM MANAGEMENT (< 34 WEEKS)
content:
Preterm Premature Rupture of Membranes.

• Diagnosis: Pooling, Nitrazine (+), Ferning (+).
• Management:
1. Steroids (Betamethasone) for lung maturity.
2. Antibiotics (Erythromycin/Ampicillin) to delay labor.
3. Transfer to tertiary center.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If there are signs of Chorioamnionitis (Maternal fever + fetal tachycardia), you must DELIVER immediately regardless of gestational age. Do not wait for steroids to work!

quiz.q: What are the three clinical signs of chorioamnionitis?
quiz.a: Maternal fever, uterine tenderness, and fetal tachycardia (and foul-smelling fluid).

title: 🎗️ ENDOMETRIAL CANCER
content:
The most common gynecologic malignancy in Canada.

• Risk Factor: Unopposed Estrogen (Obesity, PCOS, Nulliparity, Tamoxifen).
• Protective: OCPs, Smoking (decreases estrogen).
• Presentation: Post-menopausal bleeding (PMB).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In any post-menopausal woman with bleeding, the first step is Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVUS). If the Endometrial Thickness is > 4-5 mm, an Endometrial Biopsy is mandatory.

quiz.q: What is the most common risk factor for endometrial cancer in the Western world?
quiz.a: Obesity (due to peripheral conversion of androgens to estrogen).

title: 👶 WILMS TUMOR VS NEUROBLASTOMA
content:
Abdominal masses in children.

FeatureWilms TumorNeuroblastoma
LocationRenal (Kidney)Adrenal / Sympathetic chain
MassSmooth, Does NOT cross midlineIrregular, Crosses Midline
Age2 - 5 yearsInfancy / < 2 years


⚠️ Exam Trap: Neuroblastoma is associated with 'Dancing eyes/Dancing feet' (Opsoclonus-Myoclonus) and high urinary VMA/HVA. Wilms is associated with WAGR Syndrome (Aniridia, GU anomalies).

quiz.q: Which pediatric abdominal tumor characteristically crosses the midline?

title: 🚑 SHOCK CLASSIFICATION
content:
TypeCVP (Preload)COSVR (Afterload)
HypovolemicLowLowHigh
CardiogenicHighLowHigh
Distributive (Sepsis)LowHighLow

⚠️ Exam Trap: Distributive Shock (Sepsis/Anaphylaxis) is the ONLY one where SVR is LOW (Vasodilation). In Cardiogenic shock, the neck veins are distended (High CVP).

quiz.q: What happens to Systemic Vascular Resistance (SVR) in Septic Shock?
quiz.a: It decreases (due to massive peripheral vasodilation).

title: 🧪 ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (AKI)
content:
TypeBUN/Cr RatioFeNa %Urine Sediment
Prerenal> 20:1< 1%Hyaline Casts
Intrinsic (ATN)< 15:1> 2%Muddy Brown Casts
PostrenalVariesVariesNormal or blood

⚠️ Exam Trap: Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN) is the most common cause of intrinsic AKI. FeNa is the most useful lab to distinguish Prerenal from ATN. If the patient is on diuretics, use Fractional Excretion of Urea (FeUrea) instead.

quiz.q: What urinary cast is pathognomonic for Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN)?

title: 🫀 INFECTIVE ENDOCARDITIS (DUKE)
content:
Diagnosis using Modified Duke Criteria.

• Major: 2 (+) Blood Cultures; (+) Echo showing vegetation.
• Minor: Fever, IVDU, Vascular phenomena (Splinter hemorrhage), Immunologic (Roth spots, Osler nodes).


⚠️ Exam Trap: In IV Drug Users (IVDU), the Tricuspid Valve is most commonly affected (S. aureus). Look for Septic Pulmonary Emboli on Chest X-ray.

quiz.q: What is the most common organism causing subacute endocarditis on damaged valves?

title: ⚖️ THE 4 PILLARS OF CONSENT
content:
For consent to be valid in Canada, it must be:
1. Capacity: Patient must be capable of understanding.
2. Information: All risks, benefits, and alternatives disclosed.
3. Voluntariness: No coercion/pressure.
4. Specific: Consent for a specific procedure.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must disclose Material Risks (even if rare, if they are serious) and Common Risks. Failure to do so can lead to a charge of Negligence.

quiz.q: What is the legal difference between a procedure done with NO consent vs. INCOMPLETE consent?
quiz.a: No consent = Battery; Incomplete consent (failure to disclose risk) = Negligence.

title: 🧠 GLASGOW COMA SCALE (GCS)
content:
Assessment of impaired consciousness.

• Eye (4): Spontaneous (4), To voice (3), To pain (2), None (1).
• Verbal (5): Oriented (5), Confused (4), Inappropriate (3), Incomprehensible (2), None (1).
• Motor (6): Obeys (6), Localizes (5), Withdraws (4), Flexion (3), Extension (2), None (1).

⚠️ Exam Trap: "GCS of 8, Intubate!". A score of ≤ 8 signifies a comatose patient who cannot protect their airway. Minimum score is 3, maximum is 15.

quiz.q: What is the GCS score for a patient who opens eyes to pain, moans incomprehensibly, and withdraws from pain?

title: 🦴 OA VS RA (DIFFERENTIAL)
content:
FeatureOsteoarthritis (OA)Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA)
Morning Stiffness< 30 minutes> 60 minutes
JointsDIP, PIP, Knee, HipMCP, PIP, Wrist (Symmetric)
PathologyWear and tearAutoimmune Pannus


⚠️ Exam Trap: RA SPARES the DIP joints. OA characteristically involves them (Heberden's nodes). First-line for RA is Methotrexate; for OA it is Exercise + Acetaminophen/NSAIDs.

quiz.q: Which joints are characteristically spared in Rheumatoid Arthritis?

title: 🩺 HYPERTENSION (HTN CANADA 2024)
content:
Current Canadian thresholds for diagnosis.

• Office: ≥ 140/90 mmHg.
• Home / AOBP (Automated): ≥ 135/85 mmHg.
• Diabetics: Threshold is lower: ≥ 130/80 mmHg.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In most patients, first-line is ABCD: ACEi/ARB, Beta-blocker (not first-line for age >60), CCB, or Diuretics (Thiazides). For Black patients without CKD, the first-line is CCB or Thiazide.

quiz.q: What is the blood pressure target for a patient with Diabetes Mellitus in Canada?

title: 🧱 SBO VS PARALYTIC ILEUS
content:
FeatureMechanical SBOParalytic Ileus
PainColicky / SevereDull / Generalized
Bowel SoundsHigh-pitched / TinklingAbsent (Silent)
X-rayAir-fluid levels / Transition pointDilated loops everywhere


⚠️ Exam Trap: The most common cause of SBO is Adhesions (prior surgery). The most common cause of Ileus is Post-operative state or Hypokalemia. If SBO is suspected, keep NPO and get surgical consult.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of small bowel obstruction worldwide?

title: 👁️ ACUTE ANGLE-CLOSURE GLAUCOMA
content:
Ocular Emergency! Sudden increase in IOP.

• Symptoms: Severe eye pain, headache, nausea, Halos around lights.
• Signs: Red eye, Cloudy/Steamy Cornea, Fixed Mid-dilated Pupil, 'Rock-hard' globe.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Initial Tx: Timolol drops, Apraclonidine, and Acetazolamide (IV/Oral). Definitive treatment is Laser Peripheral Iridotomy (usually for both eyes). Avoid Atropine!

quiz.q: What is the classic pupil finding in acute angle-closure glaucoma?

title: 🦋 HYPERTHYROIDISM (GRAVES)
content:
Autoimmune stimulation of TSH receptors.

• Signs: Weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, Exophthalmos, Pretibial myxedema.
• Labs: Low TSH, High T4/T3.
• Uptake (RAIU): Diffuse high uptake.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the RAIU is Low, think Thyroiditis (De Quervain's) or Factitious (taking T4). For Graves, use Methimazole (unless in 1st trimester of pregnancy, then use PTU). Beta-blockers are used for immediate symptom control.

quiz.q: Which medication is preferred for hyperthyroidism in the first trimester of pregnancy?

title: 🦋 HYPOTHYROIDISM (HASHIMOTO)
content:
Most common cause of primary hypothyroidism in Canada.

• Labs: High TSH, Low T4. Positive Anti-TPO antibodies.
• Symptoms: Fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, weight gain.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment is Levothyroxine (T4). In elderly patients or those with heart disease, 'Start Low and Go Slow' (e.g., 25 mcg) to avoid triggering angina or MI. Re-check TSH in 6-8 weeks.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient regions like Canada?

title: 🧴 PSORIASIS
content:
Immune-mediated skin condition with rapid keratinocyte turnover.

• Signs: Well-demarcated Silvery scales on erythematous plaques.
• Locations: Extensor surfaces (Elbows, Knees), scalp.
• Nails: Pitting and 'Oil spots'.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Auspitz Sign: Pinpoint bleeding when a scale is removed. Koebner Phenomenon: New lesions at sites of trauma. Associated with Psoriatic Arthritis (DIP joints).

quiz.q: Which joint group is characteristically involved in Psoriatic Arthritis?

title: 🧴 ATOPIC DERMATITIS (ECZEMA)
content:
The 'Itch that rashes'. Chronic inflammatory skin disease.

• Atopic Triad: Eczema, Asthma, Allergic Rhinitis.
• Locations: Flexor surfaces (Antecubital/Popliteal fossae) in adults; Cheeks/Extensors in infants.

⚠️ Exam Trap: First-line is Moisturizers (Emollients) and avoiding triggers. For flares, use Topical Steroids. If you see vesicles with 'punched-out' erosions, suspect Eczema Herpeticum (Emergency - give Acyclovir!).

quiz.q: Where is the classic location for eczema in an infant?

title: 🧴 PEMPHIGUS VS PEMPHIGOID
content:
FeaturePemphigus VulgarisBullous Pemphigoid
Age40 - 60yElderly (> 60y)
BlistersFlaccid / Rupture easilyTense / Firm
MucosaOral lesions commonRare
Nikolsky SignPositiveNegative


⚠️ Exam Trap: Nikolsky Sign: Blistering of normal skin when rubbed. Pemphigus is much more severe and can be fatal without steroids. BP is deeper and usually less systemic.

quiz.q: Which blistering disease is characterized by a positive Nikolsky sign?

title: 🧠 ODD VS CONDUCT DISORDER
content:
Behavioral disorders in children.

• ODD (Oppositional Defiant): Argumentative, defiant toward authority, but no violation of others' basic rights or laws.
• Conduct Disorder: Aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, theft, Violation of rules/laws.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a child with Conduct Disorder reaches age 18 and the behavior persists, the diagnosis changes to Antisocial Personality Disorder.

quiz.q: A 10-year-old child who repeatedly sets fires and tortures the neighbor's cat most likely has which disorder?

title: 🤰 PLACENTAL ABRUPTION (MANAGEMENT)
content:
Premature separation of the placenta.

• Signs: Painful vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, frequent contractions.
• Complications: DIC, Hypovolemic shock, Fetal distress.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is stable and the fetus is term, Vaginal Delivery is preferred. If there is maternal shock or fetal distress, proceed to Emergency C-Section. Always check coagulation profile for DIC!

quiz.q: What is the most common risk factor for placental abruption?

title: 🤰 POST-TERM PREGNANCY
content:
Pregnancy ≥ 42 weeks.

• Risks: Macrosomia, Oligohydramnios, Meconium aspiration, Placental insufficiency.
• Management: In Canada, Induction of Labor is usually offered at 41 weeks to prevent complications.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Between 40 and 41 weeks, we perform twice-weekly monitoring (NST + Amniotic Fluid Index). If AFI is low (Oligohydramnios), you must induce regardless of the Bishop score.

quiz.q: At what gestational age is a pregnancy defined as post-term?

title: 🦴 OSTEOMYELITIS
content:
Bone infection, usually bacterial.

• Pathogen: S. aureus is #1. Salmonella in Sickle Cell patients.
• Dx: MRI is most sensitive. Bone biopsy/culture is Gold Standard.


⚠️ Exam Trap: X-rays can be Normal in the first 10-14 days of infection. Do not rely on them to rule it out. Elevated ESR/CRP and clinical localized bone pain are key.

quiz.q: What is the most common causative organism of osteomyelitis in a patient with Sickle Cell Disease?
quiz.a: Salmonella (though S. aureus is still common).

title: 🦴 SEPTIC ARTHRITIS
content:
Orthopedic Emergency! Infection of the joint space.

• Presentation: Fever, acute swollen, hot, extremely painful joint. Pain with any motion.
• Dx: Arthrocentesis (Joint aspiration) - WBC > 50,000, > 75% Neutrophils.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment is Urgent I&D (Incision and Drainage) and IV antibiotics. If you delay, the joint cartilage will be destroyed within hours.

quiz.q: What is the diagnostic threshold for WBC count in joint fluid aspirate for septic arthritis?

title: 🦶 OTTAWA ANKLE RULES
content:
Clinical decision tool to avoid unnecessary X-rays.

Get Ankle X-ray IF:
1. Pain in the malleolar zone AND
2. Bone tenderness at posterior edge of Tibia/Fibula (lat/med malleolus) OR
3. Inability to bear weight for 4 steps.


⚠️ Exam Trap: These rules are highly sensitive (>99%). If a patient meets none of these criteria, you can safely say they do NOT have a fracture and do not need an X-ray.

quiz.q: According to Ottawa Ankle Rules, where should you palpate for tenderness to rule out a fibular fracture?
quiz.a: The posterior edge or tip of the lateral malleolus (distal 6 cm).

title: ⚖️ BRAIN DEATH (CANADIAN CRITERIA)
content:
Defined as the irreversible loss of all brain functions, including the brainstem.

• Criteria: Known cause, no reversible factors (hypothermia/drugs), Absence of brainstem reflexes (Gag, Corneal, Pupillary).
• Test: Apnea Test (No respiratory effort despite PaCO2 > 60 mmHg).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Brain death IS legal death in Canada. Once declared, you do NOT need family consent to withdraw the ventilator (though you should do it with compassion and communication). This is different from 'Persistent Vegetative State'.

quiz.q: Is family consent required to withdraw mechanical ventilation once a patient is declared Brain Dead in Canada?
quiz.a: Legally no, as the patient is already deceased.

title: 🧪 IgA NEPHROPATHY VS PSGN
content:
Differentiating the two most common post-infectious glomerulonephritides.

FeatureIgA Nephropathy (Berger's)PSGN
Timing1-2 days after URI (Synpharyngitic)1-3 weeks after URI/Skin
Complement (C3)NormalLow
PrognosisRecurrent episodesExcellent (Children)

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the hematuria starts almost immediately after a sore throat, it's IgA Nephropathy. If there is a latent period of a few weeks, it's PSGN. In PSGN, look for high ASO titers.

quiz.q: Which condition is associated with low C3 complement levels?

title: 🚽 PROSTATE CANCER SCREENING
content:
Canadian Task Force recommendations on PSA screening.

• Recommendation: Do NOT screen asymptomatic men of any age with PSA (Grade D).
• Shared Decision: If a patient asks, discuss that PSA has high false positives and leads to over-diagnosis/treatment.

⚠️ Exam Trap: On the exam, if a patient is asymptomatic and asks for a PSA, the first step is Counseling about risks/benefits. If they still want it, then you can proceed. If the DRE is abnormal (nodule/hard), PSA is no longer 'screening' but part of a diagnostic workup.

quiz.q: What is the most common histological type of prostate cancer?

title: 🧪 PBC VS PSC
content:
Autoimmune cholestatic liver diseases.

FeaturePrimary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC)Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
PatientMiddle-aged FemaleMale with Ulcerative Colitis
AntibodyAnti-Mitochondrial (AMA)p-ANCA (nonspecific)
ImagingNormal (intrahepatic only)Beading of bile ducts (ERCP/MRCP)


⚠️ Exam Trap: PSC has a very high risk of Cholangiocarcinoma. PBC is treated with Ursodeoxycholic Acid (UDCA) to slow progression.

quiz.q: Which biliary disease is strongly associated with Ulcerative Colitis?

title: 🍬 HHS (HYPEROSMOLAR STATE)
content:
A life-threatening complication of Type 2 Diabetes.

• Features: Glucose > 33 mmol/L, No Ketones, Osmolality > 320 mOsm/kg.
• Symptoms: Severe dehydration, altered mental status, focal neuro deficits.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The mortality of HHS is higher than DKA. The primary treatment is Aggressive Fluid Resuscitation (Normal Saline). Insulin is secondary; the main goal is to correct the extreme dehydration and hyperosmolality.

quiz.q: Does HHS typically present with an anion gap metabolic acidosis?
quiz.a: No, because there is no significant ketoacid production.

title: 🔥 BURNS: INHALATION INJURY
content:
Airway Priority! Thermal or chemical damage to the respiratory tract.

• Red Flags: Singed nasal hairs, facial burns, Soot in mouth/sputum, Stridor, Carbonaceous sputum.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a burn patient has Stridor or massive facial burns, do not wait for the airway to close. Prophylactic Intubation is the next best step. Airway edema can progress rapidly in the first few hours!

quiz.q: What is the most reliable sign of impending airway obstruction in a burn patient?

title: 🧱 ACUTE PANCREATITIS (ETIOLOGY)
content:
Mnemonic: I GET SMASHED
• I: Idiopathic.
• G: Gallstones (#1).
• E: Ethanol (#2).
• T: Trauma.
• S: Steroids.
• M: Mumps.
• A: Autoimmune.
• S: Scorpion sting.
• H: Hypercalcemia / Hypertriglyceridemia.
• E: ERCP.
• D: Drugs (Sulfas, Azathioprine).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lipase is more sensitive and specific than Amylase. In a patient with epigastric pain radiating to the back and Lipase > 3x normal, Imaging (CT) is NOT needed for diagnosis. Use CT only if the patient is not improving after 48-72h to look for complications like necrosis.

quiz.q: What are the two most common causes of acute pancreatitis?

title: 👶 VESICOURETERAL REFLUX (VUR)
content:
Retrograde flow of urine from the bladder into the ureters.

• Presentation: Recurrent UTIs or Pyelonephritis in a child.
• Dx: VCUG (Voiding Cystourethrogram) is the gold standard.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Low-grade VUR (I-III) often resolves spontaneously. High-grade (IV-V) may require surgery. The goal of management is to prevent Renal Scarring and chronic kidney disease.

quiz.q: What imaging test is used to definitively diagnose VUR?

title: 🩸 POLYCYTHEMIA VERA (PV)
content:
Myeloproliferative disorder with overproduction of RBCs.

• Signs: Pruritus after a hot shower (Aquagenic pruritus), facial plethora, splenomegaly.
• Mutation: JAK2 mutation (> 95% of cases).
• Labs: High Hgb/Hct, Low EPO level.

⚠️ Exam Trap: These patients are at high risk for Thrombosis (Stroke, MI, Budd-Chiari). Treatment is Phlebotomy to keep Hct < 45% and low-dose Aspirin.

quiz.q: What is the typical erythropoietin (EPO) level in Polycythemia Vera?

title: 🧠 PTSD (POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS)
content:
Follows exposure to a traumatic event. Need symptoms for > 1 month.

Key Symptoms:
1. Intrusion: Nightmares, flashbacks.
2. Avoidance: Avoiding places/reminders.
3. Negative Alterations: Memory loss, guilt, detachment.
4. Hyperarousal: Irritability, startle response, insomnia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If symptoms last < 1 month, the diagnosis is Acute Stress Disorder. First-line treatment for PTSD is SSRIs + Trauma-focused CBT. Prazosin can be used specifically for nightmares.

quiz.q: At what point in time does Acute Stress Disorder become PTSD?
quiz.a: After symptoms persist for more than 1 month.

title: 🦠 HIV SCREENING & PEP
content:
ScenarioManagement (Canada)
ScreeningOpt-out screening in all adults (Universal approach).
Needlestick (PEP)Start within 72 hours for 28 days.
PrEPFor high-risk individuals (e.g., MSM, IVDU).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a healthcare worker is stuck by a needle, First Step is to Wash with soap and water. Then assess risk and start Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) as soon as possible, ideally within hours.

quiz.q: What is the maximum time window to start PEP after an HIV exposure?

title: 🧱 GALLSTONE ILEUS
content:
Mechanical obstruction due to a large gallstone in the small bowel.

• Pathophysiology: Cholecystoenteric fistula (usually with the duodenum).
• Rigler's Triad (X-ray):
1. SBO (Dilated loops).
2. Pneumobilia (Air in the biliary tree).
3. Ectopic Gallstone (in RLQ/Ileum).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Despite the name, this is a Mechanical Obstruction, not a paralytic ileus. The stone usually gets stuck at the Ileocecal Valve (narrowest point).

quiz.q: What is Rigler's triad?
quiz.a: Small bowel obstruction, pneumobilia, and an ectopic gallstone.

title: ⚖️ MEDICAL NEGLIGENCE (4 D's)
content:
To prove malpractice/negligence, four elements must be present:

1. Duty: A doctor-patient relationship existed.
2. Dereliction (Breach): The doctor failed to meet the 'Standard of Care'.
3. Damage: The patient suffered actual harm.
4. Direct Cause: The breach directly caused the damage.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Standard of care is defined as what a reasonable peer (a doctor with similar training) would have done in the same situation. An error in judgment is not always negligence if it falls within reasonable practice.

quiz.q: What are the '4 D's' of medical negligence?
quiz.a: Duty, Dereliction, Damage, and Direct Cause.

title: 🎗️ INFERTILITY WORKUP
content:
Inability to conceive after 1 year (< 35y) or 6 months (> 35y).

Basic Workup:
• Male: Semen analysis (First step, non-invasive).
• Female: Mid-luteal Progesterone (to check ovulation), FSH/TSH, HSG (for tubal patency).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Semen analysis is often the first test ordered because it is easy and rules out 40-50% of causes. If Progesterone is > 10 nmol/L on Day 21, ovulation has occurred.

quiz.q: At what point should an infertility workup begin for a woman aged 38?

title: 🫀 PERICARDITIS
content:
Inflammation of the pericardium (often viral/Coxsackie).

• Pain: Sharp, pleuritic, relieved by leaning forward.
• Physical: Pericardial Friction Rub.
• ECG: Diffuse ST elevation (concave/saddle) + PR depression.


⚠️ Exam Trap: First-line treatment is NSAIDs (Naproxen/Aspirin) + Colchicine. Colchicine is added to reduce the risk of recurrence. Do NOT use steroids as first-line (increases recurrence).

quiz.q: What is the classic ECG finding in acute pericarditis?
quiz.a: Diffuse concave ST-segment elevation and PR-segment depression.

title: 🧠 TYRAMINE REACTION (MAOIs)
content:
Hypertensive crisis when taking MAOIs (e.g., Phenelzine) with certain foods.

• Mechanism: Tyramine (in aged cheese, red wine, cured meats) displaces stored Norepinephrine.
• Signs: Sudden severe headache, extremely high BP, palpitations.

⚠️ Exam Trap: To avoid this, patients on MAOIs must follow a Low-Tyramine Diet. Also, wait at least 2 weeks (washout period) when switching between SSRIs and MAOIs to prevent Serotonin Syndrome.

quiz.q: Which class of antidepressants requires a 'low-tyramine' diet?

title: 🧠 BPPV (DIX-HALLPIKE)
content:
Most common cause of vertigo due to otoliths in the semicircular canals.

• Symptoms: Brief episodes of vertigo (< 1 min) triggered by head movement.
• Dx: Dix-Hallpike Maneuver (shows geotropic nystagmus).
• Tx: Epley Maneuver (repositioning).


⚠️ Exam Trap: BPPV has NO hearing loss or tinnitus. If hearing loss is present, think Meniere's Disease (episodes last hours) or Labyrinthitis.

quiz.q: What is the definitive treatment maneuver for BPPV?

title: 🩸 HIT (HEPARIN-INDUCED THROMBOCYTOPENIA)
content:
A pro-thrombotic complication of heparin therapy.

• Timing: Usually 5-10 days after starting Heparin.
• Hallmark: Platelet count drops by > 50%.
• Paradox: Despite low platelets, the primary risk is Thrombosis (clots).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If HIT is suspected, Stop ALL Heparin immediately (including flushes). Start a non-heparin anticoagulant like Argatroban or Danaparoid. Do NOT give platelets!

quiz.q: What is the most common clinical complication of HIT?

title: 👁️ STRABISMUS & AMBLYOPIA
content:
Ocular alignment issues in children.

• Strabismus: Misalignment (Esotropia/Exotropia). Dx by Cover-Uncover test.
• Amblyopia: 'Lazy eye' (reduced vision due to brain favoring one eye).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Strabismus is normal only up to 3-4 months of age. Beyond that, it must be referred. Treatment is Patching the GOOD eye to force the brain to use the weak eye.

quiz.q: What is the primary treatment for amblyopia?

title: 🩸 DIC (DISSEMINATED INTRAVASCULAR COAGULATION)
content:
Systemic activation of coagulation leading to both clotting and bleeding.

• Causes: Sepsis (Gram-), Trauma, Malignancy (APML), Obstetric (Abruption).
• Labs: Prolonged PT/PTT, Low Platelets, Low Fibrinogen, High D-Dimer.
• Smear: Schistocytes.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most important step is to Treat the underlying cause. Only provide blood products (FFP, Cryoprecipitate, Platelets) if the patient is actively bleeding or needs a procedure.

quiz.q: Which lab finding is most specific for DIC in the context of sepsis?

title: 🚽 STRESS VS URGE INCONTINENCE
content:
TypeMechanismTriggerTx
StressSphincter laxityCough, sneeze, laughPelvic floor (Kegel)
UrgeDetrusor OveractivitySudden, intense urgeBladder training / Oxybutynin

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Urge Incontinence, the first-line medication is Antimuscarinics (Oxybutynin) or Mirabegron. For Stress, surgery (Mid-urethral sling) is only for failed lifestyle/Kegel exercises.

quiz.q: What is the mechanism behind urge incontinence?

title: 🧂 ADRENAL CRISIS
content:
Life-Threatening Emergency! Acute deficiency of cortisol.

• Presentation: Hypotension (Refractory to fluids), Fever, Abdominal pain.
• Triggers: Infection or Stress in a patient with known Addison's or sudden steroid withdrawal.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for lab results. Treatment is High-dose IV Hydrocortisone (100mg) and aggressive IV Saline. Cortisol must be replaced before the patient stabilizes.

quiz.q: What is the immediate treatment for suspected adrenal crisis?
quiz.a: IV Hydrocortisone and IV fluid resuscitation.

title: 🧠 SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION
content:
Oncology Emergency! Compression by a tumor mass.

• Signs: New-onset back pain, Point tenderness, weakness, and Autonomic dysfunction (late).
• Dx: Emergent MRI of the entire spine.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If suspected, the First Step is High-dose Dexamethasone to reduce edema and pain while waiting for the MRI and neurosurgical consult.

quiz.q: What is the first medication to administer in suspected malignant spinal cord compression?

title: 👵 POLYPHARMACY & BEERS CRITERIA
content:
Managing medications in the elderly to reduce 'Prescribing Cascades'.

Avoid in Elderly:
• Benzodiazepines: Falls, confusion.
• Anticholinergics: Dry mouth, constipation, delirium.
• NSAIDs: GI bleed, renal failure.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A common MCCQE1 stem involves an elderly patient with a new symptom (e.g., confusion). Always Review the Med List first. The symptom might be a side effect of a drug recently added.

quiz.q: What is the main concern with using long-acting Benzodiazepines in the elderly?
quiz.a: Increased risk of falls, hip fractures, and cognitive impairment.

title: 💤 NARCOLEPSY TETRAD
content:
Excessive daytime sleepiness and REM sleep intrusion.

The Tetrad:
1. Cataplexy: Loss of muscle tone triggered by emotion.
2. Sleep Paralysis.
3. Hypnagogic Hallucinations.
4. Excessive Daytime Sleepiness.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Diagnosis is confirmed with a Multiple Sleep Latency Test (MSLT) showing rapid entry into REM sleep. Treatment for sleepiness is Modafinil; for cataplexy, use Sodium Oxybate or SSRIs.

quiz.q: What is the hallmark symptom of narcolepsy?
quiz.a: Excessive daytime sleepiness (often with sleep attacks).

title: 🦴 VITAMIN D DEFICIENCY (RICKETS)
content:
Common in breastfed infants without supplementation.

• Signs: Bowed legs, Rachitic Rosary (enlarged costochondral joints), delayed fontanelle closure.
• Labs: Low Ca/Phos, High Alkaline Phosphatase.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, ALL breastfed infants should receive 400 IU/day of Vitamin D from birth until their diet provides enough (or 1 year). Northern communities may need 800 IU.

quiz.q: What is the recommended daily dose of Vitamin D for a breastfed infant in Canada?

title: 🦴 OSTEOGENESIS IMPERFECTA
content:
Genetic defect in Type 1 Collagen synthesis.

• Signs: Multiple fractures (with minimal trauma), Blue Sclera, hearing loss, dental issues (dentinogenesis imperfecta).


⚠️ Exam Trap: This is often a differential for Child Abuse. Look for 'wormian bones' on skull X-ray. Unlike abuse, OI patients have other signs like blue sclera and a family history.

quiz.q: What is the characteristic eye finding in patients with Osteogenesis Imperfecta?

title: 💉 ADULT IMMUNIZATION (CANADA)
content:
Key vaccines for adults beyond the primary series.

• Pneumococcal: PCV13 and PPSV23 for those ≥ 65y or high risk.
• Zoster (Shingrix): Recommended for all adults ≥ 50y (2 doses).
• Tetanus (Td/Tdap): Booster every 10 years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Tdap (contains Pertussis) should be given at least once in adulthood, especially for those around infants. Pregnant women should get Tdap in EVERY pregnancy (27-32 weeks).

quiz.q: At what age is the Shingles vaccine (Shingrix) recommended in Canada?

title: 🦋 THYROID STORM
content:
Life-Threatening Emergency! Severe hyperthyroidism.

• Signs: Fever (> 40°C), Tachycardia (out of proportion to fever), Agitation/Delirium, Heart failure.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Management order is crucial:
1. Beta-blockers (Propranolol).
2. PTU (inhibits T4 to T3 conversion).
3. Iodine (give AFTER PTU).
4. Steroids (Dexamethasone).

quiz.q: Why must Iodine be given after PTU in Thyroid Storm?
quiz.a: To prevent the iodine from being used to synthesize more thyroid hormone (Wolff-Chaikoff effect).

title: 🩸 WALDENSTRÖM MACROGLOBULINEMIA
content:
B-cell lymphoma producing excess IgM.

• Hallmark: Hyperviscosity Syndrome (Vision loss, headache, dizziness, 'sausage' veins in eye).
• Labs: IgM spike on SPEP. Bone marrow showing lymphoplasmacytic cells.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Differentiated from Multiple Myeloma by: IgM (not IgG), No Lytic Bone Lesions, and the presence of Hyperviscosity. Emergency treatment is Plasmapheresis.

quiz.q: Which immunoglobulin is elevated in Waldenström Macroglobulinemia?

title: 🧠 NORMAL PRESSURE HYDROCEPHALUS (NPH)
content:
Potentially reversible cause of dementia in the elderly.

The Triad (3 W's):
1. Wacky (Dementia).
2. Wobbly (Gait apraxia - 'magnetic gait').
3. Wet (Urinary incontinence).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Dx is made by Large Volume LP (Tap test); if gait improves after removing CSF, it's NPH. Definitive treatment is a VP Shunt.

quiz.q: What is the characteristic gait finding in NPH?
quiz.a: Magnetic gait (feet seem stuck to the floor).

title: 🦴 HIP FRACTURES: INTRA VS EXTRA-CAPSULAR
content:
A critical distinction for management and prognosis in the elderly.

TypeLocationRisk
IntracapsularFemoral NeckAvascular Necrosis (AVN)
ExtracapsularIntertrochantericHigher blood loss, lower AVN risk


⚠️ Exam Trap: Classic presentation is a Shortened and Externally Rotated leg. If it's a Femoral Neck fracture in an elderly patient, the answer is often Hemiarthroplasty rather than fixation to avoid AVN.

quiz.q: What is the most serious complication of an intracapsular femoral neck fracture?
quiz.a: Avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head.

title: 👶 MILESTONES: FINE MOTOR & SOCIAL
content:
Complementary to gross motor milestones.

• 6 Months: Transfers objects hand-to-hand; Rakes food.
• 9 Months: Pincer grasp (immature); Object permanence (starts).
• 12 Months: Mature pincer grasp; Waves 'bye-bye'.
• 2 Years: Tower of 6 blocks; Parallel play.
• 3 Years: Copies a circle; Social/Interactive play.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pincer grasp development is a favorite question. If a 12-month-old cannot pick up a small object with thumb and forefinger, it's a developmental red flag.

quiz.q: At what age should a child be able to copy a circle?

title: 🤰 AMNIOTIC FLUID EMBOLISM
content:
Catastrophic Emergency! Amniotic fluid enters maternal circulation.

• Triad: Sudden Cardiorespiratory collapse + DIC + Seizures/Coma.
• Timing: Usually during labor or immediately postpartum.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is an unpredictable and often fatal event. Management is Aggressive Supportive Care (ABCs, fluids, blood products for DIC). High mortality rate for both mother and fetus.

quiz.q: What is the hallmark coagulopathy seen in amniotic fluid embolism?

title: 🧱 SIGMOID VOLVULUS
content:
Twisting of the sigmoid colon on its mesentery, causing obstruction.

• Patient: Elderly, institutionalized, or chronic constipation.
• X-ray: 'Coffee Bean' sign (large U-shaped loop).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Initial management for stable patients is Sigmoidoscopic Detorsion (rectal tube). If there's signs of ischemia or it fails, then Surgery (Hartmann's) is required.

quiz.q: What is the classic radiographic sign of a sigmoid volvulus?

title: 🧂 PRIMARY HYPERALDOSTERONISM (CONN'S)
content:
Excessive aldosterone production from the adrenal gland.

• Triad: Hypertension + Hypokalemia + Metabolic Alkalosis.
• Screening: Aldosterone-to-Renin Ratio (ARR). Expected: High Aldosterone, Low Renin.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Most patients are actually normokalemic initially. Suspect Conn's in any patient with Resistant Hypertension. Treatment for adrenal hyperplasia is Spironolactone; for adenoma, it's surgical removal.

quiz.q: What is the screening test of choice for primary hyperaldosteronism?

title: 🩸 SICKLE CELL: CRISIS MANAGEMENT
content:
Autosomal recessive defect in Hgb (HgbS).

• Vaso-occlusive Crisis: Severe bone/chest/abdominal pain. Triggered by cold, hypoxia, or infection.
• Acute Chest Syndrome: Fever + Chest pain + New infiltrate on CXR (Medical Emergency).

⚠️ Exam Trap: First-line for Pain: Aggressive hydration and Opiates (Morphine). For Acute Chest or Stroke, the answer is Exchange Transfusion. Hydroxyurea is used long-term to increase HgbF.

quiz.q: What medication is used long-term in sickle cell patients to reduce the frequency of crises?

title: 🧠 ADJUSTMENT DISORDER
content:
Emotional or behavioral symptoms in response to an identifiable stressor.

• Timing: Starts within 3 months of stressor; ends within 6 months after stressor is gone.
• Severity: Distressing but does NOT meet criteria for MDD or PTSD.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the symptoms meet the full criteria for Major Depression, the diagnosis is MDD, even if a stressor is present. Psychotherapy is the treatment of choice for adjustment disorder.

quiz.q: How long after the termination of a stressor must symptoms of an adjustment disorder resolve?

title: ⚖️ COLON CANCER SCREENING (CANADA 2024)
content:
Current Canadian Task Force recommendations.

• Who: Adults aged 50 - 74 (Average risk).
• Test: FIT (Fecal Immunochemical Test) every 2 years.
• Or: Flexible Sigmoidoscopy every 10 years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Screening is NOT recommended after age 75 for average-risk individuals. If a First-degree relative had colon cancer < 60y, start screening at Age 40 (or 10 years before their diagnosis) using Colonoscopy every 5 years.

quiz.q: What is the recommended screening interval for FIT in average-risk individuals in Canada?

title: 👶 CONGENITAL HYPOTHYROIDISM
content:
Most common preventable cause of intellectual disability.

• Signs: Prolonged jaundice, large fontanelle, Macroglossia, umbilical hernia, hypotonia (floppy baby).
• Screening: Part of the universal newborn screen (TSH/T4).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Most babies look Normal at birth due to maternal T4 crossing the placenta. Treatment with Levothyroxine must start immediately (within 2 weeks) to prevent permanent brain damage.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of congenital hypothyroidism worldwide?
quiz.a: Thyroid dysgenesis (abnormal development of the gland).

title: 🧠 ACOUSTIC NEUROMA (SCHWANNOMA)
content:
Benign tumor of the 8th Cranial Nerve.

• Triad: Unilateral Sensorineural hearing loss + Tinnitus + Disequilibrium.
• Association: If Bilateral, strongly suspect Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Any adult with unexplained unilateral hearing loss must have an MRI with Gadolinium to rule out acoustic neuroma.

quiz.q: Which cranial nerve is primarily affected by an acoustic neuroma?

title: 🦴 ANKYLOSING SPONDYLITIS (AS)
content:
Chronic inflammatory arthritis of the axial skeleton.

• Key Clue: Morning stiffness that improves with exercise/movement.
• Imaging: Sacroiliitis (earliest change) and Bamboo Spine (late).
• Extra-articular: Acute Anterior Uveitis (#1), Aortic Regurgitation.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Strongly associated with HLA-B27. First-line treatment is NSAIDs and regular physical therapy. If NSAIDs fail, use TNF-inhibitors (Etanercept).

quiz.q: What is the most common extra-articular manifestation of ankylosing spondylitis?

title: 🧪 BETA-BLOCKER OVERDOSE
content:
Bradycardia, Hypotension, and potentially hypoglycemia.

• Management:
1. Atropine (often ineffective).
2. Glucagon: The specific 'antidote' (increases cAMP in heart).
3. Calcium, High-dose Insulin, and Pacing for severe cases.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Propropanol is lipophilic and can cause Seizures and QRS widening. Always check an ECG in these patients.

quiz.q: What is the 'antidote' of choice for beta-blocker toxicity with refractory bradycardia?

title: 🧪 RHABDOMYOLYSIS
content:
Breakdown of muscle fibers releasing myoglobin into the blood.

• Causes: Crush injury, prolonged immobilization, Statins, extreme exercise.
• Triad: Myalgia + Weakness + Dark (Tea-colored) Urine.
• Labs: Very High CK (> 5x normal), Hyperkalemia, Hyperphosphatemia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: On urinalysis, you will see Positive blood but NO RBCs (the dipstick is reacting to myoglobin). The main goal is Aggressive IV Fluids to prevent Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN).

quiz.q: Which electrolyte abnormality is most immediately life-threatening in rhabdomyolysis?

title: 🦠 LYME DISEASE (CANADA)
content:
Borrelia burgdorferi transmitted by Ixodes ticks (endemic in Southern Ontario/B.C./Maritimes).

• Early Localized: Erythema Migrans ('Bull's eye' rash).
• Early Disseminated: Facial nerve (CN VII) palsy, AV Block.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If Erythema Migrans is present, the diagnosis is Clinical; you do NOT need serology to start treatment. Treatment is Doxycycline (or Amoxicillin in kids < 8y).

quiz.q: What is the first-line antibiotic for early Lyme disease?

title: 🎗️ OVARIAN TORSION
content:
Surgical Emergency! Twisting of the ovary and/or fallopian tube.

• Presentation: Sudden, severe Unilateral pelvic pain + Nausea/Vomiting.
• Risk Factor: Ovarian Mass (Dermoid cyst is common).
• Dx: Ultrasound with Doppler (may show decreased flow).

⚠️ Exam Trap: A normal Doppler does NOT rule out torsion (it can be intermittent). If suspicion is high, the definitive step is Emergency Laparoscopy to untwist the ovary and assess viability.

quiz.q: What is the most common ultrasound finding in ovarian torsion?
quiz.a: An enlarged, edematous ovary with or without decreased blood flow.

title: 🩸 MGUS VS MULTIPLE MYELOMA
content:
Differentiating asymptomatic plasma cell dyscrasia from malignancy.

FeatureMGUSMultiple Myeloma
M-Protein< 30 g/L> 30 g/L
Plasma Cells< 10%> 10%
CRAB SymptomsAbsentPresent

⚠️ Exam Trap: MGUS is common in the elderly (3% > 50y). It requires 'Watch and Wait' with serial monitoring of CBC and M-protein. Do NOT treat unless it progresses to MM (1% per year risk).

quiz.q: What is the risk of progression from MGUS to Multiple Myeloma per year?

title: 👂 OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION (OME)
content:
Fluid in the middle ear without signs of acute infection.

• Signs: Amber/yellow fluid, decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy, no fever or pain.
• Management: Observe for 3 months (most resolve spontaneously).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT use antibiotics, decongestants, or antihistamines for OME; they don't work. If it persists > 3 months or causes Hearing Loss, refer for Myringotomy & Tubes (PE tubes).

quiz.q: What is the first-line management for a child with new-onset OME and no hearing loss?

title: 🦵 LERICHE SYNDROME (TRIAD)
content:
Aortoiliac occlusive disease (atherosclerosis at the bifurcation).

The Classic Triad:
1. Claudication of hips, thighs, and buttocks.
2. Absent or weak Femoral Pulses.
3. Erectile Dysfunction.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Suspect this in a male smoker with lower limb pain and sexual dysfunction. Diagnosis is by ABI followed by CTA/MRA for surgical planning (Aortobifemoral bypass).

quiz.q: What makes up the classic triad of Leriche syndrome?
quiz.a: Hip/thigh claudication, weak femoral pulses, and erectile dysfunction.

title: 🧪 WILSON'S DISEASE
content:
Autosomal recessive defect in copper excretion.

• Signs: Liver disease, Parkinsonian symptoms (tremor/rigidity), and Psychiatric issues.
• Labs: Low Ceruloplasmin, High urinary copper.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The pathognomonic sign is Kayser-Fleischer rings in the cornea (seen on slit-lamp). Treatment is copper chelation with Penicillamine or Zinc.

quiz.q: What is the primary protein deficiency in Wilson's disease?

title: 🫀 AORTIC REGURGITATION (AR)
content:
Leaking of the aortic valve during diastole.

• Murmur: Early diastolic decrescendo murmur at the LSB, heard best with the patient leaning forward and in full expiration.
• Signs: Wide Pulse Pressure (e.g., 160/50), Water-hammer pulse (Corrigan pulse).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Chronic AR leads to left ventricular dilation. Acute AR (e.g., from dissection or endocarditis) is a surgical emergency with sudden heart failure. ACE inhibitors are used to reduce afterload in chronic cases.

quiz.q: What happens to the pulse pressure in a patient with significant Aortic Regurgitation?

title: 🧠 RAISED ICP (CUSHING'S REFLEX)
content:
A late sign of increased intracranial pressure and impending herniation.

Cushing's Triad:
1. Hypertension (Widening pulse pressure).
2. Bradycardia.
3. Irregular Respirations.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is the opposite of Shock (which has hypotension and tachycardia). If you see Cushing's triad after head trauma, give Mannitol or Hypertonic saline and call Neurosurgery immediately.

quiz.q: What are the three components of Cushing’s Triad?

title: 🎗️ HYSTEROSALPINGOGRAM (HSG)
content:
Radiographic evaluation of the uterus and fallopian tubes.

• Purpose: To check for Tubal Patency (blockage) and uterine anomalies.
• Timing: Done during the follicular phase (Days 7-10) to avoid pregnancy.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If HSG shows proximal tubal blockage, it might be a spasm; if distal blockage (Hydrosalpinx), surgery or IVF is usually required. Note: HSG can occasionally be therapeutic by flushing out debris.

quiz.q: What is the primary indication for performing an HSG in an infertility workup?

title: 🧠 DEMENTIA VS PSEUDODEMENTIA
content:
FeatureDementia (Neurocognitive)Pseudodementia (Depression)
OnsetGradual / ChronicSudden / Identifiable
EffortStruggles to answer'I don't know' (Poor effort)
AwarenessHides deficitsComplains of memory loss

⚠️ Exam Trap: In an elderly patient with memory loss, ALWAYS screen for depression (GDS scale). Pseudodementia is reversible with antidepressants.

quiz.q: Which condition is characterized by a patient who gives 'I don't know' answers with minimal effort during memory testing?

title: 👶 HUS (HEMOLYTIC UREMIC SYNDROME)
content:
Follows a GI infection with E. coli O157:H7 (Shiga toxin).

The Triad:
1. Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia (Schistocytes).
2. Thrombocytopenia (Low platelets).
3. Acute Kidney Injury (High Cr).

⚠️ Exam Trap: NEVER give Antibiotics for E. coli diarrhea in kids; it increases the release of Shiga toxin and the risk of HUS. Management is purely supportive (fluids/dialysis).

quiz.q: What is the most common trigger for HUS in children?
quiz.a: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (often from undercooked beef or contaminated water).

title: 🍭 HYPOGLYCEMIA (WHIPPLE'S TRIAD)
content:
Used to evaluate patients for Insulinoma or other causes of low sugar.

The Triad:
1. Symptoms of hypoglycemia (sweating, tremor, confusion).
2. Low blood glucose (< 3.0 mmol/L) measured at the time of symptoms.
3. Relief of symptoms after glucose administration.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If Whipple's triad is present, check C-peptide. High Insulin + High C-peptide = Insulinoma. High Insulin + Low C-peptide = Exogenous insulin use (factitious).

quiz.q: What does a high C-peptide level during a hypoglycemic episode suggest?
quiz.a: Endogenous insulin production (e.g., Insulinoma or Sulfonylurea use).

title: 🔪 DIVERTICULITIS (HINCHEY STAGES)
content:
StageDescriptionManagement
ISmall pericolic abscessAntibiotics
IIPelvic / Retroperitoneal abscessDrainage (Percutaneous)
IIIPurulent PeritonitisSurgery (Hartmann's)
IVFecal PeritonitisEmergency Surgery

⚠️ Exam Trap: In acute diverticulitis, Colonoscopy is CONTRAINDICATED due to the risk of perforation. Perform a CT Abdomen for diagnosis. Wait 6 weeks after the flare for a colonoscopy.

quiz.q: When is a colonoscopy performed after an episode of acute diverticulitis?
quiz.a: Approximately 6-8 weeks after the acute episode has resolved.

title: 🦴 OSTEOPOROSIS (FRAX SCORE)
content:
Assessing fracture risk in Canada (Osteoporosis Canada 2024).

• Screening: All adults ≥ 65 years with BMD (DXA scan).
• FRAX: Used to calculate the 10-year risk of major osteoporotic fracture.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Start Bisphosphonates if: 10-year risk is > 20% (High risk), or if there is a prior fragility fracture of the hip or spine, regardless of BMD!

quiz.q: At what age should routine BMD screening begin for average-risk individuals?

title: 🩸 THALASSEMIA (ALPHA VS BETA)
content:
TypeMutationClassic Clue
AlphaGene DeletionSoutheast Asian / African heritage
BetaPoint MutationMediterranean heritage / High HgbA2


⚠️ Exam Trap: In Beta-Thalassemia minor, the RBC count is often High despite low Hemoglobin. Differentiate from Iron deficiency by the Mentzer Index (MCV/RBC < 13). Hemoglobin Electrophoresis is diagnostic for Beta but NOT for Alpha trait.

quiz.q: Which type of thalassemia shows elevated Hemoglobin A2 (HgbA2) on electrophoresis?

title: 🧤 DERMATOMYOSITIS
content:
Inflammatory myopathy + Skin findings.

• Skin: Heliotrope rash (purple eyelids) and Gottron Papules (scaly knuckles).
• Muscle: Symmetric Proximal muscle weakness (difficulty climbing stairs).
• Labs: High CK and Anti-Jo-1 antibodies.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Dermatomyositis in an adult is a Paraneoplastic Syndrome in up to 25% of cases. You must perform an age-appropriate Cancer Screening (Lung, GI, Ovary) once diagnosed.

quiz.q: What is the most characteristic skin finding on the knuckles of a patient with dermatomyositis?

title: ⚖️ MAID: SAFEGUARDS
content:
Canadian safeguards for Medical Assistance in Dying.

• Criteria: Must be 18+, capable, and have a grievous and irremediable condition.
• Safeguards: Two independent physicians/NP must sign off. Patient must be informed they can withdraw at any time.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For patients whose death is not reasonably foreseeable (Track 2), there is a mandatory 90-day assessment period. One of the assessors must have expertise in the patient's specific medical condition.

quiz.q: What is the mandatory assessment period for a patient requesting MAID whose natural death is NOT reasonably foreseeable?

title: 🫀 HYPERTROPHIC CARDIOMYOPATHY (HCM)
content:
Autosomal dominant mutation in sarcomere proteins. Common cause of sudden death in young athletes.

• Murmur: Harsh systolic murmur at LSB. Increases with Valsalva/Standing (decreased preload); Decreases with Squatting.
• Dx: Echo showing asymmetrical septal hypertrophy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is the opposite of most murmurs (AS/MR). In HCM, anything that makes the heart 'smaller' (less blood) makes the murmur Louder. Beta-blockers are first-line for symptoms.

quiz.q: What happens to the HCM murmur during a Valsalva maneuver?

title: 🧠 CAROTID ARTERY STENOSIS
content:
Management of carotid plaque to prevent stroke.

• Symptomatic (TIA/Stroke): Surgery (CEA) if stenosis 70-99%. (Strong benefit).
• Asymptomatic: Surgery only if stenosis > 60-80% and life expectancy > 5 years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the stenosis is 100% (Complete Occlusion), surgery is NOT indicated because the risk of embolization is gone. Medical management (Aspirin/Statin) is always the foundation.

quiz.q: At what percentage of symptomatic stenosis is Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA) most beneficial?

title: 👶 LARYNGOMALACIA
content:
Most common cause of congenital stridor.

• Signs: Inspiratory stridor that worsens when supine or during feeding, improves when prone.
• Dx: Flexible Laryngoscopy showing 'Omega-shaped' epiglottis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Most cases are Self-limiting and resolve by 18-24 months. Management is usually Reassurance unless there is 'Failure to Thrive' or severe cyanosis (then surgery - Supraglottoplasty).

quiz.q: How does the stridor in laryngomalacia change with positioning?
quiz.a: It worsens in the supine position and improves when the infant is prone.

title: 🤰 HELLP SYNDROME
content:
A severe variant of Preeclampsia.

H: Hemolysis (Schistocytes, High LDH).
EL: Elevated Liver enzymes (ALT/AST).
LP: Low Platelets (< 100).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Patients often present with Epigastric / RUQ pain and nausea (can be mistaken for GI issues). The definitive treatment is Delivery (usually regardless of gestational age if HELLP is confirmed). Give Magnesium Sulfate for seizure prophylaxis!

quiz.q: What is the definitive treatment for HELLP syndrome?
quiz.a: Delivery of the fetus and placenta.

title: 🧠 PROJECTION VS DISPLACEMENT
content:
MechanismDefinitionExample
ProjectionAttributing your unwanted feelings to someone else.'I hate my boss' becomes 'My boss hates me'.
DisplacementShifting feelings to a neutral/safer target.Angry at boss → Goes home and yells at wife/dog.
Reaction FormationDoing the opposite of your true impulse.Loves someone → Acts mean/cold to them.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Sublimation (channeling energy into healthy work/sports) and Humor are 'Mature' defense mechanisms. The others are immature/neurotic.

quiz.q: A man who is angry at his physician kicks his dog when he gets home. What defense mechanism is he using?

title: ⚖️ MATURE MINOR IN QUEBEC (14+ RULE)
content:
Special rule for the province of Quebec in the MCCQE1.

• Rule: In Quebec, a minor 14 years or older can consent to health care required by their state of health alone.
• Limitation: If the minor is hospitalized for > 12 hours, parents must be informed.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In the rest of Canada, there is no fixed age (Mature Minor Doctrine). In Quebec, 14 is the 'magic number' for the exam. If the minor refuses life-saving treatment, a court order is needed.

quiz.q: In Quebec, at what age can a minor generally consent to healthcare without parental permission?

title: 🌬️ SARCOIDOSIS (LOFGREN'S SYNDROME)
content:
Multisystem granulomatous disease.

Lofgren's Triad:
1. Bilateral Hilar Adenopathy.
2. Erythema Nodosum (painful leg lumps).
3. Arthralgia (Joint pain).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Sarcoidosis causes Hypercalcemia (due to increased Vitamin D production by macrophages). Lofgren's syndrome has a Good Prognosis and often resolves without steroids.

quiz.q: What is the classic electrolyte abnormality seen in sarcoidosis?

title: 🔥 ELECTRICAL BURNS (MYOGLOBINURIA)
content:
Electrical injuries often have hidden 'internal' damage.

• Major Risk: Rhabdomyolysis leading to Acute Kidney Injury.
• Signs: 'Dark' urine, high CK.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In electrical burns, the target urine output is HIGHER (75-100 mL/hr) than in thermal burns to flush out myoglobin. Always check an ECG for arrhythmias (even if the patient feels fine!).

quiz.q: What is the goal urine output for a patient with an electrical burn and myoglobinuria?

title: 👶 NECROTIZING ENTEROCOLITIS (NEC)
content:
Life-threatening emergency in Premature infants.

• Signs: Abdominal distension, bloody stools, gastric residuals, feeding intolerance.
• X-ray: Pneumatosis Intestinalis (air in the bowel wall).

⚠️ Exam Trap: First-line management: NPO + IV Fluids + Antibiotics + NG Tube decompression. Surgery is only needed if there is Pneumoperitoneum (Perforation).

quiz.q: What is the pathognomonic x-ray finding for NEC?

title: 🩸 WARFARIN BRIDGE THERAPY
content:
Switching from Heparin (Fast) to Warfarin (Slow).

• Mechanism: Warfarin inhibits Factors II, VII, IX, X AND Protein C/S.
• Risk: Protein C has a short half-life; stopping it early causes a Hypercoagulable State.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST overlap (Bridge) Heparin and Warfarin for at least 5 days AND until the INR is therapeutic (2-3) for 24 hours. This prevents Warfarin-induced Skin Necrosis.

quiz.q: What is the minimum number of days for bridging heparin and warfarin?

title: 🧂 HYPERCALCEMIA OF MALIGNANCY
content:
Most common cause of high calcium in hospitalized patients.

• Mechanism: Production of PTHrP (PTH-related protein).
• Associated Cancers: Squamous cell (Lung/Head/Neck), Renal cell, Breast.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In malignancy, PTH will be LOW. (In primary hyperparathyroidism, PTH is High). Initial treatment is Aggressive Normal Saline Hydration followed by IV Bisphosphonates (Zoledronic acid).

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for severe hypercalcemia?
quiz.a: Aggressive IV hydration with Normal Saline.

title: 🩸 POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (4 T'S)
content:
Leading cause of maternal mortality. PPH = > 500mL (Vaginal) or > 1000mL (C-section).

The 4 T's:
1. Tone: Uterine Atony (#1 cause).
2. Tissue: Retained products.
3. Trauma: Lacerations / Rupture.
4. Thrombin: Coagulopathy (DIC).

⚠️ Exam Trap: First step for atony: Bimanual Uterine Massage + Oxytocin. If that fails, move to Carboprost (not in asthma!) or Misoprostol.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of Postpartum Hemorrhage?

title: 🧱 SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION: TX
content:
TypeInitial Management
Partial / Non-toxicNPO, IV Fluids, NG Tube (Decompression).
Complete / ToxicEmergent Surgery (Laparotomy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: 80% of adhesion-related SBO resolve with Conservative management (NG tube) within 48-72h. If pain increases, fever develops, or WBC rises, the bowel is 'strangulating' → GO TO OR.

quiz.q: What is the first-line diagnostic imaging for suspected SBO?
quiz.a: Abdominal X-ray (Upright and Supine) or CT Abdomen.

title: 🦴 OSGOOD-SCHLATTER DISEASE
content:
Traction apophysitis of the tibial tuberosity.

• Patient: Adolescent athlete (basketball/soccer) going through a growth spurt.
• Sign: Tender bump at the Tibial Tuberosity. Pain with resisted knee extension.

[attachment_0](attachment)
⚠️ Exam Trap: This is Benign and Self-limiting. No need for X-rays or surgery. Treatment is Activity Modification, Ice, and NSAIDs. It resolves when the growth plate closes.

quiz.q: Where is the pain located in Osgood-Schlatter disease?
quiz.a: The tibial tuberosity (at the insertion of the patellar tendon).

title: 🧪 NEPHRITIC SYNDROME
content:
Inflammation of the glomerulus leading to blood in urine.

The Tetrad:
1. Hematuria (Cola-colored / RBC Casts).
2. Hypertension.
3. Oliguria.
4. Mild-Moderate Proteinuria (< 3.5 g/day).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The presence of RBC Casts is pathognomonic for glomerular bleeding (Nephritis). Unlike Nephrotic syndrome, these patients are Hypertensive and have an active urine sediment.

quiz.q: What is the pathognomonic finding on urinalysis for glomerulonephritis (nephritic syndrome)?

title: 🧴 BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (BCC)
content:
The most common skin cancer. Slow-growing and rarely metastasizes.

• Appearance: Pearly papule with Telangiectasia (spider veins) and a central ulcer ('Rodent ulcer').
• Location: Sun-exposed areas (face, nose).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Diagnosis is by Shave or Punch Biopsy. First-line treatment is Surgical Excision with 4mm margins or Mohs surgery for high-risk areas (the face).

quiz.q: What is the most characteristic clinical feature of a basal cell carcinoma?

title: 🧴 SCABIES (SARCOPTES SCABIEI)
content:
Highly contagious parasitic infestation.

• Signs: Intense nocturnal pruritus and linear Burrows in web spaces (fingers), wrists, and axilla.
• Tx: Permethrin 5% cream (Apply neck-down, leave for 8-14h, repeat in 1 week).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST treat all close contacts simultaneously, even if asymptomatic, and wash all bedding/clothes in hot water. Itching may persist for 2-4 weeks after successful treatment (post-scabietic itch).

quiz.q: What is the hallmark clinical finding of scabies?
quiz.a: Burrows in the finger webs and intense itching at night.

title: 👂 MENIERE’S DISEASE
content:
Excess endolymphatic fluid pressure in the inner ear.

The Classic Triad:
1. Episodic Vertigo (lasting 20 mins to hours).
2. Sensorineural Hearing Loss (fluctuating, low-frequency).
3. Tinnitus / Aural fullness.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Unlike BPPV, Meniere’s includes Hearing Loss. Long-term management focuses on a Low-Salt Diet and avoiding caffeine/alcohol. Diuretics (HCTZ) may be added.

quiz.q: How does the duration of vertigo in Meniere’s compare to BPPV?
quiz.a: Meniere's episodes last 20 mins to hours, while BPPV lasts seconds to <1 min.

title: 🫁 LUNG CANCER: SMALL VS NON-SMALL CELL
content:
FeatureSmall Cell (SCLC)Non-Small Cell (NSCLC)
LocationCentral / PerihilarPeripheral (Adeno) / Central (Squamous)
AssociationSmoking (Strong)Smoking / Asbestos
ManagementChemo/Radiation (Rarely surgery)Surgical Resection (if early stage)

⚠️ Exam Trap: SCLC is highly aggressive and often presents with Paraneoplastic Syndromes (ACTH/Cushing's or SIADH). NSCLC (Squamous) is associated with Hypercalcemia (PTHrP).

quiz.q: Which type of lung cancer is most strongly associated with paraneoplastic SIADH?

title: 👶 MECONIUM ILEUS VS HIRSCHSPRUNG
content:
Distinguishing causes of delayed meconium in neonates.

FeatureMeconium IleusHirschsprung Disease
AssociationCystic FibrosisDown Syndrome
Level of obstructionIleum (thick mucus)Rectosigmoid (no nerves)
Contrast EnemaMicrocolonTransition zone

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Meconium Ileus, the stool is thick and 'putty-like'. In Hirschsprung, a digital rectal exam often leads to an 'Explosive' release of gas and stool (Blast sign).

quiz.q: Meconium ileus is virtually pathognomonic for which genetic condition?

title: 🧠 SUBARACHNOID HEMORRHAGE (SAH)
content:
Bleeding into the subarachnoid space, often from a Berry Aneurysm.

• Presentation: 'Worst headache of my life' (Thunderclap headache), meningismus (stiff neck), photophobia.
• Dx: 1st: Non-contrast CT Head. 2th: Lumbar Puncture (if CT is negative).


⚠️ Exam Trap: On LP, look for Xanthochromia (yellow CSF due to bilirubin). To prevent the complication of Vasospasm (occurs 3-10 days later), start Nimodipine immediately.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive test for SAH if the initial CT head is negative?

title: 🟡 ASCENDING CHOLANGITIS
content:
Surgical Emergency! Bacterial infection of the bile ducts.

Reynolds' Pentad:
1. Fever.
2. RUQ Pain.
3. Jaundice (Charcot's Triad).
4. Hypotension.
5. Altered Mental Status.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Management is ERCP for biliary decompression + IV Antibiotics. Do NOT wait for the patient to 'stabilize' if they have the Pentad; they need the ERCP to survive.

quiz.q: What are the five components of Reynolds' Pentad?
quiz.a: Fever, RUQ pain, jaundice, hypotension, and altered mental status.

title: 🧠 MYASTHENIA VS LAMBERT-EATON
content:
FeatureMyasthenia GravisLambert-Eaton (LEMS)
PathophysiologyPost-synaptic (AchR)Pre-synaptic (Ca channels)
WeaknessWorsens with useImproves with use
ReflexesNormalDiminished / Absent
AssociationThymomaSmall Cell Lung Cancer

⚠️ Exam Trap: In LEMS, autonomic symptoms (dry mouth, impotence) are common. Always screen for SCLC in these patients. MG classically involves extraocular muscles (Ptosis/Diplopia) first.

quiz.q: Which syndrome is associated with Small Cell Lung Cancer and improves with repeated muscle use?

title: 🧪 GOODPASTURE VS WEGENER'S (GPA)
content:
Pulmonary-Renal Syndromes (Hemoptysis + Hematuria).

• Goodpasture: Anti-GBM antibodies. Linear IgG deposits on biopsy.
• Wegener's (GPA): c-ANCA positive. Granulomatous inflammation of Upper Airway (sinusitis) and Lungs.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has Sinusitis / Saddle nose + Hemoptysis + Hematuria, it is GPA. Treatment involves Steroids + Cyclophosphamide or Rituximab.

quiz.q: Which antibody is highly specific for Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Wegener's)?

title: 🤰 POLYHYDRAMNIOS VS OLIGOHYDRAMNIOS
content:
ConditionAFI LevelCommon Causes
Polyhydramnios> 24 cmMaternal Diabetes, Fetal GI atresia.
Oligohydramnios< 5 cmPlacental Insufficiency, Renal agenesis (Potter).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Oligohydramnios in the 3rd trimester is a sign of chronic fetal hypoxia (blood shunted from kidneys to brain). It is a major component of the Biophysical Profile (BPP).

quiz.q: What is the most common maternal cause of polyhydramnios?

title: 👶 PEDIATRIC UTI WORKUP
content:
Management of first febrile UTI in a child < 2 years.

• Step 1: Renal/Bladder Ultrasound (RBUS) for all children < 2y after first UTI.
• Step 2: VCUG only if RBUS is abnormal or if UTI is recurrent/atypical.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For the exam, do NOT order a VCUG after the very first UTI if the ultrasound is normal. We want to avoid radiation unless necessary.

quiz.q: What is the first imaging study for a 1-year-old with their first febrile UTI?

title: ⚖️ BREAKING BAD NEWS (SPIKES)
content:
The standard protocol for delivering difficult information.

• S: Setting (Privacy).
• P: Perception (What does the patient know?).
• I: Invitation (How much do they want to know?).
• K: Knowledge (Give facts in small chunks).
• E: Empathy (Validate feelings).
• S: Strategy / Summary.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always start with 'Perception' - "What is your understanding of why we did these tests?". Do not rush into the diagnosis without assessing the patient's readiness.

quiz.q: What does the 'P' in the SPIKES protocol stand for?

title: 🧪 COCAINE TOXICITY
content:
Sympathomimetic excess.

• Signs: Tachycardia, Hypertension, Mydriasis (dilated pupils), Chest pain.
• Management: Benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment.

⚠️ Exam Trap: NEVER use Beta-blockers (like Propranolol) in cocaine-induced chest pain. It causes 'Unopposed Alpha Stimulation' leading to severe coronary vasospasm and MI!

quiz.q: Which class of medications is strictly contraindicated in cocaine-induced chest pain?

title: 🩸 MYELODYSPLASTIC SYNDROMES (MDS)
content:
Clonal stem cell disorders causing ineffective hematopoiesis.

• Patient: Typically elderly (> 70y).
• Labs: Pancytopenia (or bicytopenia).
• Smear: Ring Sideroblasts or Pelger-Huet anomaly (bilobed neutrophils).


⚠️ Exam Trap: MDS is a 'Pre-leukemia' state. It has a high risk of transforming into Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML). Diagnosis requires Bone Marrow Aspiration/Biopsy.

quiz.q: What is the primary concern with MDS in an elderly patient?

title: 🚬 SMOKING CESSATION (PHARMACOLOGY)
content:
AgentMechanismContraindication
VareniclinePartial Nicotine AgonistSevere psychiatric history (caution).
BupropionNE/Dopamine Reuptake InhibitorSeizures / Eating Disorders.
NRTNicotine ReplacementRecent MI (< 2 weeks).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Varenicline (Chantix) is currently the most effective monotherapy for smoking cessation. Bupropion is a great choice if the patient also has Depression.

quiz.q: Which smoking cessation medication is contraindicated in patients with Bulimia or Anorexia?

title: 🎀 BREAST CANCER SCREENING (CANADA 2024)
content:
Updated guidelines for average-risk women.

• Age 40-49: Individual decision (Shared decision-making). Screening is not routine but available if requested.
• Age 50-74: Routine screening with Mammography every 2 years.
• Age 75+: Stop screening (unless life expectancy > 10 years).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Clinical Breast Exam (CBE) and Self-Exams are NOT recommended for screening average-risk women in Canada (as they don't reduce mortality and increase biopsies). Focus on Mammography.

quiz.q: What is the recommended screening interval for mammography in women aged 50-74 in Canada?

title: 🕊️ PALLIATIVE PAIN & DYSPNEA
content:
Managing end-of-life symptoms with compassion.

• Pain: Use the WHO Pain Ladder. Morphine is the gold standard.
• Dyspnea: Low-dose Opioids (Morphine) are the first-line treatment for the sensation of breathlessness (air hunger).
• Secretions: Use Glycopyrrolate or Scopolamine ('Death rattle').

⚠️ Exam Trap: Oxygen is only helpful if the patient is actually hypoxic. For the sensation of dyspnea in a dying patient, Opioids are more effective than oxygen. Do not give fluids for dehydration at the very end (it increases secretions/edema).

quiz.q: What is the first-line medication for dyspnea in a palliative care patient?

title: 🏗️ PELVIC ORGAN PROLAPSE (POP)
content:
[attachment_0](attachment)
Herniation of pelvic organs into the vaginal canal.

• Cystocele: Bladder (Anterior wall).
• Rectocele: Rectum (Posterior wall).
• Uterine Prolapse: Uterus descends through the cervix.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Initial management for all types of POP is Conservative: Pelvic floor exercises (Kegels) and Vaginal Pessary fitting. Surgery (Colporrhaphy/Hysterectomy) is reserved for patients who fail or decline conservative measures.

quiz.q: What is the most common non-surgical mechanical treatment for pelvic organ prolapse?

title: 🦴 RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS: DMARDS
content:
Aggressive early treatment to prevent joint destruction.

• First-line: Methotrexate (MTX) is the 'Anchor' drug.
• Side Effects: Oral ulcers, Hepatotoxicity, Pulmonary Fibrosis, Macrocytic anemia (Low Folate).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always co-prescribe Folic Acid with Methotrexate to reduce side effects. Before starting MTX, you must check CBC, LFTs, and CXR, and ensure the patient is using reliable Contraception (it is highly teratogenic).

quiz.q: Which supplement should be given to all patients taking Methotrexate for RA?

title: 🍔 CHRONIC MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA
content:
'Intestinal Angina' due to atherosclerosis of mesenteric arteries.

• Classic Presentation: Postprandial pain (30-60 mins after eating) + Fear of food + Significant weight loss.
• Dx: CT Angiography or Doppler U/S of the mesenteric vessels.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Unlike Acute mesenteric ischemia (which is a sudden surgical emergency), Chronic ischemia is a slow, progressive disease. Management focuses on Risk Factor Modification (smoking/statins) and revascularization (Stent or Bypass).

quiz.q: What is the hallmark clinical finding of chronic mesenteric ischemia?

title: 👶 DEVELOPMENTAL RED FLAGS
content:
Critical timepoints where lack of a milestone requires immediate referral.

• No Social Smile: 3 months.
• No Sitting: 9 months.
• No Walking: 18 months.
• No Single Words: 15 months.
• No 2-word phrases: 2 years.
• Any loss of skills (Regression): AT ANY AGE.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Regression (e.g., a child who could talk but now doesn't) is the most serious red flag. It often requires urgent evaluation for Autism, hearing loss, or metabolic disorders.

quiz.q: At what age is not walking considered a major developmental red flag?

title: ⚖️ BOUNDARIES: ROMANTIC RELATIONSHIPS
content:
Canadian (CPSO/CMPA) rules on dating patients.

• Rule: NEVER date a current patient. It is professional misconduct.
• Former Patients: There must be a significant 'waiting period' (usually 1 year).
• Psychiatry: You can NEVER date a former psychiatry patient (it is an absolute, lifelong ban).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient makes a romantic advance, the physician's duty is to re-establish the professional boundary. If the boundary is irreparably breached, you must transfer care to another physician properly.

quiz.q: Is a physician ever allowed to date a former psychiatry patient?
quiz.a: No, it is considered a permanent professional and ethical violation.

title: 🫀 SECONDARY HYPERTENSION: CLUES
content:
When to look for an underlying cause of high blood pressure.

• Clues: Sudden onset, Age < 30 or > 60, Resistant to 3+ meds, Hypokalemia.
• Causes:
- Renovascular: Bruit over renal arteries.
- Conn's: Low K+.
- Pheo: Episodes of sweating/headache.
- Coarctation: BP higher in arms than legs.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Renal Artery Stenosis in a young female is usually Fibromuscular Dysplasia ('string of beads' on imaging). In an elderly male, it's Atherosclerosis.

quiz.q: What is the most likely cause of secondary hypertension in a young woman with a renal bruit?

title: 🦠 HIV: CD4 THRESHOLDS & PROPHYLAXIS
content:
Preventing opportunistic infections based on immune status.

• CD4 < 200: PCP (Pneumocystis). Tx: TMP-SMX (Septra).
• CD4 < 100: Toxoplasmosis. Tx: TMP-SMX.
• CD4 < 50: MAC (Mycobacterium avium). (Note: Routine prophylaxis for MAC is no longer recommended if on effective ART).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If an HIV patient has CD4 < 200 and is allergic to Sulfa, the second-line for PCP prophylaxis is Dapsone or Atovaquone. Always ensure the patient is on ART (Antiretroviral Therapy) regardless of CD4 count.

quiz.q: At what CD4 count should prophylaxis for Pneumocystis jirovecii (PCP) be started?

title: 🏆 THE MASTER VAULT CARD: TOP 10 TRAPS
content:
The final review of high-probability MCCQE1 'Gotchas'.

1. Epiglottitis: No tongue depressor!
2. Ectopic: hCG > 1500 + empty uterus = Ectopic.
3. Meningitis: Steroids before or with first Abx.
4. Appendicitis: Clinical diagnosis in males; CT for females.
5. Warfarin: Bridge with Heparin for 5 days.
6. Anaphylaxis: IM Epinephrine (mid-thigh) is step #1.
7. Tension Pneumo: Needle decompression before X-ray.
8. Delirium: Treat the cause; avoid Benzos (unless ETOH withdrawal).
9. Peds Fever: < 28 days old = Full sepsis workup + Admission.
10. Suicide: Asking about it does NOT increase the risk; it's mandatory.

🌟 Captain's Note: You have 450 Elite Cards. This vault is now your strongest weapon. Trust your clinical intuition and remember: Safety First, then Diagnosis. Good luck, Doctor!

quiz.q: What is the priority in an unstable patient with suspected tension pneumothorax?

title: 🪶 CULTURAL SAFETY VS COMPETENCE
content:
A core concept in the MCCQE1 regarding care for First Nations, Inuit, and Métis peoples.

• Cultural Competence: Knowledge about the patient's culture (The 'What').
• Cultural Safety: Addressing Power Imbalances and systemic racism. It is defined by the Patient, not the doctor (The 'How').

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient from an Indigenous community feels uncomfortable, the physician must practice Self-reflection on their own biases. Cultural safety means the patient feels safe and respected, and their identity is not challenged.

quiz.q: Who defines whether a medical encounter was 'culturally safe'?

title: 🗣️ DISCLOSURE OF ADVERSE EVENTS
content:
How to handle medical errors in Canada (CMPA guidelines).

• Key Steps:
1. Acknowledge: Tell the patient what happened.
2. Apologize: Say 'I am sorry' (In Canada, an apology is NOT a legal admission of guilt).
3. Plan: Explain how to mitigate harm and prevent recurrence.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must disclose Harmful events and Near misses (if the patient is at risk of future harm). The disclosure should be done as soon as reasonably possible. Do NOT blame other staff or the system in front of the patient.

quiz.q: Does an apology for a medical error constitute a legal admission of liability in Canada?
quiz.a: No, most provinces have 'Apology Acts' that protect physicians.

title: 🫀 SYNCOPE: THE INITIAL STEP
content:
A common 'Next Best Step' question.

• Initial Step: Detailed History and Physical Exam (including orthostatic vitals) + ECG for everyone.
• If high risk (Chest pain, CHF, abnormal ECG): Admit for telemetry.
• If low risk: Discharge or further outpatient testing (Holter).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT order a 'CT Head' or 'Carotid Doppler' for simple syncope unless there are focal neurological deficits. The ECG is the single most important initial investigation.

quiz.q: What is the mandatory initial investigation for any patient presenting with syncope?

title: 📊 SENSITIVITY VS SPECIFICITY
content:
Core stats for the exam.

• Sensitivity (SnNout): High sensitivity rules OUT disease when negative. (Used for Screening).
• Specificity (SpPIn): High specificity rules IN disease when positive. (Used for Confirmation).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Sensitivity and Specificity are Intrinsic properties of the test; they do NOT change with the prevalence of the disease in the population. (Unlike PPV and NPV).

quiz.q: Which statistical measure changes when the prevalence of a disease in the population increases?
quiz.a: Positive Predictive Value (PPV) increases; Negative Predictive Value (NPV) decreases.

title: 🪶 JORDAN'S PRINCIPLE
content:
A vital Canadian child-first principle for Indigenous children.

• Rule: Ensures that First Nations children can access all public services (health, social, educational) without delay caused by jurisdictional disputes between federal and provincial governments.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If there is a dispute over who pays for a child's medical equipment, the Government of First Contact pays first, and they figure out the bill later. The child’s health comes first.

quiz.q: What is the primary goal of Jordan's Principle?
quiz.a: To ensure First Nations children get the services they need without delays related to government funding disputes.

title: 🌬️ SOLITARY PULMONARY NODULE
content:
Managing an incidental finding on CXR.

• Step 1: Compare with Previous Imaging (CXR/CT).
• Step 2: If stable for 2 years → Reassurance.
• Step 3: If new or changing → CT Chest.

⚠️ Exam Trap: High-risk features (Age > 40, Smoker, Spiculated borders, size > 2cm) require Biopsy or PET scan. Low-risk features (Smooth, small, stable) can be followed with serial CTs.

quiz.q: What is the most important first step when a solitary pulmonary nodule is found on a chest X-ray?

title: ⚖️ THE HIDDEN CURRICULUM
content:
A concept about informal learning in medicine.

• Definition: The unwritten, unofficial, and often unintended lessons, values, and perspectives that students/residents learn in the clinical environment (e.g., how staff talk about patients).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you witness a senior resident or staff making an inappropriate comment about a patient, the 'Professional' response is to Address it privately or through appropriate channels, ensuring patient dignity is maintained.

quiz.q: What term describes the unofficial values and behaviors learned by observing clinical mentors?

title: 📋 REPORTABLE DISEASES (CANADA)
content:
Physicians must report certain diseases to Public Health without patient consent.

• Examples: Syphilis, Gonorrhea, HIV, TB, Measles, Hepatitis A/B/C, Meningococcal disease.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For STIs, you report to Public Health, and THEY handle the partner notification (Contact Tracing). You should encourage the patient to tell their partners, but Public Health has the legal authority to ensure it happens.

quiz.q: Does a physician need patient consent to report a case of Tuberculosis to Public Health?
quiz.a: No, it is a legal requirement for public safety.

title: ⚡ ECG: WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE (WPW)
content:
The Triad:
1. Short PR interval (< 120ms).
2. Delta Wave (Slurred upstroke of QRS).
3. Wide QRS complex.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient with WPW has Atrial Fibrillation, do NOT give AV-nodal blockers (Beta-blockers, CCBs, Digoxin, Adenosine). They can cause 1:1 conduction through the bypass tract and lead to VFib/Death. Use Procainamide or Cardioversion.

quiz.q: What is the name of the slurred upstroke seen on the QRS in WPW?

title: 🦋 THYROID NODULE WORKUP
content:
StepAction
1TSH and Ultrasound Neck
2 (If TSH Low)Thyroid Scintigraphy (Scan)
3 (If Hot Nodule)Evaluate for Hyperthyroidism
4 (If Cold or Normal TSH)FNA (Fine Needle Aspiration) based on US size/features


⚠️ Exam Trap: A 'Hot' nodule is rarely malignant. The highest risk for cancer is a Cold nodule or a nodule in a patient with a Normal TSH.

quiz.q: What is the next step for a thyroid nodule if the TSH is normal and US shows suspicious features?

title: 🪶 RESIDENTIAL SCHOOLS IMPACT
content:
A dark part of Canadian history with ongoing health impacts.

• Health Legacy: High rates of chronic disease, mental health issues, and Intergenerational Trauma.
• Physician's Role: To acknowledge the history and provide Trauma-Informed Care.

⚠️ Exam Trap: When treating an Indigenous elder, be aware that their distrust of the healthcare system may stem from historical institutional abuse. Building Trust is the priority before clinical intervention.

quiz.q: What term describes trauma passed down from survivors of residential schools to their children?

title: 🧠 TIA: THE ABCD2 SCORE
content:
Used to predict stroke risk after a Transient Ischemic Attack.

Components: Age, Blood Pressure, Clinical features, Duration, Diabetes.
• Score ≥ 4: High risk; usually requires admission and rapid workup.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The 'Next Best Step' for TIA in Canada is Imaging of the Carotid Arteries (Doppler or CTA) and ECG to look for AFib, along with starting Antiplatelets (Aspirin + Clopidogrel for high risk).

quiz.q: What is the recommended initial imaging for a patient with a TIA to assess for surgical stenosis?

title: 📚 EVIDENCE-BASED MEDICINE (LEVELS)
content:
LevelStudy Type
1 (Highest)Meta-analysis / Systematic Review of RCTs.
2Single Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT).
3Cohort Studies.
4Case-control Studies.
5 (Lowest)Expert Opinion / Case Reports.

⚠️ Exam Trap: When a question asks for the Best Evidence to change clinical practice, always look for the Meta-analysis or Systematic Review of RCTs.

quiz.q: What is considered the highest level of evidence in clinical research?
quiz.a: Systematic reviews or Meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials.

title: 🧴 ERYTHEMA MULTIFORME (EM)
content:
Appearance: Classic 'Target' or 'Bulls-eye' lesions (3 concentric color zones).
• Associations: Most commonly Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV); also Mycoplasma and drugs (Sulfa).

⚠️ Exam Trap: EM is usually limited and benign. If there is mucosal involvement and systemic symptoms, consider Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS), which is a medical emergency and usually drug-related.

quiz.q: What is the most common viral trigger for erythema multiforme?

title: 📊 NUMBER NEEDED TO TREAT (NNT)
content:
A measure of clinical effectiveness.

$$NNT = rac{1}{ARR} = rac{1}{(CER - EER)}$$
• ARR: Absolute Risk Reduction.
• Rule: The Lower the NNT, the more effective the treatment.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must be able to calculate this. If a drug reduces the risk of stroke from 5% to 3%, the ARR is 2% (0.02). The NNT is 1 / 0.02 = 50. You need to treat 50 people to prevent 1 stroke.

quiz.q: How is NNT calculated from Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR)?

title: 🌬️ SUSPECTED PE: THE ALGORITHM
content:
A classic exam scenario. Your action depends on the Wells Score.
