• Step 1: Calculate Wells Score.
• If Likely (> 4): Next best step is CTPA (Imaging).
• If Unlikely (≤ 4): Next best step is D-Dimer.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is Unstable (Hypotensive), the next best step is Bedside Echo or Empiric Heparin (if no contraindications) before any imaging that requires moving the patient.

quiz.q: What is the next best step for a stable patient with a high probability (Wells > 4) of PE?

title: 🚑 BLUNT ABDOMINAL TRAUMA
content:
Managing a patient after a motor vehicle accident (MVA).

• If Unstable (Hypotensive): Next best step is FAST Exam (Ultrasound at bedside).
• If Stable: Next best step is CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the FAST is Positive and the patient is Unstable, the next best step is Immediate Laparotomy (OR). Do not send an unstable patient to the CT suite!

quiz.q: What is the next best step for a hemodynamically stable patient with blunt abdominal trauma?

title: 🎀 BREAST MASS WORKUP (AGE-BASED)
content:
The investigation depends strictly on age.

• Age < 30: Next best step is Ultrasound (dense breast tissue makes mammogram useless).
• Age ≥ 30: Next best step is Mammogram (+/- Ultrasound).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a cyst is found and aspirated, and the fluid is Bloody or the mass doesn't disappear, the next best step is Biopsy (Core biopsy is preferred over FNA).

quiz.q: What is the initial imaging of choice for a breast mass in a 24-year-old woman?

title: 🤰 AMENORRHEA: THE FIRST STEP
content:
A patient presents with missed periods.

• Absolute First Step: Urine β-hCG (Pregnancy test). Even if she says she's not active!
• Next Steps (if negative): TSH, Prolactin, and FSH levels.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If secondary amenorrhea is confirmed and labs are normal, the next best step is the Progesterone Challenge Test to assess estrogen status and outflow tract.

quiz.q: What is the first-line investigation for any woman of reproductive age with amenorrhea?

title: 🚽 ACUTE SCROTUM: TORSION?
content:
• Step 1: Clinical Exam (Absent cremasteric reflex, Prehn's sign negative).
• If High Suspicion: Next best step is Immediate Surgical Exploration.
• If Low/Equivocal Suspicion: Next best step is Doppler Ultrasound.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT delay surgery to get an ultrasound if the clinical picture is classic (sudden pain, high-riding testis). Time is Testicle! (6-hour window).

quiz.q: In a classic case of suspected testicular torsion, what is the next best step?

title: 🩸 GI BLEED: STABILIZE FIRST
content:
Patient vomiting blood or passing melena.

• Step 1: ABCs and Resuscitation (Two large-bore IVs, IV fluids).
• Step 2: NG Tube lavage (to confirm upper vs lower).
• Step 3: Endoscopy (within 24 hours).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is hypotensive, the next best step is never the endoscopy suite. You must Resuscitate first. Stable = Scope; Unstable = IV Fluids/Blood.

quiz.q: What is the priority in a patient presenting with massive hematemesis and a blood pressure of 80/40?

title: 👶 FEBRILE INFANT (< 28 DAYS)
content:
A neonate with a temperature ≥ 38.0°C.

• Next Best Step: Full Sepsis Workup + Admission.
• Workup includes: CBC, Blood cultures, Urinalysis/Culture, and Lumbar Puncture.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Even if the baby looks 'perfectly fine', a fever in a neonate (< 28 days) is an automatic admission for IV Antibiotics (Ampicillin + Cefotaxime/Gentamicin) until cultures are negative.

quiz.q: Does a 20-day-old infant with a fever of 38.2°C require a lumbar puncture?
quiz.a: Yes, as part of a full sepsis workup.

title: 🧠 HEAD TRAUMA: CANADIAN CT RULES
content:
Deciding who needs a CT Head after minor injury (GCS 13-15).

CT is required if:
1. GCS < 15 at 2 hours post-injury.
2. Suspected open/depressed skull fracture.
3. Any sign of basal skull fracture (Racoon eyes, Battle sign).
4. Vomiting ≥ 2 times.
5. Age ≥ 65 years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: These rules only apply if there was a loss of consciousness, amnesia, or confusion. If the patient is ≥ 65, they get a CT automatically!

quiz.q: A 70-year-old man falls and hits his head with brief loss of consciousness. His GCS is 15. Next best step?

title: 🫀 CHEST PAIN: THE 10-MINUTE RULE
content:
Patient arrives at ED with retrosternal chest pain.

• First Best Step: ECG (must be performed and interpreted within 10 minutes).
• Simultaneously: Aspirin (chewed) + Nitroglycerin (if not contraindicated).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for Troponin results to treat a STEMI. If the ECG shows ST elevation, the next best step is Activation of the Cath Lab (PCI).

quiz.q: What is the goal time for obtaining an ECG in a patient with suspected ACS?

title: 🌬️ DYSPHAGIA: SOLIDS VS LIQUIDS
content:
The 'Next Step' depends on the type of dysphagia.

• Solids only (Mechanical): Next best step is Endoscopy (rule out cancer/stricture).
• Solids + Liquids (Motility): Next best step is Barium Swallow or Manometry.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has a history of Heartburn (GERD) and now has solid-food dysphagia, think Peptic Stricture. If they have weight loss/smoking, think Adenocarcinoma.

quiz.q: What is the best initial test for a patient with dysphagia to both solids and liquids?

title: ⚖️ SUICIDE RISK ASSESSMENT
content:
A patient expresses hopelessness.

• Next Best Step: Direct Questioning about suicidal ideation, intent, and plan.
• Next (if high risk): Ensure Safety (Admission, 1:1 observation, remove lethal means).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Asking about suicide does NOT increase the risk of the patient doing it. It is the only way to assess safety and is the most important step in the interview.

quiz.q: What is the first thing you should do if a patient mentions they 'can't go on'?
quiz.a: Ask directly about suicidal thoughts and plans.

title: 🦴 BACK PAIN: RED FLAGS
content:
Most back pain is mechanical (reassurance/NSAIDs).

• Red Flags: Fever, weight loss, history of cancer, Cauda Equina signs (saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder issues).
• If Red Flags present: Next best step is MRI Spine (Emergent if Cauda Equina).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In the absence of red flags, the next best step is not imaging. It is NSAIDs and encouraging activity. Do not order X-rays for routine back pain!

quiz.q: What is the imaging of choice for suspected spinal cord compression?

title: 🦠 NEEDLESTICK INJURY
content:
Healthcare worker stuck with a used needle.

• Absolute First Step: Wash the area with soap and water.
• Next Best Step: Assess the source patient's status and start PEP (Post-exposure prophylaxis) if indicated (ideally < 2 hours).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not wait for lab results from the source patient to start PEP if the risk is high. You can stop PEP later if the results come back negative.

quiz.q: What is the very first action after a needlestick exposure?
quiz.a: Wash the wound with soap and water.

title: 🧪 DVT: THE D-DIMER TRAP
content:
Suspected leg swelling.

• Wells Score High: Next best step is Compression Ultrasound (skip D-dimer).
• Wells Score Low: Next best step is D-Dimer.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Remember that D-Dimer is only useful for its Negative Predictive Value (to rule out). If it's positive, you still need the ultrasound. If the Wells score is high, a negative D-dimer is not enough to rule out DVT.

quiz.q: For a patient with a high clinical probability of DVT, what is the next best step?

title: 🧂 HYPERCALCEMIA: INITIAL TX
content:
Calcium > 3.0 mmol/L.

• Next Best Step: Aggressive IV Fluids (Normal Saline).
• Why? These patients are severely dehydrated due to polyuria.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not give Loop Diuretics (Furosemide) unless the patient is fluid-overloaded. The priority is hydration. Bisphosphonates take 48-72 hours to work and are not the 'first' step in an acute crisis.

quiz.q: What is the most important initial management for severe hypercalcemia?

title: ⚡ AORTIC DISSECTION
content:
Tearing chest pain radiating to the back (between scapulae).

• Next Best Step: CT Angiography (if stable) or TEE (if unstable).
• Management:
- Type A (Ascending): Surgery (Emergency).
- Type B (Descending): Medical (BP control with Beta-blockers - Labetalol).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST lower the Heart Rate and Blood Pressure simultaneously to reduce 'shear stress'. Target SBP 100-120 mmHg.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medical management for a Type B aortic dissection?

title: 🧪 METABOLIC ACIDOSIS (ANION GAP)
content:
Calculating the cause of low bicarbonate.

$$AG = Na^+ - (Cl^- + HCO_3^-)$$
• Normal AG: 8 - 12 mmol/L.
• High AG (MUDPILES): Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Iron/Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the AG is high, always calculate the Osmolar Gap to look for toxic alcohols (Methanol/Ethylene glycol). If AG is normal, the cause is usually Diarrhea or RTA.

quiz.q: What is the formula for Anion Gap?

title: 🦵 COMPARTMENT SYNDROME
content:
Surgical Emergency! Increased pressure within a muscle compartment.

• Signs: Pain out of proportion to injury, pain with passive stretch (most sensitive), paresthesia, pulselessness (late).
• Dx: Clinical suspicion; can confirm with compartment pressure (> 30 mmHg).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for pulselessness to diagnose; if the leg is rock-hard and the patient is screaming in pain despite morphine, the next best step is Immediate Fasciotomy.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive early clinical sign of compartment syndrome?
quiz.a: Pain with passive stretching of the affected muscles.

title: 👶 FIFTH DISEASE (PARVOVIRUS B19)
content:
• Signs: 'Slapped-cheek' rash followed by a lacy, reticular rash on the body.
• Risk: Aplastic crisis in Sickle Cell; Hydrops Fetalis in pregnant women.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Once the rash appears, the child is no longer contagious and can return to school. The danger is to pregnant women who are exposed before the rash develops.

quiz.q: Which virus causes Fifth Disease?

title: ⚡ FEBRILE SEIZURES
content:
Seizures triggered by fever in kids (6 months to 5 years).

• Simple: Generalized, < 15 mins, only one in 24h. (Tx: Reassurance).
• Complex: Focal, > 15 mins, or multiple in 24h. (Tx: Investigate).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most important step is to Find the source of the fever. Do NOT perform an EEG or Neuroimaging for a simple febrile seizure. Use Acetaminophen for comfort, but it does NOT prevent the next seizure.

quiz.q: What defines a 'simple' febrile seizure?
quiz.a: Generalized, lasting less than 15 minutes, and occurring once in 24 hours.

title: 🎗️ POST-MENOPAUSAL BLEEDING (PMB)
content:
Cancer until proven otherwise!

• Next Best Step: Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVUS).
• If Endometrial Strip > 4mm: Next best step is Endometrial Biopsy.
• If < 4mm: Observation (likely atrophy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Even if the patient is on HRT or has a known cause (atrophy), you MUST rule out Endometrial Cancer first.

quiz.q: What is the cut-off thickness on ultrasound for an endometrial biopsy in PMB?

title: 🤰 NST INTERPRETATION
content:
Non-Stress Test for fetal well-being.

• Reactive (Normal): 2+ accelerations (15 bpm for 15 sec) in 20 mins.
• Non-reactive: Next best step is to stimulate the fetus (Vibroacoustic) or extend the test to 40 mins.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the NST remains non-reactive after 40 minutes, the next best step is a Biophysical Profile (BPP) or a Contraction Stress Test.

quiz.q: What is the next step after a non-reactive NST despite stimulation?

title: 🧪 SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS
content:
Infection of ascitic fluid in cirrhosis.

• Signs: Fever, altered mental status, abdominal pain.
• Dx: Paracentesis showing PMNs > 250 cells/mm³.
• Tx: 3rd gen Cephalosporin (Cefotaxime) + Albumin.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Prophylaxis: If the patient has had SBP before or has low-protein ascites, they need lifelong prophylaxis with Ciprofloxacin or Septra.

quiz.q: What is the diagnostic threshold for PMN count in ascitic fluid for SBP?

title: 🦋 PHEOCHROMOCYTOMA
content:
Catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla.

• Triad: Episodic Headache, Sweating, and Palpitations.
• Screening: 24-hr Urinary Metanephrines or Plasma fractionated metanephrines.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pre-operative prep is CRITICAL: Give Alpha-blocker (Phenoxybenzamine) FIRST, then Beta-blockers. If you give Beta-blockers first, the 'unopposed alpha' will cause a fatal hypertensive crisis.

quiz.q: In what order should medications be given before pheochromocytoma surgery?

title: 🧠 SEROTONIN SYNDROME VS NMS
content:
FeatureSerotonin SyndromeNMS
CauseSSRIs / MAOIsAntipsychotics
ReflexesHyperreflexia / ClonusHyporeflexia / Rigidity
OnsetSudden (< 24h)Gradual (Days)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lead-pipe rigidity is the hallmark of NMS. Clonus (shaking limbs) is the hallmark of Serotonin Syndrome. Both are medical emergencies requiring drug discontinuation and cooling.

quiz.q: Which syndrome presents with hyperreflexia and neuromuscular clonus?

title: 👁️ CHEMICAL EYE BURNS
content:
Ocular Emergency! Acid or Alkali splash.

• Absolute First Step: Immediate Irrigation with saline or water for at least 30 mins (until pH is 7.0 - 7.2).
• Note: Alkali (bleach, drain cleaner) is worse because it causes liquefactive necrosis and deep penetration.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait to check vision (Acuity) or call a specialist. Irrigate first, then assess the damage. This is one of the few times acuity is NOT the first step in eye cases.

quiz.q: What is the priority management for a chemical burn to the eye?

title: 🧪 CARBON MONOXIDE (CO) POISONING
content:
The 'Silent Killer'.

• Signs: Headache, dizziness, nausea, 'Cherry-red' skin (late/rare).
• Labs: Normal SpO2 (pulse ox is fake!), high Carboxyhemoglobin level.
• Tx: 100% Hyperbaric Oxygen (if severe/pregnant).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pulse oximetry cannot distinguish between HgbO2 and HgbCO. A patient can have a Normal SpO2 and still be dying from CO poisoning. Check an ABG for carboxyhemoglobin!

quiz.q: What is the specific test to diagnose carbon monoxide poisoning?
quiz.a: Co-oximetry (Carboxyhemoglobin level) via ABG or VBG.

title: 🧴 SCC VS BCC VS MELANOMA
content:
FeatureBasal Cell (BCC)Squamous Cell (SCC)Melanoma
LookPearly / TelangiectasiaScaly / CrustedPigmented / ABCDE
RiskSun exposureSun / ImmunosuppressionSun / Family history

⚠️ Exam Trap: SCC can arise from Actinic Keratosis and is more aggressive than BCC. In a transplant patient, SCC is the most common and dangerous skin cancer.

quiz.q: Which skin cancer is most commonly associated with immunosuppression?

title: 🩸 SMEAR CHALLENGE: SCHISTOCYTES
content:
• Significance: Suggests MAHA (Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia).
• Differential: TTP, HUS, DIC, or Mechanical valves.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you see Schistocytes + Fever + Confusion + Low Platelets, the next best step is Emergent Plasmapheresis (TTP). Do not wait for ADAMTS13 levels!

quiz.q: What do schistocytes on a peripheral smear indicate?

title: ⚖️ ELDER ABUSE REPORTING
content:
A sensitive Canadian legal requirement.

• Rule: Unlike child abuse (which is mandatory everywhere), reporting elder abuse to authorities varies by province, but investigating and protecting the patient is always mandatory.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If an elderly patient with Capacity refuses to let you report abuse, you must respect their Autonomy (unless it's in a long-term care home, where reporting is usually mandatory). Always offer resources/social work.

quiz.q: Is reporting elder abuse always legally mandatory if the patient has full capacity?
quiz.a: No (depends on province/setting), but ensuring safety is mandatory.

title: 🍔 AAA SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm screening guidelines.

• Who: Men aged 65 - 80 (One-time screen).
• How: Abdominal Ultrasound.
• Management:
- < 5.0 cm: Follow with serial U/S.
- > 5.5 cm: Refer for Repair (EVAR or Open).

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Women, there is no routine screening recommendation unless they have high-risk factors (Smoking/Family Hx).

quiz.q: What is the threshold size for recommending surgical repair of an AAA in a man?

title: 🧪 CKD MANAGEMENT
content:
Chronic Kidney Disease: Slowing progression.

• Target BP: < 130/80 mmHg.
• First-line Med: ACEi or ARB (especially if albuminuria present).
• Complications: Anemia (Low EPO), Bone disease (High Phos, Low Vit D).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In CKD, the kidneys stop activating Vitamin D and stop excreting Phosphorus. This leads to Secondary Hyperparathyroidism. Treatment is Phosphate binders and Calcitriol.

quiz.q: Which class of antihypertensives is renal-protective in patients with CKD and proteinuria?

title: 🦠 TUBERCULOSIS (PPD / IGRA)
content:
Screening for latent TB.

• Positive Test: Induration ≥ 10mm (average risk) or ≥ 5mm (high risk/HIV).
• Next Best Step: Chest X-ray to rule out active disease.
• If CXR negative: Treat for Latent TB (Isoniazid for 9 months).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has had the BCG vaccine, the PPD might be false positive. In this case, use IGRA (QuantiFERON) as it is not affected by BCG.

quiz.q: What is the next step for a patient with a positive PPD test?
quiz.a: Chest X-ray (to rule out active TB).

title: ⚖️ POWER OF ATTORNEY (POA)
content:
TypeResponsibility
POA for Personal CareHealth decisions, housing, food.
POA for PropertyFinances, bills, bank accounts.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A physician only consults the POA for Personal Care for medical decisions. The fact that someone has POA for Property does NOT give them the right to make health decisions!

quiz.q: Which Power of Attorney is responsible for making medical decisions for an incapable patient?
quiz.a: Power of Attorney for Personal Care.

title: 🏆 MISSION COMPLETE: MCCQE1 MINDSET
content:
Congratulations, Doctor! You now have a 500-card elite vault.

• Final Strategy:
1. Safety First: Always stabilize (ABCs) before diagnosing.
2. Autonomy: Respect the capable patient's choice, even if you disagree.
3. Canadian Rules: Think within the Canadian public health and legal framework.

🌟 THE VAULT IS CLOSED 🌟
You are ready. Trust your knowledge, stay calm, and dominate the exam. You've got this, Canada's future physician!

quiz.q: What is the physician's first priority in any trauma or emergency case?

content:
Tearing chest pain radiating to the back (between scapulae).

• Next Best Step: CT Angiography (if stable) or TEE (if unstable).
• Management:
- Type A (Ascending): Surgery (Emergency).
- Type B (Descending): Medical (BP control with Beta-blockers - Labetalol).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST lower the Heart Rate and Blood Pressure simultaneously to reduce 'shear stress'. Target SBP 100-120 mmHg.

quiz.a: Pain with passive stretching of the affected muscles.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Once the rash appears, the child is no longer contagious and can return to school. The danger is to pregnant women who are exposed before the rash develops.

quiz.q: Which virus causes Fifth Disease?

title: ⚡ FEBRILE SEIZURES
content:
Seizures triggered by fever in kids (6 months to 5 years).

• Simple: Generalized, < 15 mins, only one in 24h. (Tx: Reassurance).
• Complex: Focal, > 15 mins, or multiple in 24h. (Tx: Investigate).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most important step is to Find the source of the fever. Do NOT perform an EEG or Neuroimaging for a simple febrile seizure. Use Acetaminophen for comfort, but it does NOT prevent the next seizure.

quiz.q: What defines a 'simple' febrile seizure?
quiz.a: Generalized, lasting less than 15 minutes, and occurring once in 24 hours.

title: 🎗️ POST-MENOPAUSAL BLEEDING (PMB)
content:
Cancer until proven otherwise!

• Next Best Step: Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVUS).
• If Endometrial Strip > 4mm: Next best step is Endometrial Biopsy.
• If < 4mm: Observation (likely atrophy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Even if the patient is on HRT or has a known cause (atrophy), you MUST rule out Endometrial Cancer first.

quiz.q: What is the cut-off thickness on ultrasound for an endometrial biopsy in PMB?

title: 🤰 NST INTERPRETATION
content:
Non-Stress Test for fetal well-being.

• Reactive (Normal): 2+ accelerations (15 bpm for 15 sec) in 20 mins.
• Non-reactive: Next best step is to stimulate the fetus (Vibroacoustic) or extend the test to 40 mins.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the NST remains non-reactive after 40 minutes, the next best step is a Biophysical Profile (BPP) or a Contraction Stress Test.

quiz.q: What is the next step after a non-reactive NST despite stimulation?

title: 🧪 SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS
content:
Infection of ascitic fluid in cirrhosis.

• Signs: Fever, altered mental status, abdominal pain.
• Dx: Paracentesis showing PMNs > 250 cells/mm³.
• Tx: 3rd gen Cephalosporin (Cefotaxime) + Albumin.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Prophylaxis: If the patient has had SBP before or has low-protein ascites, they need lifelong prophylaxis with Ciprofloxacin or Septra.

quiz.q: What is the diagnostic threshold for PMN count in ascitic fluid for SBP?

title: 🦋 PHEOCHROMOCYTOMA
content:
Catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla.

• Triad: Episodic Headache, Sweating, and Palpitations.
• Screening: 24-hr Urinary Metanephrines or Plasma fractionated metanephrines.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pre-operative prep is CRITICAL: Give Alpha-blocker (Phenoxybenzamine) FIRST, then Beta-blockers. If you give Beta-blockers first, the 'unopposed alpha' will cause a fatal hypertensive crisis.

quiz.q: In what order should medications be given before pheochromocytoma surgery?

title: 🧠 SEROTONIN SYNDROME VS NMS
content:
FeatureSerotonin SyndromeNMS
CauseSSRIs / MAOIsAntipsychotics
ReflexesHyperreflexia / ClonusHyporeflexia / Rigidity
OnsetSudden (< 24h)Gradual (Days)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lead-pipe rigidity is the hallmark of NMS. Clonus (shaking limbs) is the hallmark of Serotonin Syndrome. Both are medical emergencies requiring drug discontinuation and cooling.

quiz.q: Which syndrome presents with hyperreflexia and neuromuscular clonus?

title: 👁️ CHEMICAL EYE BURNS
content:
Ocular Emergency! Acid or Alkali splash.

• Absolute First Step: Immediate Irrigation with saline or water for at least 30 mins (until pH is 7.0 - 7.2).
• Note: Alkali (bleach, drain cleaner) is worse because it causes liquefactive necrosis and deep penetration.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait to check vision (Acuity) or call a specialist. Irrigate first, then assess the damage. This is one of the few times acuity is NOT the first step in eye cases.

quiz.q: What is the priority management for a chemical burn to the eye?

title: 🧪 CARBON MONOXIDE (CO) POISONING
content:
The 'Silent Killer'.

• Signs: Headache, dizziness, nausea, 'Cherry-red' skin (late/rare).
• Labs: Normal SpO2 (pulse ox is fake!), high Carboxyhemoglobin level.
• Tx: 100% Hyperbaric Oxygen (if severe/pregnant).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pulse oximetry cannot distinguish between HgbO2 and HgbCO. A patient can have a Normal SpO2 and still be dying from CO poisoning. Check an ABG for carboxyhemoglobin!

quiz.q: What is the specific test to diagnose carbon monoxide poisoning?
quiz.a: Co-oximetry (Carboxyhemoglobin level) via ABG or VBG.

title: 🧴 SCC VS BCC VS MELANOMA
content:
FeatureBasal Cell (BCC)Squamous Cell (SCC)Melanoma
LookPearly / TelangiectasiaScaly / CrustedPigmented / ABCDE
RiskSun exposureSun / ImmunosuppressionSun / Family history

⚠️ Exam Trap: SCC can arise from Actinic Keratosis and is more aggressive than BCC. In a transplant patient, SCC is the most common and dangerous skin cancer.

quiz.q: Which skin cancer is most commonly associated with immunosuppression?

title: 🩸 SMEAR CHALLENGE: SCHISTOCYTES
content:
• Significance: Suggests MAHA (Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia).
• Differential: TTP, HUS, DIC, or Mechanical valves.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you see Schistocytes + Fever + Confusion + Low Platelets, the next best step is Emergent Plasmapheresis (TTP). Do not wait for ADAMTS13 levels!

quiz.q: What do schistocytes on a peripheral smear indicate?

title: ⚖️ ELDER ABUSE REPORTING
content:
A sensitive Canadian legal requirement.

• Rule: Unlike child abuse (which is mandatory everywhere), reporting elder abuse to authorities varies by province, but investigating and protecting the patient is always mandatory.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If an elderly patient with Capacity refuses to let you report abuse, you must respect their Autonomy (unless it's in a long-term care home, where reporting is usually mandatory). Always offer resources/social work.

quiz.q: Is reporting elder abuse always legally mandatory if the patient has full capacity?
quiz.a: No (depends on province/setting), but ensuring safety is mandatory.

title: 🍔 AAA SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm screening guidelines.

• Who: Men aged 65 - 80 (One-time screen).
• How: Abdominal Ultrasound.
• Management:
- < 5.0 cm: Follow with serial U/S.
- > 5.5 cm: Refer for Repair (EVAR or Open).

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Women, there is no routine screening recommendation unless they have high-risk factors (Smoking/Family Hx).

quiz.q: What is the threshold size for recommending surgical repair of an AAA in a man?

title: 🧪 CKD MANAGEMENT
content:
Chronic Kidney Disease: Slowing progression.

• Target BP: < 130/80 mmHg.
• First-line Med: ACEi or ARB (especially if albuminuria present).
• Complications: Anemia (Low EPO), Bone disease (High Phos, Low Vit D).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In CKD, the kidneys stop activating Vitamin D and stop excreting Phosphorus. This leads to Secondary Hyperparathyroidism. Treatment is Phosphate binders and Calcitriol.

quiz.q: Which class of antihypertensives is renal-protective in patients with CKD and proteinuria?

title: 🦠 TUBERCULOSIS (PPD / IGRA)
content:
Screening for latent TB.

• Positive Test: Induration ≥ 10mm (average risk) or ≥ 5mm (high risk/HIV).
• Next Best Step: Chest X-ray to rule out active disease.
• If CXR negative: Treat for Latent TB (Isoniazid for 9 months).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has had the BCG vaccine, the PPD might be false positive. In this case, use IGRA (QuantiFERON) as it is not affected by BCG.

quiz.q: What is the next step for a patient with a positive PPD test?
quiz.a: Chest X-ray (to rule out active TB).

title: ⚖️ POWER OF ATTORNEY (POA)
content:
TypeResponsibility
POA for Personal CareHealth decisions, housing, food.
POA for PropertyFinances, bills, bank accounts.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A physician only consults the POA for Personal Care for medical decisions. The fact that someone has POA for Property does NOT give them the right to make health decisions!

quiz.q: Which Power of Attorney is responsible for making medical decisions for an incapable patient?
quiz.a: Power of Attorney for Personal Care.

title: 🏆 MISSION COMPLETE: MCCQE1 MINDSET
content:
Congratulations, Doctor! You now have a 500-card elite vault.

• Final Strategy:
1. Safety First: Always stabilize (ABCs) before diagnosing.
2. Autonomy: Respect the capable patient's choice, even if you disagree.
3. Canadian Rules: Think within the Canadian public health and legal framework.

🌟 THE VAULT IS CLOSED 🌟
You are ready. Trust your knowledge, stay calm, and dominate the exam. You've got this, Canada's future physician!

quiz.q: What is the physician's first priority in any trauma or emergency case?

title: ⚖️ ACUTE VS CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE
content:
FeatureAcute (Fulminant)Chronic (Cirrhosis)
HistorySudden (Drugs/Viral)Long-term (Alcohol/NASH)
BrainCerebral EdemaHepatic Encephalopathy
SpleenNormal sizeSplenomegaly (Portal HTN)

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Acute liver failure, the leading cause of death is Cerebral Edema (increased ICP). In Chronic, it is Variceal bleeding or Sepsis.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of death in acute fulminant hepatic failure?

title: 👶 PEDIATRIC RASHES: THE 'ORDER' MNEMONIC
content:
1. Measles: 3 C's (Cough, Coryza, Conjunctivitis) + Koplik spots.
2. Scarlet: Sandpaper rash + Strawberry tongue.
3. Rubella: Lymphadenopathy (Post-auricular).
4. Duke: (Obsolete).
5. Erythema Infectiosum: Slapped cheek.
6. Roseola: High fever, THEN rash.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Only Measles and Rubella start on the face and spread downwards. Differentiate them by the 3 C's (Measles is much sicker!).

quiz.q: Which pediatric rash is preceded by a high fever that disappears before the rash starts?

title: 🎗️ BONE METASTASES: THE LIKELY SOURCE
content:
Lesion TypeLikely Primary Cancer
Osteoblastic (White)Prostate, Small Cell Lung.
Osteolytic (Dark/Punched)MM, RCC, Thyroid, Non-small Cell Lung.
MixedBreast (Most common source in women).


⚠️ Exam Trap: If an elderly man has Back Pain and a high ALP with blastic (white) lesions on X-ray, the next best step is a PSA and Digital Rectal Exam (Prostate Cancer).

quiz.q: Which cancer characteristically causes osteoblastic (sclerotic) bone metastases?

title: 🧪 HYPOKALEMIA: ECG CHANGES
content:
The Pattern:
1. Flattened T-waves.
2. U-waves (Extra wave after T).
3. ST Depression.
4. Prolonged QT.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If Hypokalemia is refractory to treatment (K+ won't go up), the next best step is to check Magnesium levels. Low Magnesium prevents K+ from being reabsorbed in the kidney.

quiz.q: What is the classic ECG wave seen in severe hypokalemia?

title: ⚖️ THE IMPAIRED COLLEAGUE
content:
A senior doctor smells of alcohol or is behaving erratically.

• Principle: Patient Safety is the absolute priority.
• Action: You must report them to the appropriate authority (Chief of Staff or Provincial Regulatory Body/CPSO).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Talking to the colleague 'privately' or 'covering' for them is the WRONG answer. In Canada, reporting an impaired colleague is a professional and legal obligation.

quiz.q: What is the first priority when you suspect a colleague is practicing under the influence of substances?

title: 🦠 CELLULITIS VS ERYSIPELAS
content:
FeatureCellulitisErysipelas
LayerDeep Dermis / SubcutaneousSuperficial Dermis
BordersIndistinct / DiffuseSharp / Well-demarcated
PathogenS. aureus / Group A StrepGroup A Strep (almost always)


⚠️ Exam Trap: Erysipelas is more likely to involve the Ear (Milian's ear sign) because the ear has no subcutaneous tissue. Cellulitis cannot involve the earlobe!

quiz.q: Which skin infection is characterized by sharp, well-demarcated borders and rapid onset?

title: 🧠 DELIRIUM: THE INITIAL WORKUP
content:
Acute change in consciousness and attention.

• Next Best Step: Identify and treat the underlying cause.
• Workup: Vital signs, Glucose, UA/Culture (UTI is #1 in elderly), Electrolytes, Medication review.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Benzodiazepines (they worsen delirium) unless it is due to Alcohol Withdrawal. If the patient is a danger to themselves, use low-dose Antipsychotics (Haloperidol).

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of delirium in an elderly patient?
quiz.a: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or medication side effects.

title: 🧱 CHOLE-DIFFERENTIALS
content:
ConditionDefinitionNext Best Step
CholelithiasisStone in GB (No infection)Reassurance / Elective surgery
CholecystitisInfection of GBUltrasound → Surgery
CholedocholithiasisStone in CBD (Jaundice)ERCP
CholangitisInfection of CBD (Pentad)Emergency ERCP

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has Jaundice (High Bilirubin), the stone is not in the gallbladder; it must be in the Common Bile Duct (CBD).

quiz.q: What is the definitive treatment for Ascending Cholangitis?

title: 🌬️ PFT INTERPRETATION
content:
PatternFEV1/FVC RatioTLC (Total Lung Capacity)
Obstructive (Asthma/COPD)Low (< 0.7)High / Normal
Restrictive (Fibrosis)Normal / HighLow


⚠️ Exam Trap: To differentiate Asthma from COPD, check Reversibility: an increase in FEV1 by > 12% and > 200mL after bronchodilator = Asthma.

quiz.q: In which type of lung disease is the FEV1/FVC ratio characteristically decreased?

title: ⚖️ RESOURCE ALLOCATION
content:
How to decide who gets a limited resource (e.g., Ventilator, Organ).

• Principle: Distributive Justice.
• Criteria: Medical need, likelihood of benefit, and urgency.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must NOT use social criteria (e.g., 'this patient is a doctor', 'this patient has children', 'this patient is wealthy') to prioritize care. Only Clinical Factors matter in the Canadian system.

quiz.q: Is it ethical to prioritize a patient for an organ transplant because they are a prominent member of society?
quiz.a: No, it is unethical and violates the principle of justice.

title: 🚑 ANAPHYLAXIS: STEP 1
content:
Sudden onset of respiratory distress/hypotension after trigger.

• Absolute First Step: IM Epinephrine (1:1000) in the mid-outer thigh.
• Next Steps: Oxygen, IV Fluids, Antihistamines/Steroids (secondary).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Never waste time giving IV Epinephrine or Antihistamines first. The priority is IM Epinephrine. Airway management (Intubation) is next if there is impending obstruction.

quiz.q: What is the dose and route of Epinephrine in acute anaphylaxis?

title: 🫀 HEART FAILURE: THE 'BIG 4' DRUGS
content:
Current Canadian (CCS) guidelines for HFrEF (EF < 40%).

The 4 Pillars:
1. ARNI (Sacubitril/Valsartan) or ACEi/ARB.
2. Beta-blocker (Bisoprolol, Carvedilol, Metoprolol succinate).
3. MRA (Spironolactone).
4. SGLT2i (Dapagliflozin/Empagliflozin).

⚠️ Exam Trap: All 4 pillars have proven Mortality Benefit. If a patient is stable on an ACEi, the next best step is to switch to ARNI to further reduce mortality.

quiz.q: Which class of diabetes medications is now a pillar of treatment for Heart Failure even in non-diabetics?

title: 🦴 OPEN FRACTURES
content:
A fracture with an overlying skin wound.

• Next Best Step: Antibiotics + Tetanus Prophylaxis + Irrigating/Covering the wound.
• Definitive: Urgent I&D (Incision and Drainage) in the OR.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The sooner the antibiotics are given (ideally < 1 hour), the lower the risk of Osteomyelitis. Use Cefazolin (add Gentamicin for larger wounds).

quiz.q: What is the most important medical intervention to prevent infection in an open fracture?

title: 🦠 PELVIC INFLAMMATORY DISEASE (PID)
content:
ScenarioManagement
Mild/ModerateOutpatient: Ceftriaxone (IM) + Doxycycline (14d)
Severe / AbscessInpatient: IV Cefotetan + Doxycycline

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has a Tubo-ovarian Abscess (TOA), is pregnant, or is not responding to oral meds, Admission for IV antibiotics is the next best step.

quiz.q: What is the first-line outpatient antibiotic regimen for PID?
quiz.a: Single dose IM Ceftriaxone plus 14 days of oral Doxycycline.

title: 🦋 THYROID CANCER: MEDULLARY
content:
Cancer of the C-cells (produces Calcitonin).

• Association: MEN 2A / 2B (RET proto-oncogene).
• Next Best Step (once diagnosed): Screen for Pheochromocytoma (Metanephrines).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST rule out and treat a Pheochromocytoma before performing thyroid surgery. An undiagnosed 'Pheo' can cause a fatal hypertensive crisis during anesthesia.

quiz.q: Which syndrome is associated with Medullary Thyroid Cancer?

title: 👶 INTUSSUSCEPTION: TX
content:
Telescoping of the bowel; sudden abdominal pain + 'Currant jelly' stool.

• Next Best Step: Air or Contrast Enema (both diagnostic and therapeutic).
• Note: If signs of peritonitis/perforation, go straight to surgery.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Ultrasound is the best initial diagnostic test (Target sign). The Enema is the next best step to treat it.

quiz.q: What is the non-surgical treatment of choice for intussusception?

title: 🩸 TTP (PENTAD)
content:
Deficiency of ADAMTS13.

FAT RN Mnemonic:
1. Fever.
2. Anemia (MAHA/Schistocytes).
3. Thrombocytopenia.
4. Renal failure.
5. Neurological symptoms.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is a Medical Emergency. Next best step: Plasmapheresis (Plasma exchange). Never wait for labs! Never give Platelets (it 'fuels the fire' of thrombosis).

quiz.q: What is the immediate treatment for suspected Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)?

title: 🗣️ THE GOSSIPING STAFF
content:
You overhear a nurse or doctor discussing a patient's private details in the elevator/cafeteria.

• Principle: Confidentiality.
• Next Best Step: Intervene immediately but politely. Remind them that the elevator is a public space and they are violating patient privacy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not wait to report them later. The priority is to Stop the breach now.

quiz.q: What should you do if you witness a colleague discussing a patient in a public area?
quiz.a: Immediately and politely intervene to stop the discussion.

title: 🦠 SEPSIS: THE 1-HOUR BUNDLE
content:
Critical management in the first hour.

1. Measure Lactate.
2. Obtain Blood Cultures (Before antibiotics).
3. Administer Broad-spectrum Antibiotics.
4. Rapid administration of 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the question asks for the 'Next Step' and both cultures and antibiotics are options: Cultures first, then immediately Antibiotics. Do NOT delay antibiotics if cultures are taking too long.

quiz.q: Should blood cultures be obtained before or after starting antibiotics in sepsis?
quiz.a: Before (but do not delay antibiotics significantly).

title: 💎 THE DIAMOND SUMMARY: 5 TOP TRAPS
content:
The most important 'Final' traps to remember:

1. Shoulder Dystocia: McRoberts maneuver + Suprapubic pressure. (No fundal pressure!).
2. DKA: Check Potassium before starting Insulin.
3. Stroke: CT Head (non-contrast) before Aspirin/tPA.
4. Cervical Spine: Clear it clinically (NEXUS/Canadian rules) before removing the collar.
5. Domestic Violence: Your role is to Assess Safety and provide resources; you cannot report it for a capable adult without their consent.

🌟 CONGRATULATIONS DOCTOR! 🌟
You have reached the final card (520). Your Imperial Vault is now complete and unique. You have all the tools to succeed. Go and make Canada proud!

quiz.q: Can you report domestic violence of a capable adult to the police without their consent in Canada?
quiz.a: No, you must respect their autonomy (unlike child or elder abuse in some provinces).
